database_info.title: Credit Hire Case Law Database
database_info.note: 379 cases processed. UKSC and Court of Appeal cases have high-quality AI summaries. County Court cases have partial summaries. Run build_case_database.py with your Claude API key to refresh or improve any entries. | 2026-04-17: Added Bourgrine v Natoora Ltd (Liverpool County Court, HHJ Wood KC, 23 Feb 2026) - appellate decision on burden of proof for need in credit hire, argued by Benjamin Williams KC. | 2026-04-19: Added Gray v Going Places Leisure Travel Ltd [2005] EWCA Civ 189 - scoped narrowly to the promptness of third-party costs applications at [11] and [15]. Shared by Mr Benjamin Williams KC for defending retrospective NPCO applications from Keoghs LLP. | 2026-04-19: Added Da Silva v Mihaylov (County Court, HHJ Carter, 2024) - fundamental dishonesty on concealed Brazilian bank account in impecuniosity disclosure. Sourced from Keoghs' case comment of 27 February 2024. Aliases: Mesquita, De Silva. | 2026-04-21: Added Hartshorn v NFU Mutual Insurance (CC Newcastle, HHJ Freeman on appeal) - taxi credit hire, Hussain SDP exception, household income in Hussain impecuniosity. Sourced from DWF case comment by Gavin Perry. | 2026-04-27: Added Powell v Moody (1966) - filtering on the offside, 80/20 against motorcyclist; significance flagged for claimant-side rebuttal.

cases.filename: Lloyd v RSA Insurance [2025] NIKB 43 (18 June 2025).pdf
cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland (King's Bench Division)
cases.defendant: RSA Insurance (as insurer of the Education Authority)
cases.outcome: Liability admitted. Claim for £43,122 in credit hire (149 days at £288/day) and £8,010 in storage (253 days) reduced to 7 days of hire (£2,016) and 17 days of storage (£510). Credit hire element reduced by over 95% on mitigation grounds: the vehicle had passed an MOT inspection while the hire was running, and the AMC had failed to act on the conditional findings of the engineer's report to establish whether economic repair was viable. Repair cost was awarded at the £6,720 the plaintiff actually paid for private second-hand repairs.
cases.summary: A parked Mercedes owned by the plaintiff Mr Lloyd was struck by an Education Authority vehicle insured by RSA. AH Assist (an accident management company) provided the plaintiff with a replacement vehicle and storage. The plaintiff claimed £12,100 pre-accident value, £43,122 for 149 days of credit hire at £288/day, and £8,010 for 253 days of storage. Liability was admitted; the issues were reasonableness, mitigation, the extent of damage (whether the vehicle was beyond economic repair) and whether continued hire and storage were reasonable in the circumstances. The plaintiff's motor assessor (Mr Carvill) initially treated repair costs as just above 80% of pre-accident value and treated the vehicle as beyond economic repair, but his figures included items 'subject to confirmation' that, when examined, brought the true cost to about £6,950 (around 57% of pre-accident value). The plaintiff had also presented the vehicle for MOT inspection on 2 January 2025 with a duct-taped bumper, and it passed. McLaughlin J held that an MOT certificate is compelling evidence of roadworthiness and that the plaintiff had failed to discharge the burden of proving otherwise. Although the defendant submitted that the only issue was whether the vehicle was unroadworthy when hire commenced, McLaughlin J rejected a purely binary assessment. He restated the overriding principle as restitutio in integrum, with the assessment made from the plaintiff's perspective by reference to the reasonableness of the steps taken (or not taken) in the aftermath of the accident. On the facts, prompt arrangement of a replacement vehicle pending the engineer's report was reasonable; continuing the hire after the MOT pass, and continuing it after Mr Carvill's conditional report without any follow-up to verify whether the potential repairs were in fact necessary, was not. Recovery was limited to 7 days of hire (£2,016) and 17 days of storage (£510); the balance was disallowed. Repair cost was awarded at the £6,720 the plaintiff in fact paid at a private garage using second-hand parts. The judgment also notes (obiter, by reference to McCauley v Brennan and Clarke v McCullough) that the result might have been different if impecuniosity had been properly pleaded and proven.
key_principles: The overriding principle in credit hire / credit repair mitigation cases is restitutio in integrum, assessed from the perspective of the plaintiff by reference to the reasonableness of the steps taken (or not taken) in the aftermath of the accident.
Whether the vehicle was roadworthy when hire commenced is not the only or decisive issue; a binary roadworthiness test is rejected.

A valid MOT certificate is compelling evidence of roadworthiness; conversely a failed MOT is prima facie evidence the vehicle is not roadworthy. Defendants should proactively obtain MOT records when defending hire claims.

Where an engineer's report contains conditional findings on which the economic viability of repair depends, the plaintiff and any AMC acting on their behalf have a duty to take timely steps to confirm whether the conditional items are in fact damaged. Failure to do so renders the continuation of credit hire / storage unreasonable for that period.
Credit hire claims are not to be assessed in the round; the court is willing to dissect the day-by-day reasonableness of hire and storage.

Higher credit rental / repair rates may still be reasonable on the facts of the case, particularly where the plaintiff can demonstrate impecuniosity or that it is otherwise unreasonable to expect them to fund the costs themselves or claim under their own policy.
legal_topics: AMC duty to follow up engineer's reports
cases.significance: Leading Northern Ireland High Court (KBD) decision on the mitigation analysis in credit hire / credit repair claims. McLaughlin J restates the proper framework as restitutio in integrum tested by reference to the reasonableness of the plaintiff's steps, expressly rejecting a binary roadworthiness test, and treats an MOT certificate as compelling evidence of roadworthiness. Not authority on the assessment of the basic hire rate itself — the case was not decided on a BHR analysis. For the right NI authority on BHR methodology see Burrows v Ross [2014] NIQB 99 and McAteer v Kirkpatrick [2011] NIQB 52.

cases.filename: Ali v HSF Logistics [2024] EWCA Civ 1479 Approved Judgment.pdf
cases.case_name: Ali v HSF Logistics EWCA Civ 1479 Approved Judgment
cases.summary: MAJID ALI brought proceedings against HSF LOGISTICS POLSKA SP. Z O.O in relation to a vehicle hire matter. atively standard form Particulars of Claim pleaded that the Claimant had suffered loss and damage, loss of use and inconvenience. It was alleged that the Claimant, having been deprived of his own vehicle, needed alternative transport for the period of hire claimed . The hire charges were claim The key issues for the court to determine included the proper legal principles to apply and whether the claimant was entitled to recover the claimed losses. held that the doctrine of ex turpi causa did not preclude recovery of any of the three heads of damage. That finding was not challenged on the first appeal to Martin Spencer J who described the Record. The lower court decision was upheld.
key_principles: The Court of Appeal's preferred approach to illegality in credit hire (missing MOT) is 'targeted illegality' based on causation — not a blanket ex turpi causa bar to the entire claim
The causation question: but for the accident, for how long would the claimant's vehicle have remained without an MOT? Hire charges are disallowed only for that period

A missing MOT is a summary offence carrying no penalty points and no disqualification — denying the entire credit hire claim for this minor breach would be disproportionate under Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42
The same targeted illegality principle applies to no-insurance and no-road-tax cases — the question is always causation, not a blanket bar (para 19)

At paragraph 19, Stuart-Smith LJ observed that driving without insurance is a more serious offence than driving without an MOT, and may attract a stronger illegality argument — but the causation-based approach still applies
Hewison v Meridian Shipping [2002] EWCA Civ 1821 was referenced as an example where illegality was sufficiently serious and causally connected to bar recovery
Patel v Mirza [2016] UKSC 42 provides the modern framework: the court must balance (a) the purpose of the prohibition, (b) other relevant public policies, and (c) proportionality
Loss of use is a genuine loss even where the vehicle lacked a valid MOT — the question is whether the claimant actually used the vehicle to meet a genuine need before the accident
The claimant should adduce evidence of intention to renew the MOT (or insurance) — booked appointments, short remaining period, history of renewal — to limit the period of disallowance

cases.case_name: Armstead v Royal & Sun Alliance Insurance Co Ltd
cases.defendant: Royal & Sun Alliance Insurance Company Ltd (RSA)
cases.outcome: Claimant's appeal allowed - the clause 16 loss-of-use sum was recoverable as damages from the second tortfeasor's insurer
cases.summary: Mrs Armstead was involved in two non-fault road traffic accidents in quick succession. After the first accident, while her own car was being repaired, she hired a Mini Cooper from Helphire Ltd (a credit hire organisation) on credit hire terms. The hire agreement included clause 16 which required the hirer to pay Helphire's daily hire rate (up to 30 days) for loss of use if the hire car was damaged while on hire. While driving the Helphire vehicle she was hit by a van in a second accident (the second tortfeasor being insured by RSA). The hire car was damaged and off the road for repairs. Mrs Armstead claimed against RSA (the second tortfeasor's insurer) for: (a) the cost of repairing the hire car, and (b) the clause 16 sum of 1,560 pounds representing Helphire's loss of use while the hire car was off the road. RSA accepted liability for the repair costs but disputed the clause 16 sum. The central issue was whether the clause 16 loss-of-use liability was recoverable from the second tortfeasor or was irrecoverable pure economic loss. The Supreme Court held unanimously that the clause 16 sum was recoverable. Mrs Armstead, as bailee in possession of the hire car, had a possessory right equivalent to ownership as against the wrongdoer (the second tortfeasor). The financial loss she suffered - her contractual liability to Helphire for loss of use - was consequential on damage to property she had a right to possess, not pure economic loss. The loss was not too remote: it was reasonably foreseeable that a driver of a hire car who was contractually liable to the hire company would incur such a liability if the car was damaged by a negligent third party. The clause 16 sum was recoverable provided it represented a genuine and reasonable pre-estimate of Helphire's actual loss of use. NOTE: This case does NOT concern the first tortfeasor's liability for ongoing hire charges, nor whether the first tortfeasor's liability is broken by the second accident. The first accident hire charges continue to accrue against the first tortfeasor until the claimant's own vehicle is repaired. The Armstead claim is solely against the SECOND tortfeasor's insurer for the CHO's loss of use of its own hire car while that car is being repaired after the second accident.
key_principles: A bailee in possession of a hire car can claim damages from a negligent third party (the second tortfeasor) for loss of or damage to the hire car, including the hire company's loss-of-use charges that the bailee is contractually liable to pay - this is NOT pure economic loss because the loss is consequential on damage to property the claimant had a right to possess (The Winkfield [1902] P 42 applied).

Where a credit hire agreement includes a loss-of-use clause (such as clause 16) requiring the hirer to pay the CHO's daily rate while the hire car is off the road for repairs, that contractual liability is recoverable as damages from the at-fault insurer of the party who damaged the hire car - provided the sum is a genuine and reasonable pre-estimate of the CHO's actual loss.

The remoteness test (The Wagon Mound) is satisfied: it is reasonably foreseeable that a driver of a hire car who is contractually liable to the hire company will incur a loss-of-use liability if the hire car is damaged by a negligent third party.

Where a claimant has two non-fault accidents and is on credit hire from the first, the claim against the second tortfeasor for the CHO's loss of use is a SEPARATE claim from the ongoing hire charges claimed against the first tortfeasor. The first tortfeasor remains liable for hire charges until the claimant's own vehicle is repaired. The second tortfeasor (or their insurer) is liable for the CHO's loss of use of its fleet vehicle while that vehicle is off the road after the second accident.

The CHO (as bailor/owner of the hire vehicle) has the underlying loss - its vehicle is off the road and unavailable for hire. The claimant (as bailee) is contractually liable to the CHO for that loss. The claimant can recover that contractual liability as damages from the second tortfeasor. The CHO and claimant cannot both recover for the same loss.
cases.significance: Landmark Supreme Court authority establishing that a credit hire claimant who suffers a second non-fault accident while driving a hire car can recover the CHO's loss-of-use charges (under the hire agreement) from the second tortfeasor's insurer. Confirms that such liability is consequential loss on damage to property in the claimant's possession, not pure economic loss. The first Supreme Court case directly addressing the recoverability of CHO loss-of-use provisions in a two-accident scenario.
key_paragraphs: Para 1: This claim for 1,560 pounds has reached the Supreme Court on a third appeal. Although the amount at stake is small, the issue raised is of general importance.
Para 7: It should be noted that the daily rental hire rate was what is termed the credit hire rate - higher than the spot rate - reflecting the CHO's business model of providing vehicles on credit.
Para 11: Remoteness and the concession made on behalf of Ms Armstead - the clause 16 sum must be a genuine and reasonable pre-estimate of the CHO's loss of use.
Para 19: A person owes a duty of care not to cause physical damage to another person's property and is liable for financial loss consequent on such damage. This is distinct from pure economic loss.

Para 21: To count as the claimant's property it is sufficient that the claimant has a right to possession. A bailee in possession can claim damages from a stranger whose negligence causes damage to the property (The Winkfield [1902] P 42).
Para 31: There is no reason in principle why recoverable loss should not include a contractual liability incurred by the claimant as a consequence of damage to property in her possession.

Para 46-47: The concession that the clause 16 sum must represent a genuine and reasonable pre-estimate of Helphire's actual losses was rightly made as a matter of law - this is an aspect of the normal rules on remoteness.
Para 53: In addition to remoteness, three other legal principles capable of limiting the recovery were raised but did not bar the claim.
Para 75: I agree that this appeal should be allowed.

cases.case_name: Mr Mazahar Hussain v EUI Ltd
cases.claimant: Mr Mazahar Hussain (self-employed private hire taxi driver)
cases.outcome: Credit hire claim of £33,140.52 (Toyota Auris on credit hire at £203.46/day for ~5 months following damage to a Toyota Avensis on 27 May 2021) substantially reduced. HHJ Malek held: (i) the Claimant satisfied the social-and-domestic-use exception in Hussain v EUI [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB), so was entitled to private spot rates for 5 weeks rather than nothing; (ii) the Claimant did NOT satisfy the 'greater loss' (compromised future trading) exception, having provided no financial information or evidence of profit and a 'Barkerend Taxis' letter the judge considered commercially implausible. Recovery limited to BHR for private use over 5 weeks plus £4,000 for personal injuries.
cases.outcome_party: mixed (claimant partially succeeded under the social/domestic-use exception)
cases.summary:
County Court (Bradford) decision by HHJ Malek (Circuit Judge), handed down 5 November 2024, intended by the judge as a 'further clarification' of Pepperall J's three exceptions in Humayum Hussain v EUI [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB). Important to note up front: (a) this is a county court decision, not binding authority — Pepperall J's judgment in the original Humayum Hussain remains the binding High Court framework; (b) the two cases share the Hussain surname and the EUI defendant, but the claimants are different and the cases address different questions. 

Facts: the Claimant was a self-employed private hire taxi driver whose Toyota Avensis was damaged on 27 May 2021. On 29 May 2021 he entered into a credit hire agreement with Bespoke Credit Hire for a Toyota Auris at £185.46/day plus £18.00/day CDW (£203.46/day total), running until 1 November 2021. Total credit hire charges of £33,140.52 were claimed. Liability and quantum on PI and vehicle damage were not at issue at trial; the live issues were credit hire and storage. 

Holdings: HHJ Malek confirmed that the burden of bringing himself within a Pepperall J exception lies on the claimant ([9]). The Claimant relied on the first ('compromised future trading') and second ('social and domestic use') exceptions. On the first exception the judge held that, to satisfy it, the claimant has to evidence: (i) the profitability of his trade pre-accident; and (ii) the likelihood that his business will be 'permanently impaired' — which involves considering whether the driver might be 'dropped' by his base, whether he could move to another base, and whether he could take advantage of an online platform such as Uber ([11]-[13]). The judge said the relevant balance depended on profitability, the size of the loss, and the likelihood of permanent impairment ([13]). On the facts the Claimant had provided 'absolutely no financial information or evidence of his profit (or hypothetical loss of profit)' ([16]) and the 'Barkerend Taxis' letter (signed by an unidentified 'A M Khan' and threatening disconnection in seven days) was considered commercially implausible ([17]). The first exception was not made out. The second (social and domestic use) exception WAS made out; recovery was therefore limited to private spot rates for 5 weeks.
key_principles: This is a county court decision and is not binding authority. The binding High Court framework is Pepperall J's three exceptions in Humayum Hussain v EUI [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB).

HHJ Malek's analysis of the 'greater loss' exception requires the claimant to evidence (i) pre-accident profitability and (ii) the likelihood of permanent impairment of the business, with consideration of whether the driver might be dropped by his base, whether he could move to another base, and whether online platforms (e.g. Uber) provide an alternative ([11]-[13]).

On HHJ Malek's analysis a bare assertion that the claimant has thought about mitigation does not suffice; the claimant 'should come to court fully prepared to evidence' profitability and likelihood of permanent impairment ([15]).

Burden of proof: once the defendant has established that the vehicle was a profit-earning chattel and that the cost of hire significantly exceeded the avoided loss of profit, the burden of bringing the case within a Hussain exception lies on the claimant ([9]).
The social-and-domestic-use exception was applied: the Claimant recovered private spot rates rather than nothing, his personal need for the vehicle not being seriously contested.

Important divergences from Pepperall J's High Court framework noted by commentators (Hogan; Azeem Ali): the High Court's word was 'compromised' future trading, not 'permanently impaired'; HHJ Malek's 'exceptionally' / 'permanent impairment' formulation is on its face a higher threshold than Pepperall J's text supports and is a matter properly for higher court guidance.
legal_topics: Hussain v EUI exceptions — county court interpretation
cases.significance: Non-binding county court decision (HHJ Malek, Bradford) frequently deployed by defendant insurers as if it were a 'landmark' clarification of Pepperall J's three exceptions in the High Court decision in Hussain v EUI [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB). Practitioner commentary (Andrew Hogan; Azeem Ali) is critical: the case sets a higher evidential bar than Pepperall J's binding High Court framework actually requires, particularly by recasting 'compromised future trading' as 'permanent impairment' and by demanding a level of financial sophistication ('audited accounts unicorn problem' per Hogan) inconsistent with the way most self-employed taxi drivers actually keep their records. Note that this case is sometimes referred to as 'Hussain 2' alongside the High Court 'Hussain 1' (Humayum Hussain) — the two cases share a surname and defendant only, and are doctrinally distinct.

cases.source_notes: Entry rewritten 2026-05-30 from three sources: (i) the actual judgment as handed down at Bradford County Court on 5 November 2024 (HHJ Malek; CPR PD39A para 6.1 release); (ii) Andrew Hogan of Counsel's commentary 'In the back of my cab' (Credit Hire Barrister blog, 2 June 2025), which is critical of the financial-sophistication standard the judgment imposes ('audited accounts unicorn problem') and concerned about the dual-use exception being read down; (iii) Mohammed Azeem Ali of Counsel's commentary 'The Implications of Mr Mazahar Hussain' (The Barrister Group, 11 July 2025), which emphasises that the decision is not binding, distinguishes 'exceptions' from a test of 'exceptionality', and contrasts the High Court's 'compromised' wording with HHJ Malek's 'permanently impaired'. Steve Evans (the user) confirms the case is real and that HHJ Malek is a Circuit Judge sitting at Bradford. The previous case database entry was an AI placeholder dated [2024] with no real summary; this entry replaces it with a properly-sourced summary. The case ID has been renamed from 'hussain-v-eui' to 'mazahar-hussain-v-eui-2024-ewcc-16' to distinguish it from the Humayum Hussain v EUI High Court entry.

cases.case_name: Kindertons Ltd v Murtagh and Esure Services Ltd
cases.outcome: Appeal dismissed — non-party costs order against Kindertons upheld
cases.summary:
A landmark High Court decision on non-party costs orders (NPCOs) against credit hire companies, described by Turner J as another chapter in the 'continuing war of forensic attrition between motor insurers and credit hire companies'. The accident involved minor rear-end damage to Mr Ibrahim's Audi A5. Within two days Kindertons contacted Mr Ibrahim and persuaded him not to drive his car — even though an assessor later confirmed it had suffered no damage rendering it unroadworthy. A Kindertons agent actively advised Mr Ibrahim to ignore any call from Esure (the at-fault driver's insurer) and recruited him into a credit hire agreement for a Jaguar XF at £345.08 per day, later replaced with a Mercedes Benz C250. Repairs would have taken 4-5 days; the total hire was 33 days. The underlying claim failed when Mr and Mrs Ibrahim were found to have been fundamentally dishonest in relation to the personal injury element.

The Recorder made a non-party costs order against Kindertons. Kindertons appealed on six grounds, arguing it was not a 'real party', that the causation test was not met, and that it was unjust to make the order when it was as much a victim of the claimants' dishonesty as the insurer. Turner J dismissed all six grounds. He found that Kindertons was plainly a 'real party' — it had proactively recruited the claim, instructed the claimant to ignore the insurer, and stood to profit substantially from the litigation. The credit hire element directly increased the costs of the litigation. Kindertons voluntarily assumed the risk that claimants might prove dishonest. Combined legal costs in the case reached nearly £100,000, dwarfing the £2,543 repair claim. The case is a stark illustration of the commercial and legal risks facing credit hire companies who adopt aggressive recruitment practices.
key_principles: A credit hire company which proactively recruits a claimant and instructs them to ignore the insurer's offer may be a 'real party' for NPCO purposes
The fact that the underlying claimants are found to be dishonest does not prevent a NPCO against a credit hire company — it voluntarily assumes that risk
The credit hire element of a claim can increase the costs of litigation such that the causation test for a NPCO is met
CPR PD 44 para 12.2 expressly warns credit hire companies of the risk of NPCO applications
Appellate courts will not lightly interfere with a first-instance judge's discretion to make a NPCO
cases.significance: A major High Court authority on non-party costs orders against credit hire companies; directly relevant to any case where an insurer seeks to make a credit hire company liable for costs following an unsuccessful claim.

cases.case_name: Pereira and Others v Chambers and Others; Direct Accident Management Ltd (Respondent)
cases.outcome: Non-party costs orders against DAML granted — DAML found to be a 'real party' and just to make the order
cases.summary:
A significant County Court decision in which HHJ Saunders made non-party costs orders against Direct Accident Management Limited (DAML), a credit hire company operating as part of the Anexo Group Plc (which also owns Bond Turner, the solicitors who acted for the claimants in each case). The judge dealt with four consolidated applications arising from failed or part-failed credit hire claims. Two claims ended by acceptance of a Part 36 offer for substantially less than claimed; one was struck out for non-payment of a court fee; one was dismissed after trial on liability.

HHJ Saunders scrutinised DAML's business model in detail. Under the standard DAML hire agreements, claimants were effectively bound to instruct Bond Turner as their solicitors, DAML required their co-operation in the litigation, and claimants faced significant financial consequences (the full hire charges becoming immediately due) if they failed to co-operate. Although DAML maintained it played no direct role in the conduct of proceedings, the judge found that the terms of the agreement gave DAML a sufficient controlling commercial interest in the litigation — including attending trial — to satisfy the 'real party' test. On the causation and justice elements, the judge found that DAML knowingly operated a model of charging substantially above basic hire rates and recovering those charges through litigation at the claimants' expense (or risk), and that it was just to make them bear the costs risk accordingly.
key_principles: A credit hire company that contractually requires claimants to instruct a connected firm of solicitors and to co-operate in litigation may be a 'real party' for NPCO purposes
The Anexo Group business model (DAML providing hire, Bond Turner acting as solicitors, both under common ownership) was subject to close scrutiny
The 'justice' element of the NPCO test is satisfied where a credit hire company knowingly charges above basic rates and requires claimants to pursue litigation to recover those charges
CPR 44.16(3) and PD 12 expressly contemplate costs orders against credit hire companies in appropriate circumstances
A credit hire company that attends trial and has a financial stake in the outcome may cross the line from commercial interest to 'real party'
cases.significance: Important 2024 County Court decision on NPCOs against DAML (Anexo Group); relevant to all cases involving DAML, McAMS, Bond Turner or similar business models where the hire company and solicitors are under common ownership.

judges: His Honour Judge Graham Wood KC
cases.outcome: Claimant's appeal allowed — a bailor/owner can claim credit hire even where a third party is the exclusive user of the vehicle; remitted for rehearing on quantum
cases.summary:
A 2024 County Court appeal decision on the novel and important question of whether a vehicle owner who never personally used the vehicle can claim credit hire charges when it is damaged through another's negligence, where the exclusive user of the vehicle was a third party. Mr Smallworth owned a Honda NSC 110 electric motorcycle (an electric scooter) acquired on hire purchase for his friend Mr Tudorie (a Deliveroo driver who lacked sufficient credit rating to buy it himself). Mr Smallworth himself never rode the vehicle and commuted by public transport. BT's driver reversed into the parked motorcycle, causing a total loss. Mr Tudorie hired replacement motorcycles on credit hire for 42 days (total: £12,359.52), but the hire agreement was in Mr Smallworth's name as owner.

The Deputy District Judge dismissed the credit hire claim, finding that Mr Smallworth had no 'need' for a replacement vehicle as he never used it himself. HHJ Graham Wood KC allowed the appeal. He held that the judge had applied the wrong principle: a bailor (the owner) has standing to bring a claim for loss of use of a chattel, including credit hire charges, even where the bailee (the actual user) is a third party. The fact that the vehicle is used exclusively by a third party does not extinguish the owner's right to recover the cost of providing a replacement. The claim was remitted for rehearing to address quantum issues including impecuniosity, hire period, and basic hire rate — matters which could not be resolved on appeal without hearing evidence.
key_principles: A bailor/owner of a vehicle damaged by a tortfeasor can claim credit hire charges even where the vehicle was used exclusively by a third party bailee
The 'need' test in credit hire cases must be assessed in the context of the purpose for which the owner made the vehicle available, not restricted to the owner's own personal use
Pleadings in owner/third party user cases should clearly identify the actual user and the basis on which the claim is advanced
Where a judge dismisses a claim on an incorrect legal principle, the appeal court will remit rather than substitute its own findings on factual matters such as impecuniosity and hire rate
If the owner cannot establish impecuniosity, the claim may still succeed but limited to the basic hire rate
cases.significance: Important 2024 authority establishing that a vehicle owner can claim credit hire for a third party's use of the vehicle — relevant to all cases where the owner and user are different people (e.g. family vehicles, vehicles provided to employees or friends).

cases.claimant: Mr Oscar Jose De Sousa Fernandes
cases.outcome: Claimant's appeal allowed — strike out was disproportionate; Part 7 claim reinstated
cases.summary:
A County Court appeal decision of some significance for road traffic practitioners. Following a road traffic accident on 20 October 2020, Mr Fernandes' claim was notified to the defendant. Unusually, it was the defendant (Mr Khan) who submitted a Claim Notification Form through the RTA portal, and two days later the claimant admitted liability through the portal. Several months later, having reconsidered his position, Mr Fernandes issued his own Part 7 claim challenging liability. The defendant raised the previous admission as a bar to the proceedings and the District Judge struck out the Part 7 claim, finding there was a binding settlement and/or that the claim constituted a Henderson v Henderson abuse of process.

HHJ Saunders allowed the appeal on the ground that the District Judge had applied the wrong test — the admission in the portal did not constitute a binding settlement and the Part 7 claim was not an abuse of process. Even if it had been an abuse, the judge held that strike out was a disproportionate remedy in the circumstances given the significance of the claim and the fact that an interim payment had already been made. The case has significance for those advising claimants who have made portal admissions and subsequently wish to challenge liability.
key_principles: An admission made through the RTA portal does not necessarily constitute a binding settlement preventing a fresh Part 7 claim
The Henderson v Henderson abuse of process doctrine requires careful analysis before strike out will be appropriate
Strike out is a disproportionate remedy unless the abuse of process is clear and there is no other adequate remedy
Courts will consider the overriding objective and the significance of the claim before striking out
cases.significance: Important County Court authority on the binding effect of portal admissions and the use of strike out as a remedy in credit hire and personal injury claims.

cases.filename: Holt v Allianz Insurance Plc [2023] EWHC 790 (KB).pdf
cases.claimant: Holt (the insured of Allianz on a third party application by Auxillis)
cases.defendant: Allianz Insurance Plc (respondent / appellant in the High Court)
cases.outcome: On the narrow technical point, Auxillis' appeal was allowed: the proper applicant for pre-action disclosure was the insured (Holt) rather than the insurer, a point capable of being cured by bringing future applications in the joint name of insurer and insured. On the wider substantive question of whether pre-action disclosure of impecuniosity documents is appropriate, the court found emphatically against the CHO position and upheld HHJ Harrison's earlier decision.
cases.summary: Appeal from HHJ Harrison's order that a credit-hire claimant disclose basic documents going to impecuniosity under the Practice Direction on Pre-Action Conduct. Auxillis, the credit-hire organisation, refused to address impecuniosity pre-action on the basis that it was a matter for litigation. Baker J upheld Auxillis' appeal on the narrow procedural ground that any such application should be brought in the name of the insured (Holt) and not the insurer (Allianz), but went on — permission having been given on the wider importance of the case — to give detailed substantive guidance. He held that the CPR's provisions on pleading impecuniosity and on in-litigation disclosure do not displace the pre-action regime ([33]); that basic documentary evidence of impecuniosity is part of the 'key documents' expected to be exchanged under PD-PAC ([37]); that a CHO cannot, on the basis that litigation has not begun, refuse to state a position on whether the claim it is asserting includes a case of impecuniosity ([38]); and that impecuniosity should generally be dealt with before proceedings, particularly where the claim is met with a serious response that the BHR is very much lower ([39]). Auxillis' pre-litigation correspondence was found to be 'unhelpful and the opposite of engaging in constructive dialogue' ([48]); the suggestion that Auxillis might not contend that the BHR was unavailable to the claimant was 'contrived and invalid' ([81]); the privacy and confidentiality objection was 'misplaced' and 'somewhat bizarre' ([93]-[94]); and Auxillis had 'adopted, applied, and chosen to stand their ground on, a wrongheaded policy' ([111]). HHJ Harrison's costs disposal — the CHO to pay Allianz's costs of the application and to be limited to its own copying costs — was upheld as well within the trial judge's discretion ([112]).
key_principles: Impecuniosity should normally be addressed pre-action, not deferred until after proceedings have issued ([33], [39]).
Basic documentary evidence supporting a claim of impecuniosity falls within the 'key documents' a party is expected to disclose under PD-PAC ([37]).
A credit hire organisation cannot, by reason that litigation has not yet started, refuse to state whether its claim involves a case of impecuniosity ([38]).
Where a credit hire claim is met with a serious response asserting that the BHR is materially lower, impecuniosity should generally be dealt with before proceedings ([39]).

Pre-action correspondence that refuses to engage with reasonable requests for impecuniosity information is 'unhelpful and the opposite of engaging in constructive dialogue' ([48]); a stance that the CHO might not contend the BHR is unavailable is 'contrived and invalid' if relied upon to avoid the issue ([81]).

The fact that pre-action disclosure would reveal personal financial information is no good objection where the disclosure would help resolve the dispute or save litigation costs; privacy / confidentiality objections are 'misplaced' ([86], [93]-[94]).

Where a CHO has refused even to consider impecuniosity pre-action, an order for pre-action disclosure together with an adverse costs order against the CHO is within the trial judge's discretion ([111]-[112]).
legal_topics: credit hire — claim handling at correspondence stage
cases.significance: Important High Court (KBD) authority on the obligations of credit hire organisations to engage with impecuniosity at the pre-litigation stage and to disclose basic supporting financial documents on reasonable request. Not authority on the assessment of the BHR itself, on the test for 'need', or on the equivalence of vehicles or rates under Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management — those questions remain separate.

cases.significance: Case on mitigation and related credit hire matters.

cases.outcome: Appeal allowed — non-party costs order against On-Hire overturned; District Judge had conflated the 'real party' and 'causation' tests
cases.summary:
An important County Court appeal decision on the correct legal framework for non-party costs orders against credit hire companies. The underlying claim arose from a road traffic accident on 4 February 2019. The claimant entered a credit hire agreement with On-Hire Limited for 31 days (total cost £12,864) plus repair, recovery and storage claims. The total claim exceeded £28,500. Following allegations of fundamental dishonesty by the defendant's solicitors — including that the claimant had concealed a known witness and failed to disclose prior accident history — the claim was discontinued and judgment entered for the defendant. The defendant applied for a non-party costs order against On-Hire, which the District Judge granted for 50% of costs (£7,500 plus hearing costs of £5,100).

HHJ Freedman allowed the appeal. He held that the District Judge had erred in law by conflating two legally distinct requirements for an NPCO: (1) whether the non-party is a 'real party' (i.e. has a controlling interest in the litigation) and (2) whether the non-party caused the costs for which it is sought to be made liable. The DJ had reasoned that finding the 'real party' test satisfied automatically meant the causation test was also met — this was wrong in law. Applying each test separately, HHJ Freedman found that On-Hire was not a 'real party' as it had no actual control of the litigation: the claimant and his solicitors (Winns) remained in control. Even if the 'real party' test had been met, causation was not established on the evidence — there was no demonstration that the credit hire element had materially increased the costs of the litigation beyond what would have been incurred in any event given the contested liability and personal injury elements.
key_principles: A non-party costs order against a credit hire company requires two distinct findings: (1) that the non-party is a 'real party' and (2) that it caused the costs
These two tests are separate and distinct — satisfying the 'real party' test does not automatically satisfy the causation test
A credit hire company that provides a vehicle and defers payment, but hands over control of the litigation to the claimant's solicitors, may not be a 'real party'
The causation test requires cogent evidence that the non-party's involvement caused additional costs that would not otherwise have been incurred
Criticisms of failure to identify a claimant's dishonesty should be directed at the claimant's solicitors, not the credit hire company
cases.significance: Important authority clarifying the two-stage legal test for non-party costs orders against credit hire companies, and particularly the need to establish causation separately from the 'real party' test.

cases.case_name: Smyth v Markerstudy Insurance Company Ltd
cases.outcome: Claimant partially succeeded — awarded 80% of claim (£947.84); mitigation deduction for hire company's unreasonable advice to ignore the insurer's intervention offer
cases.summary:
A Northern Ireland High Court appeal in a credit hire case raising the important issue of whether a claimant who rejects an insurer's intervention offer (offering a free replacement vehicle) and instead retains a more expensive credit hire vehicle has failed to mitigate his loss. Mr Smyth was involved in a minor damage-only RTA on 10 January 2019. On 21 January 2019 — the same day he signed a credit hire agreement with CRASH Services for a BMW 4 Series (£205.58/day) — the defendant insurer sent an intervention letter offering to provide him with a vehicle at no cost. Mr Smyth did not accept the offer and continued with the credit hire, claiming £1,184.78 for four days' hire.

The County Court found the plaintiff had failed to mitigate. On appeal McAlinden J held that the question of mitigation depends on the particular facts of each case. Three factors were relevant: (1) the 'basket of services' provided by CRASH (covering repairs, legal fees and other expenses) which the plaintiff would have forfeited by accepting the insurer's offer; (2) the fact that CRASH had advised the plaintiff to ignore the intervention letter (advice McAlinden J found was clearly wrong); and (3) whether the insurer's offer was too good to be true, requiring inquiry by the plaintiff. On balance, the plaintiff acted reasonably in retaining the CRASH hire to preserve his wider contractual protections. However, the wrong advice given by CRASH to disregard the letter justified a 20% reduction in the award to reflect the injustice to the defendant of ignoring that specific factor.
key_principles: Whether a claimant has failed to mitigate by rejecting an insurer's intervention offer is a question of fact in each case

A claimant may reasonably reject an insurer's offer of a free vehicle if accepting it would forfeit a wider 'basket of services' (repairs, legal costs, ATE cover etc.) provided by the credit hire company
A credit hire company that advises a claimant to ignore an intervention letter is giving wrong and unreasonable advice
Where a credit hire company gives unreasonable advice, this may justify a reduction in the claimant's damages even if the claimant's overall decision was reasonable
When intervention letters are received, claimants (and their advisers) have a responsibility to consider them carefully and may need to make enquiries of the insurer
cases.significance: Northern Ireland High Court authority on the 'basket of services' argument and the correct approach to intervention offers; relevant to any case where a claimant has rejected an insurer's offer of a cheaper or free replacement vehicle.

cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Plaintiff's hire period reduced by 2 days at the start (Crash's 2-day delay in instructing motor assessor was unreasonable); total award £2,320.92
cases.summary:
Companion judgment to McKibbin v UK Insurance [2021] NIQB 27, applying the same principles to a slightly different fact pattern. The plaintiff hired from Crash Services following a road traffic accident. The defendant argued the hire period was excessive due to delays in instructing the motor assessor, receiving the report, and forwarding it to the defendant's insurers (AXA).

Applying the McKibbin framework, Scoffield J found that most of the challenged delays were reasonable in the ordinary way of business. However, Crash delayed for two days at the start of the hire in instructing the motor assessor — there was no reason why the assessor could not have been instructed on the same day the hire car was delivered. This two-day delay was attributable to Crash as the plaintiff's agent and reduced the recoverable hire period by two days.

All other delays (including the inspection period, time to produce the report, and forwarding to AXA) were either reasonable or not attributable to the plaintiff/Crash. The defendant's insurer's own delay was noted but was not the plaintiff's responsibility.

Final award: £2,320.92 (36 days at agreed rate of £41.27, plus storage £636, temporary insurance £97.20, recovery/delivery £102).
key_principles: A 2-day delay by Crash in instructing the motor assessor at the start of hire (with no justification for the delay) is an unreasonable act attributable to the plaintiff as Crash's principal — 2 days' hire is deducted
The motor assessor should be instructed on the same day (or the next working day) the hire vehicle is delivered to the plaintiff — delay beyond this is prima facie unreasonable
Most ordinary administrative delays in the repair authorisation chain (inspection, report preparation, forwarding to insurer) are not unreasonable and will not reduce the hire period
The defendant insurer's delay in responding to the assessor's report is relevant context but does not make an otherwise unreasonable delay by the plaintiff/Crash reasonable
Specific, identifiable acts of unreasonable delay by the plaintiff's agent result in a precise, targeted reduction to the hire period — not a global reduction
Total award of £2,320.92 for 36 days hire (at agreed rate £41.27) is a real-world NI benchmark for short hire claims; storage at modest daily rates is recoverable where reasonable
cases.significance: Applies the McKibbin framework to produce a precise, targeted 2-day reduction for an identifiable Crash delay; establishes the day-one motor assessor instruction as the benchmark standard in NI.

cases.case_name: McKibbin v UK Insurance Ltd (heard with Clarke v McEvoy)
cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland (Queen's Bench Division)
cases.claimant: Karen McKibbin (primary school teacher); Mrs Rhonda Clarke (in conjoined Clarke v McEvoy [2021] NIQB 28)
cases.outcome: Two NI High Court appeals on credit hire duration handed down together on 8 March 2021. McKibbin: 37-day hire with £6,909.34 in charges; the County Court had deducted 9 days, Scoffield J on appeal restored 8 of those days and reduced the hire by only 1 day. Diminution in value reassessed at 7.5% of pre-accident value (between the parties' 5% and 12% positions). Clarke v McEvoy: 38-day hire; County Court allowed 29 days; on appeal Scoffield J disallowed 2 days at the front end for unreasonable delay between engaging the AMC and instructing the engineer (rest of the engineer's six-day inspection delay was reasonable; the 17-day delay between report and the insurer's cheque could not be laid at the plaintiff's door).
cases.summary: Two conjoined NI appeals on credit hire duration, agency, and mitigation. The conceptual centre of gravity is the agency question: where a plaintiff delegates full control of their accident claim to an accident management company, that company — together with the engineers and solicitors it instructs — is treated as the plaintiff's agent, and delays it causes can be imputed to the plaintiff. Importantly, the repair garage is not an agent: delays during the actual repair phase remain outside the plaintiff's control (the protection in Mattocks v Mann remains intact). Scoffield J adopted a stage-by-stage analysis of the hire timeline (accident to engineer instruction; engineer instruction to inspection; inspection to report; report to settlement), with the burden on the defendant to establish that any given delay was unreasonable and material. The court also held that minor or practical delays — for example a two-day delay in collecting the repaired car due to a primary school teacher's teaching commitments — will not be deducted; the court is not to apply a 'counsel of perfection'.
key_principles: Where a plaintiff delegates full control of their credit hire claim to an accident management company, the AMC and the experts / solicitors it instructs are the plaintiff's agents, and unreasonable delays they cause can be imputed to the plaintiff.
Repair garages are NOT agents of the plaintiff for this purpose; delays in the actual repair phase are outside the plaintiff's control (Mattocks v Mann protection preserved).

The court will conduct a stage-by-stage analysis of the hire timeline — accident to engineer instruction; instruction to inspection; inspection to report; report to settlement — and may deduct days at the earlier stages where unreasonable delay is established.
Delay caused by the defendant insurer's own processing time between report and cheque cannot be attributed to the plaintiff.

The court will not impose a 'counsel of perfection': minor, practical delays attributable to the plaintiff's working life will not be deducted. Burden is on the defendant to establish that any deduction is appropriate.
legal_topics: Mattocks v Mann — repair garages not agents
cases.significance: Leading NI High Court (QBD) authority on the duration of credit hire, the agency status of accident management companies, and the proper approach to delay analysis. Defendant-side commentary treats it as a framework for probing AMC-induced delay; the case equally affirms the Mattocks protection for repair-garage delays and the no-counsel-of-perfection limit on deductions. Not authority on BHR methodology.

cases.outcome: Appeal dismissed (the judges below were wrong about the status of 'Signed For 1st Class' under CPR 6.26, but relief from sanctions was rightly refused because the Reply did not properly plead impecuniosity and the application was late).
cases.summary: Procedural appeal in a credit-hire case. The Particulars of Claim asserted impecuniosity in a single line. An unless order required the claimant to file and serve a Reply setting out 'all facts in support of any assertion that the claimant was impecunious'. The Reply was posted by 'Signed For 1st Class' on the deadline, did not contain the required factual detail (only that the claimant 'earned cash as a minicab driver, he expended the same on bills and daily living allowances for his family'), and the application for relief from sanctions was made about eight weeks later. The Court of Appeal held: (i) the lower courts were wrong to treat 'Signed For 1st Class' as outside CPR 6.26 — it is either first-class post or 'another service which provides for delivery on the next business day' ([34]-[41]); (ii) the Reply nevertheless did not comply with the substance of the Unless Order — it failed to set out the income, expenditure and inability-to-afford case that the Order required ([48]); (iii) on the Denton three-stage test, the breach was serious and significant, there was no good reason, and the delay in applying for relief militated strongly against grant ([54], [65], [67]); (iv) the 'opportunism' warning in Denton was 'considerably over-stated' by the appellant — the bar for treating an innocent party's reliance on a breach as opportunistic is relatively high ([69]).
key_principles: A claimant must properly plead impecuniosity from the outset — a bare assertion is inadequate. The Reply or pleading must set out 'all facts' (income, expenditure, inability to afford the BHR) on which impecuniosity is said to rest (Coulson LJ at [7], [48], [54]).
Royal Mail 'Signed For 1st Class' falls within CPR 6.26's deemed-service regime as either first-class post or 'another service which provides for delivery on the next business day' ([34]-[41]).
On Denton stage 1, the substantive failure of an Unless-Order Reply to plead the required facts is itself a serious breach, not just the delay in service ([50], [54]).
The 'opportunism' caveat in Denton operates only at a relatively high bar — it does not let a defaulting party blame the other side for delays caused by its own breach ([69]).
cases.significance: Important Court of Appeal authority on (a) the standard of pleading and proof of impecuniosity in credit-hire claims and (b) procedural CPR points on deemed service and Denton relief from sanctions. Not authority on the test for 'need' of a replacement vehicle nor on the BHR comparison itself.

cases.summary:
MEF v St George’s Healthcare NHS Trust [2020] EWHC 1300 (QB)
The High Court has held that a Calderbank offer relating to costs could be accepted by the
claimant part way through the detailed assessment hearing. The decision starkly illustrates
the difference between a Calderbank offer and an offer pursuant to Part 36 of the Civil
Procedure Rules. It was held at first instance, that unlike a Part 36 offer, the Defendant’s Calderbank offer was
capable of acceptance without the permission of the Court, despite the hearing having
started.

cases.outcome: Appeal dismissed. The High Court upheld HHJ Wall's first-instance decision to cap recovery at the avoided loss of profit (~£423) rather than the full credit hire charges of £6,596.50 for the 18-day hire of a plated taxi. The second ground of appeal — challenging the trial judge's acceptance of the defendant's BHR evidence — was not reached because the first ground (loss of profit cap) was rejected.
cases.summary: A self-employed taxi driver whose vehicle was damaged in an admitted-liability RTA hired a replacement plated taxi on credit hire for 18 days. The credit hire charges were £6,596.50; the total avoided loss of profit during the hire period was about £423. At first instance HHJ Wall held that, where the loss is of a 'profit-earning chattel', the measure of damages is kept at the loss of profits and it was unreasonable mitigation to spend more in trying to earn the profit than the profit itself; damages were therefore capped at the lost profit. The claimant was separately debarred from relying on impecuniosity for non-compliance with a financial disclosure order; HHJ Wall also indicated that, had the hire charges been recoverable, recovery would have been limited to the BHR in the defendant's rates evidence. On appeal, Pepperall J set out the principles for taxi / profit-earning-chattel cases. The general rule at [16.5] is that where the cost of hire significantly exceeds the avoided loss of profit, damages are ordinarily capped at the lost profit. At [16.6] he identified three circumstances in which hire may nonetheless be reasonable even where the hire charges exceed the lost profit: (a) where future trading would be compromised — for example a chauffeur unwilling to let down a regular client, or a self-employed taxi driver risking being dropped by the taxi company that provides most of his work; (b) where the need for the replacement vehicle is also for social and domestic use, in which case the claimant is entitled to recover the cost of hire as any private motorist would, even if it exceeds the lost profit; (c) where the claimant is impecunious — it may be reasonable to hire where the driver simply cannot afford to be out of work, an impecunious self-employed claimant being not to be expected to be left without any income. On the facts of Hussain none of those exceptions was established and the loss-of-profit cap applied. Because that disposed of the appeal, Pepperall J did not need to address the BHR ground.
key_principles: Where the credit hire charges significantly exceed the avoided loss of profit that the hire enabled the claimant to earn, recovery is ordinarily capped at the lost profit (Pepperall J at [16.5]).

That cap does not apply where future trading would be compromised — e.g. a chauffeur not wanting to let down a regular client, or a self-employed taxi driver who risks being dropped by the taxi company that provides most of his work ([16.6(a)]).

The cap does not apply where the claimant proves that the replacement vehicle was also needed for social and domestic use; in that case the claimant recovers the cost of hire as any private motorist would, even if it exceeds the lost profit ([16.6(b)]).
The cap does not apply where the claimant is impecunious — an impecunious self-employed claimant cannot be expected to be left without any income ([16.6(c)]).
The burden of establishing one of the three exceptions sits on the claimant; a bare assertion that the claimant 'could not afford not to work' is not enough — evidence is required.
cases.significance: Leading High Court authority on credit hire recovery for self-employed claimants whose vehicle is a profit-earning chattel (notably taxi drivers and chauffeurs). Sets the general loss-of-profit cap and identifies the three exceptions which a claimant must affirmatively establish on the evidence. Not authority on BHR methodology; the appeal did not reach the BHR ground.

cases.filename: Morgan v Bryson Recycling Limited [2018] NIQB 12.pdf
cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Defendant's appeal allowed in full; credit hire and storage claims dismissed — vehicle had no valid insurance at time of accident; plaintiff could not lawfully use it regardless
cases.summary:
A causation case with an unusual fact pattern: the plaintiff's vehicle was uninsured at the time of the accident (insurance had lapsed). Because the vehicle was uninsured, the plaintiff could not lawfully drive it in any event, regardless of the accident damage.

Burgess J applied the fundamental causation principle: the court must identify whether the defendant's negligence caused the plaintiff's loss. Here, because the vehicle had no valid insurance, the plaintiff would not have been lawfully driving it at all after the insurance lapsed in any event. The plaintiff himself confirmed that if the accident had not occurred, he would have been driving the car until his insurance renewed in August 2016 — but since the insurance had already lapsed, he could not do so. There was therefore no causal nexus between the defendant's negligence and the need for a replacement vehicle.

Storage charges were also refused: no evidence of a reasonable daily storage rate was adduced. The burden of proving quantum rests on the plaintiff and unsupported assertions of daily rate are insufficient.
key_principles: Where a plaintiff's vehicle was uninsured at the time of the accident, and the plaintiff could not lawfully drive it regardless of accident damage, there is no causal nexus between the defendant's negligence and the need for a replacement vehicle
It is driving without insurance (not merely having an unroadworthy vehicle) that defeats the credit hire claim on causation — the vehicle could not lawfully be used in any event
A plaintiff who would have continued driving their uninsured car until insurance renewal cannot use that as a counterfactual — the relevant question is what they were lawfully entitled to do
Storage charges require positive evidence of a reasonable daily rate — the burden of proof on quantum rests on the plaintiff; bare assertions without evidential foundation are rejected
Morgan is the NI equivalent of the English causation principle applied in cases where the plaintiff's own prior conduct (allowing insurance to lapse) breaks the chain of causation
cases.significance: NI causation authority: an uninsured plaintiff who could not lawfully drive their vehicle cannot recover credit hire — insurance lapse breaks the causal link between the tort and the claimed loss.

cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Defendant's appeal allowed in part; paint labour charge reduced from £40/hour to £30/hour; total award reduced by £275 plus VAT
cases.summary:
A vehicle repair rate appeal heard by Morgan LCJ (Lord Chief Justice of Northern Ireland). The dispute concerned a single line in the repair invoice: the hourly rate for paint/bodywork labour. The garage charged £40/hour; the defendant's evidence suggested the reasonable rate was £30/hour.

Morgan LCJ applied the Coles case approach: the question is not whether the overall bill is reasonable item by item, but whether the overall outcome is reasonable. However, where only one item is in dispute and that item is shown to be excessive, and there is no other compensating adjustment in the rest of the bill, the excess must be corrected.

The court found £40/hour was unreasonable for paint work; £30 was the appropriate rate. With 27½ hours of paint work charged, the excess was £10/hour × 27.5 = £275, reduced from the award. Total award: £2,316.81. The judgment also notes that evidence of a compensating factor (discounted parts, unclaimed hours etc.) could potentially displace an item-by-item finding of excessiveness.
key_principles: The correct approach to vehicle repair cost disputes is holistic — the question is whether the overall outcome is reasonable — but where a single item is clearly excessive with no compensating adjustment, the excess must be corrected
Paint/bodywork labour rates are subject to the same reasonableness assessment as mechanical labour rates — Matchett/Bates principles apply to all components of a repair invoice
Where one repair invoice component is shown to be excessive and the overall bill is therefore more than the reasonable repair cost, the award is reduced to the reasonable repair cost
A party could displace an item-by-item finding of excess by showing that other components of the bill contained discounts or that the overall figure remained reasonable despite one higher item
Morgan LCJ applied the Coles case approach in NI: the focus is on whether the total repair cost exceeds what the claimant could reasonably have obtained on the open market
cases.significance: Confirms the holistic/Coles approach to repair cost disputes in NI applies at the LCJ level; a clearly excessive single item with no offsetting reduction will be corrected.

cases.summary: Clayton v EUI Ltd (Admiral Insurance) [2017] EWCA Civ. Court of Appeal judgment addressing both zero excess and ATE insurance arguments in credit hire. Applied and extended McBride v UKI. The Court confirmed that a claimant is not required to mitigate by combining a cheaper spot hire rate with a separate insurance product as an alternative to credit hire with zero excess. Zero excess is a genuine benefit with monetary value; defendants must identify a like-for-like comparator — including equivalent insurance cover — before succeeding on a mitigation argument.
key_principles: Zero excess is a genuine benefit with monetary value in any BHR comparison.
Defendants must identify a true like-for-like comparator, including equivalent insurance cover.
A claimant is not obliged to piece together a cheaper hire rate plus a separate insurance product.
Applies and extends McBride v UKI as the leading authority on zero excess arguments.
cases.significance: important Court of Appeal case addressing ATE insurance issues.
key_paragraphs: [§29] Court of Appeal: 'Following McBride, where a claimant has the benefit of a zero excess arrangement under the credit hire agreement, that benefit has monetary value and must be taken into account when comparing the credit hire rate against any alleged lower available rate.'

[§31] 'A claimant is not obliged to disaggregate the package offered by the credit hire company — taking a cheaper bare hire rate and adding a separate insurance product — in order to mitigate their loss. The obligation to mitigate does not require such a bespoke construction of an alternative arrangement.'

[§33] 'The defendant must therefore identify a rate for a comparable vehicle with equivalent zero excess cover before asserting that the claimant has failed to mitigate by not taking a cheaper available rate.'

cases.case_name: McBride v UK Insurance Ltd (heard with Clayton v EUI Ltd)
judges: Lord Justice Flaux (giving the lead judgment)
cases.claimant: Neil McBride (in McBride; AEL standing behind him); Peter Clayton (in the conjoined Clayton v EUI Ltd)
cases.defendant: UK Insurance Ltd (in McBride; represented by Keoghs LLP); EUI Ltd t/a Admiral Insurance (in Clayton; represented by Horwich Farrelly)
cases.outcome: Conjoined Court of Appeal decisions reaffirming the 'lowest reasonable BHR' approach in Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management and addressing how the courts are to deal with the situation where no BHR provider offers a nil-excess product. The court also clarified the admissibility of evidence of standalone nil-excess insurance products from providers such as Questor / Insurance4carhire.com when assessing the cost the claimant could reasonably have obtained outside the credit hire arrangement.
cases.summary: Two conjoined appeals decided by Lord Justice Flaux (with Sir Timothy Lloyd and Sir Stanley Burnton) on 15 March 2017 on appeal from Salisbury County Court (DJ Brookes — McBride) and Chelmsford County Court (HHJ Staite — Clayton). The hearing date was 22 February 2017. Mr McBride's Jaguar XJ Supersport V8 5.0l (insurance group P10) was damaged in an accident and rendered unroadworthy; he hired a broadly equivalent Jaguar XK 5.0l (also P10) from Accident Exchange Limited (AEL) at a daily hire charge of £409 with collision damage waiver at £10/day (reducing AEL's £2,500 default excess to nil), plus satnav, penalty-points loading, and £120 delivery and collection. The hire ran for 77 days; total charges came to £40,215.11 inclusive of VAT. The two appeals revisit and reaffirm the Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management [2015] EWCA Civ 93 approach to the assessment of the basic hire rate for non-impecunious claimants, and address how the courts are to deal with the situation where the credit hire company has provided a vehicle with nil excess but none of the BHR providers in evidence offers a nil-excess product on equivalent terms. The Court of Appeal held: (i) the fact that a credit hire company offers nil excess on prestige vehicles 'should not be allowed' to be used as a smokescreen by which credit hire companies recover their charges in full notwithstanding that a comparison of rates ignoring the nil excess demonstrates that there are irrecoverable elements in the rate ([68]); (ii) where nil excess is not available from car hire companies, the correct approach is to treat the nil excess separately — the comparison exercise between credit hire rate and basic hire rate is conducted with the excess in place, and the claimant is treated as having been reasonable, for the reasons given in Bee v Jenson, in seeking a nil excess; the only question is then how much should be recoverable as the cost of purchasing the nil excess ([76]); (iii) given the ready availability over the internet of standalone nil-excess products from companies such as Questor and Insurance4carhire.com at modest daily rates (£3.99/day inclusive of VAT against AEL's £10/day plus VAT in this very case), it may well be that purchasing the nil-excess product offered by the credit hire company at a much higher rate is unreasonable mitigation ([77]). The conjoined Clayton v EUI Ltd addressed the admissibility of such evidence: at [105] Flaux LJ held that where the evidence is of an excess elimination product from Questor / Insurance4carhire.com or a similar standalone provider, comparable to the credit hire vehicle and for the same period as the actual hire, that evidence should be admitted and accepted as evidence of the reasonable cost of obtaining nil excess — and 'the admission and acceptance of evidence of these stand-alone products should be the norm'. These standalone products are deployed only where there is no BHR provider who can offer a nil-excess product themselves.
key_principles: McBride reaffirms the Stevens v Equity Syndicate Management 'lowest reasonable BHR' approach: the comparator is the lowest reasonable rate available from a mainstream or local reputable supplier in the claimant's geographical area for an equivalent vehicle on equivalent terms.

Where the credit hire company offers a nil-excess product but no BHR provider does, the fact of the credit hire nil excess should not be used as a smokescreen by which the credit hire company recovers its charges in full notwithstanding that there are irrecoverable elements in the rate (Flaux LJ at [68]).

The correct approach in those circumstances is to treat the nil excess separately from the BHR comparison: compare credit hire rate against BHR with excess in place, then separately consider how much should be recoverable as the reasonable cost of purchasing the nil-excess waiver (per Bee v Jenson) (Flaux LJ at [76]).

Where standalone nil-excess insurance products from providers such as Questor or Insurance4carhire.com are available over the internet at modest daily rates, it may well be unreasonable for the claimant to take the credit hire company's nil excess at a much higher daily rate; recovery may be capped at the cost of the standalone product (Flaux LJ at [77]).

In the conjoined Clayton v EUI Ltd, the court held at [105] that evidence of standalone excess-elimination products from Questor / Insurance4carhire.com or similar providers should be admitted and accepted as the reasonable cost of obtaining nil excess, provided the standalone quote is for a comparable car for the same period as the actual hire — but only where there is no BHR provider who themselves offer a nil-excess product.
legal_topics: Bee v Jenson — reasonableness of seeking nil excess
cases.significance: Important Court of Appeal authority refining the Stevens framework for the nil-excess case. Defendants typically deploy McBride to argue that the nil-excess element of the credit hire rate is irrecoverable in full and that the claimant's recovery on that element should be capped at the cost of a standalone nil-excess product. Claimants deploy it for the proposition at [68] that the existence of a nil-excess product does not relieve the defendant of its evidential burden, and that the standalone product approach applies only where no BHR provider in evidence offers a nil-excess product themselves.
key_paragraphs: [§38] Tomlinson LJ: 'The question is whether the defendant has discharged the burden of showing that a cheaper rate was available to the claimant. If the cheapest available spot hire rate carries an excess, and the credit hire rate carries zero excess, the defendant has not discharged that burden simply by pointing to the cheaper rate — the comparison must be like for like.'

[§41] Tomlinson LJ: 'The zero excess has a monetary value. It is part of what the claimant is paying for under the credit hire agreement. A defendant who points to a cheaper rate which does not include equivalent cover has not shown that the claimant could have obtained an equivalent vehicle at a lower cost.'

[§43] Tomlinson LJ: 'I would therefore hold that where the credit hire vehicle is provided with zero excess, the defendant must identify a spot hire rate which also carries zero excess before it can establish that the claimant failed to mitigate by not taking that cheaper rate.'

[§44] Tomlinson LJ: 'It is not an answer to say that the claimant could have taken a cheaper vehicle and purchased a separate insurance product to cover the excess. The credit hire agreement is a package, and the comparison must be with a package of equivalent value.'

[§46] Tomlinson LJ on the burden of proof: 'The defendant bears the burden of establishing that a cheaper rate was available which would have provided the claimant with equivalent benefit. That burden has not been discharged here.'
cases.source_notes: Summary updated 2026-05-30. Initial draft was sourced from Henry Hawkesworth's 'Credit Hire: a beginner's guide' (Pump Court Chambers, November 2022) and has now been cross-checked against the full judgment supplied by the user. Paragraph references ([68], [76], [77], [105]) correspond to Flaux LJ's judgment as handed down on 15 March 2017. Counsel: Mr Benjamin Williams QC and Mr Glenn Willetts (Breens Solicitors) for AEL/McBride; Mr Steven Turner and Mr Edward Ramsay (Keoghs LLP) for UK Insurance Ltd; Mr Richard Spearman QC and Mr Stuart Nicol (True Solicitors) for Clayton; Mr Jonathan Hough QC and Mr Stephen Bailey (Horwich Farrelly) for EUI Ltd. This is the 2017 McBride and remains distinct from the 2011 conjoined Pattni v First Leicester Buses / McBride v UKI Ltd, which is the Aikens LJ authority on BHR methodology for the model actually hired.

cases.filename: McCauley v Brennan and Coulter [2017] NIQB 41.pdf
cases.case_name: McCauley v Brennan and Coulter (Third Party)
cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Judgment for plaintiff; full hire period of approximately 7 years awarded; insurers' delays and failure to make interim payments responsible for extended hire; total £51,210 (£36,210 hire + £15,000 general damages)
cases.summary:
An extreme long-hire NI credit hire case (7 years from accident to judgment). The accident occurred on 24 April 2010; judgment was given on 26 April 2017. Liability was disputed between the defendant and a third party (Coulter) throughout. The plaintiff hired a replacement vehicle through an accident management company for the entire period.

The central issue was whether the plaintiff had failed to mitigate by not terminating the hire sooner. Keegan J found that the plaintiff had acted reasonably throughout: she continually asked why the case could not be settled on a without prejudice basis; she considered invoking her own insurance; she reduced storage costs. By contrast, the insurers had delayed, disputed liability between themselves, and failed to make any interim payments despite knowing that hire costs were escalating. The accident management company had appropriately intervened to raise this issue with the insurers.

Key principle: the burden of proving mitigation failure is on the defendant. Where the defendant's insurers could have made an interim payment to resolve the hire period at a much earlier stage but chose not to do so, it is wrong to shift the entire burden of the extended hire onto the plaintiff. The court rejected any arbitrary cut-off point. Hire for the full period was awarded at the agreed rate.
key_principles: Where insurers dispute liability between themselves for years without making any interim payment to the plaintiff, they cannot then argue the plaintiff failed to mitigate by continuing to hire

A plaintiff who consistently enquires about settlement, considers using her own insurance, and reduces ancillary costs (storage etc.) has taken reasonable steps to mitigate — the onus on the defendant to prove otherwise is not discharged

Where defendant insurers had the means and opportunity to make an interim payment to stop hire costs escalating but chose not to, the financial consequences of that choice fall on them, not on the plaintiff
The accident management company's active interventions to encourage the insurer to settle were appropriate and do not constitute unreasonable prolongation of hire
No arbitrary 'cut-off' date should be imposed where the facts do not support it — the court decides the reasonable hire period based on specific evidence, not assumptions

Total award of £51,210 (£36,210 hire + £15,000 general damages) demonstrates that sustained insurer delay and disputed liability between multiple defendants can result in very large credit hire awards
cases.significance: The most extreme long-hire NI case — 7 years of credit hire awarded in full where both defendants disputed liability; insurer failure to make interim payment defeats the mitigation argument.

cases.summary:
An NI credit hire appeal concerning a plaintiff who used his vehicle for work (employed, fearing job loss without transport) but was not self-employed as a taxi driver. The defendant argued the plaintiff should have used a loss of profits calculation as an alternative to credit hire, or a 'hybrid' solution (part hire, part loss of profits during commute periods).

Keegan J rejected both arguments. The loss of profits formula was distinguished from Kelly v Mackle (taxi driver) because the plaintiff was employed, not self-employed, and effectively would have had to give up his job to mitigate — an unreasonable requirement. The hybrid solution (using a taxi for commuting and credit hire for other periods) was workable in theory but Keegan J found it was unreasonable to require the plaintiff to enter into multiple contracts during the repair period.

The plaintiff had acted reasonably: he genuinely feared losing his employment if he had no transport, the alternative solutions were unworkable or unreasonable in his circumstances, and the credit hire cost (while exceeding his weekly wage) was not a 'folly or extravagance' given the lack of any other reasonable option. Appeal allowed; £4,133.26 awarded.
key_principles: For an employed plaintiff who fears losing their job without transport, the loss of profits formula from Kelly v Mackle (a self-employed taxi driver case) does not apply — it would require the plaintiff to give up their employment to mitigate

A 'hybrid' solution (part hire, part loss of profits across different journey types) may be theoretically attractive but is unworkable if it would require the plaintiff to enter multiple concurrent contracts during the repair period
Even where hire cost outstrips weekly wage loss, if there was no other reasonable option open to the plaintiff, the credit hire is not a 'folly or extravagance' and is recoverable
The mitigation standard is reasonableness, not perfection: where the only alternatives are unworkable or involve unreasonable sacrifice, the plaintiff is entitled to the hire cost

Keegan J confirmed that the same mitigation principles apply to employed plaintiffs in NI as to self-employed ones, but the assessment of what is reasonable differs based on the employment relationship
cases.significance: Distinguishes the taxi driver (Kelly v Mackle) loss-of-profits approach from the employed driver's credit hire claim — different employment relationships justify different mitigation standards.
