cases.case_name: Clayton v EUI Ltd (trading as Admiral Insurance)
cases.outcome: Permission to appeal refused — this is the permission to appeal ruling preceding Clayton v EUI [2017] EWCA Civ 144
cases.summary:
This is the permission to appeal decision in the Clayton litigation, in which LJ Tomlinson considered whether to grant permission to appeal a county court decision concerning credit hire rates for a specialist vehicle. The claimant had a Ford Mustang (an unusual, open-topped American car) and during the protracted repair period hired a sequence of similar vehicles — two BMWs and a Mercedes. The defendant insurer, Admiral, challenged the rate charged.

Tomlinson LJ granted permission to appeal on the basis that the case raised important points of principle about how the credit hire rate and the basic hire rate interact for specialist or unusual vehicles, and how courts should approach the question of the appropriate comparator vehicle. The case value was around £24,000, with the district judge awarding some £13,000.

This decision is primarily of procedural interest as the antecedent to the substantive Clayton v EUI [2017] EWCA Civ 144 judgment which definitively addressed the like-for-like and basic hire rate issues for prestige/specialist vehicles.
key_principles: Permission to appeal may be granted even in credit hire cases of modest value where important points of principle arise
The appropriate comparator for basic hire rate purposes in specialist vehicle cases is a live issue
Credit hire cases involving unusual vehicles raise distinct questions about the like-for-like principle
cases.significance: The permission to appeal ruling in Clayton v EUI — procedurally important as the gateway to the substantive 2017 judgment on specialist vehicle hire rates.

Court: Bradford CC
Date: 26th August 2016

Summary
In a credit hire claim following a road traffic collision, a judge had not erred in
allowing the defendant to rely on a car hire quote obtained on the internet from a
company that was not in the claimant's locality. In an era when the internet was the
first resort for research, the concept of locality was immediately enlarged. Abstract
The claimant appealed against a district judge's decision concerning the car hire
charges that he was entitled to recover from the defendant following a road traffic
collision.

cases.summary:
Leite v Adamietiz [2016] 7 WLUK 34
Court: Lambeth CC
Date: 4th July 2016
Judge: DDJ Linwood
Summary
In assessing special damages in a claim arising from a road traffic accident, the court
emphasised the need for all special damages items to be properly evidenced, and
indicated that claims for "miscellaneous expenses" needed to be itemised and proven. It
also emphasised that the claimant's statement needed to contain sufficient detail so that
it matched his case at the hearing and so that new facts did not emerge at the hearing. Abstract
The court was required to assess special damages in a claim concerning a road
traffic accident for which the defendant admitted liability.

cases.summary: McBride v UK Insurance Ltd — permission to appeal decision (2016), preceding the definitive 2017 Court of Appeal judgment. Tomlinson LJ granted permission to appeal, finding it arguable that the zero excess arrangement negated the defendant's ability to identify a genuinely cheaper available rate. The PTA confirmed the significance of the zero excess point and set the stage for the binding 2017 judgment.
key_principles: Permission granted: the zero excess argument — that it eliminates any cheaper comparator — was held to be arguable.
Precursor to the binding 2017 McBride judgment on zero excess in BHR comparisons.
cases.significance: important Court of Appeal case on credit hire liability and damages.
key_paragraphs: [PTA §1] Tomlinson LJ granting permission: 'It is arguable that the zero excess arrangement provided under the credit hire agreement negates the defendant's ability to point to any cheaper available rate, since no equivalent rate without excess has been identified. Permission to appeal is granted on this ground.'

cases.case_name: Kerr v Toal and conjoined appeals
cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland (Queen's Bench Division)
cases.outcome: Appeals concerned with scope of interrogatories in credit hire cases; guidance given on appropriate interrogatories relating to impecuniosity and need; limited set of interrogatories ordered
cases.summary: Conjoined credit hire appeals in which Burgess J addressed how the issue of impecuniosity is to be raised, pleaded and proven in Northern Ireland. The judgment is the leading NI procedural authority on impecuniosity: it holds that the issue should be addressed at the outset of a claim and supported by financial documentation, including bank statements (typically for the three months pre-accident), proof of income and liabilities, and details of essential outgoings. Per the Umerji line followed in NI, the burden lies on the claimant who asserts impecuniosity, and impecuniosity is not limited to the rate of hire — it can also apply to the duration of hire where the claimant cannot promptly afford to repair or replace their own vehicle. The case is regularly cited alongside Stephens J's decision in South Eastern Health & Social Care Trust v Flanagan [2015] NIQB 30 (impecuniosity is not strictly a question of being penniless — a claimant with funds may still be impecunious if they cannot reasonably be expected to make the sacrifices necessary to pay car hire upfront).
key_principles: Impecuniosity is to be addressed at the outset of a credit hire claim in Northern Ireland.

A claimant relying on impecuniosity must support the case with financial documentation — typically three months of pre-accident bank statements, proof of income and liabilities, and details of essential outgoings.

Impecuniosity applies not only to the rate of hire but to the duration of hire: a claimant who cannot promptly afford to repair or replace their own vehicle may rely on impecuniosity to justify a longer hire period.
The burden of pleading and proving impecuniosity sits on the claimant (per Underhill LJ in Zurich Insurance v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357, followed in NI).

An impecunious claimant is not necessarily 'penniless' — a claimant with funds may still be impecunious if those funds cannot reasonably be sacrificed (Stephens J in SEH&SCT v Flanagan [2015] NIQB 30, regularly cited alongside Kerr).
legal_topics: Umerji burden of proof — followed in NI
cases.significance: Leading Northern Ireland High Court (QBD) authority on the pleading and proof of impecuniosity in credit hire claims. Read alongside SEH&SCT v Flanagan [2015] NIQB 30 (Stephens J) and McCauley v Brennan & Coulter [2017] NIQB 41 (Keegan J), it sets the NI procedural standard for impecuniosity. Not authority on the basic hire rate methodology itself.

cases.filename: South Eastern Health & Social Care Trust v Flanagan and Capper Trading Limited [2015] NIQB 30.pdf
cases.case_name: South Eastern Health and Social Care Trust v Flanagan and Capper Trading Limited
cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Defendant's appeal allowed; award reduced from credit hire rate to Avis daily BHR (£1,810 ex VAT); institutional plaintiff held to have failed to mitigate by not using Avis
cases.summary:
An important NI BHR case where the plaintiff was an NHS Trust (an institutional/commercial plaintiff) rather than a private individual. The Trust hired a vehicle through Crash Services. The defendant proved that Avis at Belfast City Airport offered a substantially lower daily rate.

Applying Burrows v Ross [2014] NIQB 99, Stephens J found that the defendant discharged BOTH stages of the BHR test. At stage 2 (mitigation), the Trust's institutional nature was significant: unlike a private individual with personal circumstances that might justify using Crash, an NHS Trust was in a position to arrange hire with Avis. The Trust had no personal vulnerability or difficulty that would prevent it from accessing the Avis airport counter. The defendant's evidence that Avis cars of equivalent specification were available (with sufficient fleet capacity) was accepted.

On the question of hire period: the court held that since the repairs took 17 days, and it could not reasonably have been anticipated they would take that long, the daily rate was the appropriate benchmark (not a cheaper weekly rate). Award reduced to Avis daily rate of £1,810 (ex VAT).
key_principles: An institutional or commercial plaintiff (such as an NHS Trust) is in a different position from a private individual when assessing mitigation — personal vulnerability is absent, and the Trust could reasonably have arranged hire from Avis

The BHR two-stage test (Burrows v Ross) applies to institutional plaintiffs; at stage 2 (personal circumstances), an institution has fewer personal constraints that might justify using a more expensive credit hire company
Where the defendant proves both BHR availability and that the institutional plaintiff had no personal impediment to using the cheaper alternative, the defendant succeeds on both stages

The appropriate hire rate (daily vs weekly) is determined by what could reasonably have been anticipated at the outset — if the repair duration was unpredictable, the daily rate applies even if a cheaper weekly rate was available
A plaintiff with sufficient fleet capacity to supply a car without an advance booking commitment satisfies the availability element of stage 1 of the BHR test
cases.significance: Establishes that institutional plaintiffs face a higher standard on the BHR mitigation question — personal circumstances arguments that protect individual claimants are weaker for organisations.

cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Defendant's appeal dismissed; plaintiff's cross-appeal allowed; award increased to £2,496.78 — defendant proved BHR difference (Avis Belfast City Airport) but failed to prove plaintiff should have used it given personal circumstances
cases.summary:
A NI credit hire case involving the basic hire rate (BHR) argument. The plaintiff hired from Crash Services. The defendant argued she should have hired from Avis at Belfast City Airport at a lower weekly rate, relying on evidence that Avis cars were available and cheaper.

Stephens J applied a two-stage test: (1) Has the defendant proved a difference between the credit hire rate and the basic hire rate for the same model in the same broad geographical area? (2) Has the defendant proved that the plaintiff failed to mitigate by not using the lower-rate alternative, having regard to her personal circumstances?

Stage 1: Yes — Avis at Belfast City Airport would have charged significantly less for 6 weeks' hire. Avis had sufficient fleet availability (14.7% unutilised = ~2,969 cars nationally).

Stage 2: No — the defendant failed to prove that, given the plaintiff's personal circumstances, it was unreasonable for her to have used Crash rather than travel to Belfast City Airport. The burden falls on the defendant at stage 2 and it was not discharged. Award: £2,496.78.
key_principles: The NI BHR test has two stages: (1) defendant must prove a genuine BHR difference between the credit hire rate and available market rates for the same vehicle type in the same broad area; (2) defendant must also prove the plaintiff failed to mitigate by not using the cheaper alternative
Evidence of an Avis fleet availability figure (14.7% unutilised = ~2,969 cars nationally) is sufficient to prove availability on the balance of probabilities

Even if a cheaper alternative vehicle is available, the defendant must prove the plaintiff unreasonably failed to use it having regard to her specific personal circumstances — the burden remains on the defendant throughout
A plaintiff who had no reason to make a special journey to Belfast City Airport to hire from Avis is not shown to have failed to mitigate simply because Avis was theoretically cheaper
Stephens J's two-stage BHR test is the applicable NI framework for BHR challenges; it applies the same evidential burden structure as Gilheaney v McGovern on credit hire rate disputes
cases.significance: Applies the two-stage BHR test in NI: defendant proves rate difference AND mitigation failure; evidence of Avis availability sufficient on stage 1 but personal circumstances defeated stage 2.

cases.outcome: Defendant's appeal upheld; labour rate claimed (£34/hour) found clearly excessive compared to comparable rates in the area (including rates at the same garage)
cases.summary:
A vehicle repair labour rate dispute involving a Toyota Landcruiser. The plaintiff's garage charged £34/hour for labour. The defendant's motor assessor (Mr Foster) gave evidence of comparable hourly rates in the same geographical area, including two estimates from the very same garage at lower rates. No evidence was adduced on behalf of the plaintiff.

Gillen J applied the Coles case principle (on which Matchett v Hamilton is grounded) — that the measure of loss is the reasonable cost of repair assessed by reference to what the claimant could obtain on the open market. Only where a charge is 'clearly excessive' will the court interfere.

On the facts, Mr Foster's evidence (rates from the same garage at lower figures, plus comparable local market rates) was 'overwhelming'. The gap between £34/hour and the reasonable market rate was so great as to be clearly excessive. The defendant's appeal was allowed and the County Court decision affirmed.
key_principles: The measure of loss for vehicle repair labour is the reasonable cost of repair assessed by reference to what the claimant could obtain on the open market — not necessarily what the chosen garage charged
Only where the claimed rate is 'clearly excessive' compared to comparable local market rates will the court interfere — there is a range of reasonable rates

Evidence of comparable rates from the same garage as that used by the plaintiff is powerful evidence of what is reasonable — the plaintiff cannot claim a rate that even the chosen garage does not consistently charge
The defendant's motor assessor must actually visit the area and obtain comparable estimates; general assertions without local evidence are insufficient

Gillen J applied the 'range or band' concept: the common law does not condemn a higher rate as unreasonable simply because a lower rate also exists — the question is whether the claimed rate falls outside the band of reasonable rates
cases.significance: Confirms that a repair rate clearly exceeding comparable local market rates (including from the same garage) will be reduced; Coles/Matchett approach applied by Gillen J.

§13: From these cases, I have distilled the following principles:

cases.summary: Zurich Insurance plc v Umerji [2014] EWCA Civ 357 is a Court of Appeal decision addressing two key issues in credit hire: (1) the off-hire period following a total loss — the court confirmed that a claimant is entitled to a reasonable period (broadly a fortnight / 14 days) after notification of total loss to make alternative arrangements before hire should end; (2) the scope of a debarring order — which must be construed narrowly. The claimant was impecunious and entitled to credit hire rates. The case is frequently cited for the proposition that a reasonable off-hire period of approximately two weeks runs from when the claimant has a realistic ability to act on the total loss notification, not merely from the date of notification itself.
key_principles: After a claimant receives notice that their vehicle is a total loss, they are entitled to continue hiring a replacement vehicle for a reasonable period to make alternative arrangements. The Court of Appeal held that two weeks (a fortnight) is a reasonable period for off-hire following notification of a total loss settlement.

The assessment of reasonableness in continuing hire after a total loss is fact-specific. The claimant must be given a genuine and workable opportunity to obtain alternative transport before they can be expected to off-hire — simply being told the vehicle is a write-off is not enough if settlement funds have not yet been received.

The debarring order in this case (which prevented the claimant from relying on certain evidence) was construed narrowly by the Court of Appeal. An order debarring a party from relying on evidence goes only so far as its plain terms — it should not be read more broadly than necessary.

Where a claimant is impecunious, credit hire rates rather than the basic hire rate are recoverable. The burden is on the claimant to prove impecuniosity, but once established the full credit hire rate can be recovered including for a reasonable post-total-loss off-hire period.
ATE insurance premiums are not recoverable as a head of loss in credit hire claims (pre-LASPO context) — the Court of Appeal addressed the recoverability of ATE-related costs in this case.
cases.significance: Leading Court of Appeal authority on the reasonable off-hire period (approximately a fortnight) following total loss notification. Also addresses the narrow construction of debarring orders and ATE insurance recoverability in credit hire.

cases.court: Court of Appeal in Northern Ireland
cases.outcome: Defendant's appeal allowed; reasonable hire period reduced from McCloskey J's 164 days to April–end of June 2010 only (approximately 80 days); misplaced advice from Accident Exchange cannot bind the defendant
cases.summary:
The Northern Ireland Court of Appeal's decision on the appeal from McCloskey J's first instance judgment in Clarke v McCullough [2012] NIQB 104. This is the highest NI domestic binding authority on the mitigation question in long-hire credit hire cases (below the UK Supreme Court).

McCloskey J at first instance had found a 'hiatus' at 23 September 2010 — reasoning that until then there had been 'uncertainty and equivocation' about liability and the plaintiff reasonably continued to hire. The Court of Appeal (Girvan LJ, delivering the judgment) disagreed.

The NICA held that liability was clearly and unambiguously in dispute from April 2010. The emails confirmed this 'unambiguously' (per McCloskey J's own finding). The Accident Exchange (AX) advice that the plaintiff was 'entitled' to a replacement vehicle was incorrect and self-serving: AX stood to profit from prolonged hire. A plaintiff cannot shelter behind incorrect advice from their credit hire company to extend the period of hire recoverable from the defendant. AX's breach of contract/fiduciary duty towards the plaintiff, not the defendant, was responsible for the extended hire.

The Court of Appeal fixed the reasonable hire period at April to end of June 2010 only — the maximum period that even the plaintiff's counsel had conceded could be justified.
key_principles: NICA binding authority: where liability is clearly in dispute from an early date, the plaintiff (and their accident management company acting as agent) must take steps to end the hire — continuing to hire in reliance on incorrect AX advice does not extend the defendant's liability

Self-serving advice from an accident management company (telling the plaintiff they are 'entitled' to an indefinite hire period) cannot bind the defendant — the court looks at what a reasonable plaintiff in those circumstances should have done

The 'uncertainty and equivocation' test from Clarke v McCullough first instance is wrong as a matter of law: unambiguous denial of liability from early in the hire period means the mitigation clock runs from that point

Where AX or another credit hire company acts in breach of contract or fiduciary duty in giving incorrect hire advice, the remedy is against AX, not a claim that the defendant must bear extended hire costs

The Court of Appeal reduced the reasonable hire period from approximately 164 days (first instance) to approximately 80 days — representing a very significant reduction in credit hire claims for long-hire cases
This decision is binding on all NI County Courts and the NI High Court; it applies a more stringent mitigation standard than the first instance Clarke judgment
cases.significance: The NICA's most important credit hire mitigation decision: unambiguous early denial of liability means the mitigation duty arises promptly; incorrect AX advice does not extend the defendant's liability — a major reduction from first instance.

cases.outcome: Appeal dismissed — claimants entitled to recover reasonable retail cost of repair
cases.summary: Leading Court of Appeal authority on the recovery of credit repair costs. The court held that the measure of damage for a chattel (vehicle) that has been repaired is the reasonable cost of repair at the time and place of repair — not the cost the at-fault insurer would have incurred through its own managed-repair network. The claimant's loss crystallises at the moment of damage as the diminution in value of the vehicle, and insurance arrangements between the parties are irrelevant (res inter alios acta). The obligation to pay for repairs — not actual payment — gives rise to the recoverable loss. Supreme Court refused permission to appeal in 2014.
key_principles: The measure of damage for a chattel that is damaged but repairable is the diminution in value of the chattel, which is evidenced by the reasonable cost of repair at the time and place of repair (paragraphs 28-29).

The claimant is entitled to recover the reasonable retail cost of repair — not the lower rate that the at-fault insurer would have paid through its own managed-repair network or approved repairer panel.

Insurance arrangements between the parties are irrelevant to the measure of loss under the principle of res inter alios acta. The fact that the at-fault insurer could have arranged cheaper repairs through its own network does not reduce the claimant's entitlement.

The claimant's loss arises from the obligation to pay for the repairs, not from the act of payment itself. A claimant who has incurred a liability for repair costs (e.g. under a credit repair arrangement) can recover that liability as damages even if a third party (such as a CHO) has paid the repair invoice.

The cost of repair is a 'rule of thumb' representing the diminution in value of the vehicle, but may not always represent the full amount of diminution. There may also be a claim for residual depreciation.
A further claim for loss of use of the vehicle (e.g. credit hire charges) is characterised as consequential loss and is a separate head of damage from the repair costs.

The court followed and applied the principles in Jones v Stroud District Council [1986] 1 WLR 1141 (obligation to pay), The London Corporation [1935] P 70 (res inter alios acta), and Bee v Jenson [2007] EWCA Civ 923 (subrogation and credit hire/repair).
The Supreme Court refused permission to appeal (Lords Mance, Sumption, and Hodge) on the basis of no arguable point of law — without the usual caveat about timing.
cases.significance: The leading Court of Appeal authority on credit repair cost recovery. Establishes that the measure of recoverable repair costs is the reasonable retail cost of repair at the time and place of repair — not the at-fault insurer's managed-repair network rate. Foundational to all credit repair claims. Supreme Court refused permission to appeal in 2014.

cases.outcome: Mixed costs order — AEL (Accident Exchange Ltd) was substantially the successful party and recovered the majority of costs, with some reduction
cases.summary:
The costs judgment following the main Dickinson v Tesco appeal [2013] EWCA Civ 36, which concerned the proper approach to Autofocus evidence and the basic hire rate in credit hire cases. AEL (Accident Exchange Ltd, the credit hire company) and AXA (the defendant insurer) both claimed costs, each arguing the other had behaved unreasonably and/or that offers to settle had been unreasonably rejected.

AEL argued it had won the appeal overall and should recover its costs in full. AXA argued it had succeeded on the key issue of whether it should be prevented from adducing further evidence on hire rates at retrial, and that AEL had made excessive settlement demands. Both parties had made various offers to settle.

The Court of Appeal held that AEL was broadly the successful party and should recover the majority of its costs, but with some reduction to reflect AXA's success on certain discrete issues and AEL's conduct in settlement negotiations.
key_principles: The costs of a credit hire appeal follow the general principle that costs follow the event
Where each party has succeeded on different issues, a proportionate costs order reflecting each party's relative success is appropriate
Unreasonable refusal of settlement offers may be reflected in costs orders
The existence of commercial backing (hire company/insurer) behind individual claimants does not change the fundamental costs principles
cases.significance: The costs judgment in the landmark Dickinson v Tesco series; relevant to how courts apportion costs where both parties achieve partial success on appeal in credit hire litigation.

judges: (Trial judge: HHJ Cowell, Central London. Court of Appeal panel: to be confirmed against full transcript.)
cases.claimant: Hardip Singh (appellant; partner in a business partnership owning a fleet of seven vehicles, one of which — the damaged Rolls-Royce — was involved in a minor accident)
cases.outcome: Appeal dismissed. The Court of Appeal upheld HHJ Cowell's finding that, on the evidence, the appellant had failed to discharge the burden of showing a reasonable need for a replacement Rolls-Royce during the period of repair. Hire claim of just under £93,000 dismissed.
cases.summary: Credit hire claim by Mr Singh personally, relying on the need of his business partnership, in respect of a damaged Rolls-Royce that was one of seven fleet vehicles owned by the partnership. The hire claim was for just under £93,000. The Court of Appeal addressed the question of need in a business-use case and applied Lord Mustill's famous statement in Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142 that the need for a replacement car is not self-proving (though for a private motorist, who has gone to the considerable expense of running a vehicle, an inference of need can readily be drawn, subject to 'ample scope' to displace it). HHJ Cowell illustrated the kind of evidence that would be expected in a business-use case by reference to the hypothetical example of a self-employed plumber: detailed evidence of actual pre-accident use of the damaged vehicle, supported by documents such as a diary identifying journeys; and equivalent detail of the post-accident use to which the hired vehicle was in fact put (§28). On the evidence, no such detail had been provided: the only evidence about the use of the Rolls-Royce before the accident was 'very general' and the evidence about the hired vehicle was no better; the use given for the other fleet vehicles included Mr Singh's wife using one to drop and pick up the children from school and his brother using one, of which the judge observed he did not know what it had to do with the partnership (§31). HHJ Cowell held that the existence of six other fleet vehicles displaced the inference of need and that the burden then lay on the claimant to provide detail of actual use, which it had not done; the larger the claim, the greater the cogency of the evidence required (§29). The Court of Appeal upheld that analysis: the burden on the appellant was to show a reasonable need for a replacement Rolls-Royce during the period of repair, the required need being the need of the partnership; need not being proved, detailed questions about the reasonableness of mitigation did not arise (§33). Need had been put in issue in the pleaded defence; had the appellant proved need, the burden of showing that the need had not been met in a reasonable manner would have fallen on the respondent (§38).
key_principles: In a business-use credit hire case, the legal burden of proving need always lies on the claimant; need is not self-proving (Lord Mustill in Giles v Thompson, applied at §27).

An inference of need can be drawn for a private motorist who has incurred considerable expense in running a vehicle, with 'ample scope' to displace it (Giles v Thompson, applied at §27); the case note's view is that a similarly low inference can arise where a business has incurred considerable expense in running a vehicle for business purposes — though Hardip itself does not decide that.
The inference can be displaced — and was displaced in Hardip — by evidence that there were other vehicles available to the business (the partnership had six other fleet vehicles).

Once the inference is displaced, the burden shifts to the claimant to provide specific evidence of need: detailed evidence of the actual use of the damaged vehicle for business purposes before the accident and detailed evidence of the use to which the hired vehicle was in fact put (§§28-29, 39).
If detailed evidence of need is not provided, the question of reasonableness of mitigation does not arise (§33).

If detailed evidence of need IS provided, the evidential burden then shifts back to the defendant to show that the need had not been met in a reasonable manner — for example by demonstrating that one of the other fleet vehicles could in fact have substituted for the damaged vehicle (§38).
legal_topics: burden of proof — need vs mitigation
Giles v Thompson — Lord Mustill inference
specific evidence — pre-accident and post-hire use
cases.significance: Important Court of Appeal authority on the question of 'need' in business-use credit hire claims. The case note treats it as the leading appellate decision on the structure of inference, displacement and shifting evidential burden in business-fleet cases. Not authority on the assessment of the basic hire rate itself — Pattni / McBride [2011] (Aikens LJ) governs that question, and Singh applies Pattni at §32.

cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Defendant's appeal dismissed; judge at first instance correctly applied Matchett principles to particular evidential matrix; no universal hourly rate rule
cases.summary:
An ex tempore appeal judgment clarifying the scope and effect of Matchett v Hamilton [2011] NIQB 131 on vehicle repair labour rates. The defendant argued that Matchett established a universal rule that the correct NI hourly labour rate for vehicle repairs is £26/hour.

McCloskey J firmly rejected this. Matchett was an evidential finding on the particular facts of that case, not a legal rule creating a universal rate. The judge at first instance in Bates had correctly applied the Matchett methodology: adjudicating on conflicting evidence about what was a reasonable hourly rate in the particular case, applying burden and standard of proof. That is the paradigm approach; the result depends on the evidence in each case.

McCloskey J added an important practical observation: if the motor insurance and vehicle repair industries have found it convenient to use Matchett as a tool to settle disputes at a particular hourly rate, that is a welcome commercial practice — but it is industry convenience, not a legal rule. Reasonableness is contextual and evolves over time.
key_principles: Matchett v Hamilton [2011] NIQB 131 does NOT establish a universal rule that £26/hour is the correct labour rate in Northern Ireland — it is an evidential finding on its particular facts
Every vehicle repair labour rate dispute must be decided on the evidence adduced in that case; the paradigm approach is adjudicating on conflicting evidence, applying burden and standard of proof

An industry practice of using Matchett as a convenient settlement benchmark is welcome but is commercial practice, not a legally mandated rate — Matchett cannot be cited as legal authority for a particular figure
Reasonableness of a labour rate is contextual and evolves: what was reasonable in 2011 may not be the same in later years, and different geographical areas may have different market rates

The correct methodology is to assess what a reasonable repair rate is in the particular area and circumstances by reference to actual evidence from the market — not by reference to an assumed NI-wide standard
cases.significance: Prevents misuse of Matchett as a universal NI hourly rate rule; confirms the evidence-based, case-specific approach to all repair cost disputes.

cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Judgment for plaintiff but damages reduced from £32,380.97 to £12,408.37; hire period limited to 164 days on mitigation grounds; non-standard driver charge irrecoverable
cases.summary:
The plaintiff's vehicle was damaged in a road traffic accident on 10 April 2010 when the defendant opened a parked car door into his moving vehicle. Liability was denied throughout (the court found this 'baffling' and described it as 'quite hopeless'). The plaintiff engaged Accident Exchange as his accident management company, which arranged five successive credit hire agreements covering a total period of 328 days (12 April 2010 to 5 March 2011). A protection insurance policy was taken out; when the hire period concluded, the underwriter (IGI/AM Trust) paid Accident Exchange £19,676 as the plaintiff's liability under the agreements. Accident Exchange then pursued the defendant for £32,380.97 by way of subrogated claim.

McCloskey J applied the same eleven principles of NI credit hire law set out in Smyth v Diamond [2010] NIQB 74. On enforceability: the second and third of the five agreements (162 of 328 days) were unenforceable under the Cancellation of Contracts Made in a Consumer's Home Regulations 2008 for lack of a compliant cancellation notice. However, following W v Veolia ES [2011] EWHC 2020, enforceability was irrelevant: the charges had already been paid by the plaintiff through his protection insurance policy, giving rise to an accrued financial loss fully recoverable in tort.

On mitigation: the court held that by 23 September 2010 (commencement of the fourth hire agreement, at which point ~£12,000 had accrued and the daily rate was rising to ~£105), the plaintiff should have mitigated by arranging repair of his own vehicle at an out-of-pocket cost of ~£2,500. His vehicle could have been repaired through a credit card facility. Hire charges were therefore limited to the first 164 days. The non-standard driver charge of £7.17/day was disallowed as an unexplained additional benefit. Storage charges were also proportionately reduced.
key_principles: Once credit hire charges have been paid by the plaintiff (even through a protection insurance policy), the question of enforceability of the underlying agreements is irrelevant: the plaintiff has an accrued financial loss recoverable in tort (W v Veolia ES [2011] EWHC 2020 followed)
The claimant may recover damages for a loss incurred through the mechanism of a protection insurance policy; this is treated as a payment by the plaintiff for the purposes of the law of damages

The Cancellation of Contracts Made in a Consumer's Home Regulations 2008 (Reg 7(4)) require that any cancellation notice be 'incorporated in the same document' as the hire agreement; failure renders the agreement unenforceable against the consumer

Agreements and cancellation notices constitute proper 'incorporation' under the 2008 Regulations where they form part of a package received, considered and signed by the plaintiff simultaneously, even without physical attachment

On mitigation: once the repair cost is within the plaintiff's financial reach (credit card; ability to pay without unreasonable sacrifice) and there is no imminent prospect of liability being admitted, the plaintiff must take steps to repair his own vehicle rather than continuing to incur escalating credit hire charges

A 'non-standard driver' charge is irrecoverable if unexplained and unsupported by evidence — it is treated either as an unjustified expenditure in mitigation or an additional benefit to be stripped out
McCloskey J applied his own eleven principles from Smyth v Diamond [2010] NIQB 74; NI credit hire law is thus built on a coherent body of High Court authority specific to Northern Ireland
The court strongly criticised the failure of the defendant's insurers to admit liability throughout, describing it as 'quite hopeless' and the outcome as 'not less than inevitable'

Where a credit hire company pursues a subrogated claim, it is the real claimant and its conduct (including accepting higher daily rates and delaying advice on repair) is relevant to the assessment of damages
NI High Court decision: binds NI County Courts; English decisions on the 2008 Regulations and Veolia are applied as persuasive authority by NI courts
cases.significance: Establishes in Northern Ireland that once credit hire charges have been paid (including through a protection insurance policy), enforceability of the agreement is irrelevant; also applies the NI mitigation principles to limit hire recovery where the plaintiff could have repaired within their financial means.

cases.filename: Larcombe v. Arnold Clark Automobile Ltd Approved Judgment 28.02.11.doc
cases.case_name: Larcombe v Arnold Clark Automobiles Ltd
cases.outcome: Claimant succeeded — the notice of right to cancel was sufficiently incorporated into the credit hire agreement
cases.summary:
The third judgment in a prolonged credit hire dispute, addressing the discrete but commercially significant question of whether the notice of right to cancel required under the Consumer Credit Act 1974 and the associated regulations was properly incorporated into the credit hire agreement. The hire documentation provided to Mr Larcombe at the time of delivery comprised separate pages — the agreement itself and a notice of right to cancel — and the defendant argued that because the notice was not contained on the face of the agreement, it was not 'included' in the same document as required by the regulations, rendering the agreement unenforceable.

HHJ Cotter QC reviewed six conflicting county court decisions on the same point, noting with 'no little concern' the volume of satellite litigation on this issue. He held that substance must prevail over form: the notice of right to cancel cross-referenced the agreement and was signed at the same time. There was sufficient cross-referencing between the two documents for them to be treated as a single document for regulatory purposes. The agreement was therefore enforceable. The judge expressed a preference for the approach of HHJ Armstrong in Orley over that of HHJ Vosper in Guerrero.
key_principles: Whether a notice of right to cancel is 'incorporated' in a credit hire agreement is a question of substance, not form
A notice of right to cancel that cross-references the agreement and is signed simultaneously can be treated as part of the same document
The Consumer Credit Act regulations require the notice to be included in the same document as the agreement, but this can be satisfied by cross-referencing where the documents are signed together
Courts should prefer the analysis of substance over technical arguments about document format in consumer credit cases
cases.significance: County Court authority on whether a separately-delivered notice of right to cancel is properly incorporated into a credit hire agreement — relevant to any credit hire enforceability challenge based on document format.

Applied Lagden v O'Connor — full credit hire rate recoverable without stripping out additional benefits to reach spot rate. Defendant adduced no evidence to displace the prima facie invoice amount. Delivery/collection charge of £62 also awarded in full as defendant produced no evidence against it; the first instance reduction to £45 was arbitrary.
key_principles: The Lagden v O'Connor impecuniosity principle applies in NI: a plaintiff without credit card or savings who could not afford spot hire recovers the full credit hire rate
An 18-year-old on £140/week net with no credit card and no savings qualifies as impecunious under Lagden — full Crash credit hire rate recoverable
Where the defendant adduces no evidence to challenge any component of the credit hire invoice, a reduction at first instance is arbitrary and will be reversed on appeal
The defendant must adduce evidence against each separate invoice component; bare assertions or intuitive reductions without evidential basis will not stand

Impecuniosity under Lagden in NI: the dividing line is inability to pay without making sacrifices the plaintiff could not reasonably be expected to make — lack of any credit/debit card is the practical touchstone
cases.significance: Applies Lagden impecuniosity in NI; establishes that an 18-year-old on modest wages with no credit card qualifies; evidentially unsupported invoice reductions reversed.

cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Plaintiff's appeal dismissed; Crash's hourly repair rate of £35 unreasonable (25-30% above market); additional driver and delivery charges also disallowed
cases.summary: Influential vehicle repair labour rate case. Crash garage charged £35/hour; substantial majority of NI repairers charged £26/hour. McCloskey J upheld the £26 rate as the reasonable market rate. Critical warning: Matchett does NOT create a universal £26/hour rule — it is an evidential finding on particular facts; each case decided on its own evidence. Additional driver charge disallowed (plaintiff's own policy covered it); collection/delivery charge disallowed (no service actually provided).
key_principles: Matchett v Hamilton does NOT establish a universal £26/hour NI labour rate — it is an evidential finding on its particular facts; each case must be decided on its own evidence
A vehicle repair labour rate 25-30% above what a substantial majority of local repairers charge is unreasonable and will be reduced to the market rate
The prima facie invoice principle applies to labour rates: if the defendant proves the rate exceeds the reasonable market rate, the court substitutes the market figure
An additional driver charge is irrecoverable where the plaintiff's personal insurance already covered additional drivers on the replacement vehicle
A collection/delivery charge is irrecoverable where no such service was actually provided — a purely fictitious charge
Industry use of Matchett as a convenient settlement tool at a particular figure is commercial practice, not a legally mandated rate
cases.significance: Leading NI vehicle repair labour rate authority; often misread as a universal £26/hour rule — it is not; also disallows fictitious charges in Crash invoices.

cases.outcome: Plaintiff's appeal allowed; decree varied from £1,532.44 to £1,778.50; ABI rate rejected; VAT at current rate (20%) applies
cases.summary: Restatement of 11 governing principles of NI credit hire law. Critical postscript: some NI County Courts had been applying the ABI rate as the correct measure of damages — McCloskey J emphatically rejected this, stating strict adherence to the 11 principles is required. Also held: VAT at the rate current at time of payment (not invoice date) applies; interest unlikely to be recoverable in standard credit hire cases.
key_principles: The ABI rate is NOT the applicable standard for credit hire damages in Northern Ireland — any NI court approach that treats the ABI rate as the measure of damages is incorrect
Strict and faithful adherence to the 11 governing principles from Smyth v Diamond [2010] NIQB 74 is required in all NI credit hire cases
VAT at the rate applicable at the time of payment applies to credit hire awards — not the rate when the invoice was generated
Interest is unlikely to be recoverable in standard NI credit hire cases given expedition of County Court processing and the commercial nature of the beneficiary
Where the defendant adduces no evidence to displace the prima facie invoice amount, the full invoice amount is recoverable
An amended invoice specifying the correct current VAT rate should be included in agreed documentary evidence where the rate has changed
cases.significance: Definitive NI rejection of the ABI rate as a credit hire measure; restatement of 11 principles; critical warning that some NI courts were wrongly applying the ABI benchmark.

cases.outcome: Defendant's appeal allowed; entire credit hire claim dismissed — pre-existing door handle defect (not caused by defendant) was effective cause of vehicle being unroadworthy
cases.summary: Causation case. The plaintiff's vehicle had a pre-existing defect — a faulty front driver's door handle rendering it unroadworthy under road traffic legislation — which predated and was unrelated to the defendant's negligence. McCloskey J found this defect was the real and effective cause of the vehicle being unable to be legally driven. No causal nexus between the defendant's tort and the credit hire claim. Claim dismissed entirely.
key_principles: A credit hire claim requires a direct causal nexus between the defendant's tort and the plaintiff's need for a replacement vehicle — if a pre-existing defect is the effective cause, the claim fails
Where a vehicle cannot be legally driven due to a pre-existing defect unrelated to the defendant's negligence, credit hire incurred after that defect rendered it unroadworthy is irrecoverable
The causal nexus question is fact-sensitive: the court examines what was the real and effective cause of the vehicle's condition at each stage of the hire
Expert motor assessor evidence identifying a pre-existing defect (e.g. defective door handle causing RTA non-compliance) can be decisive and defeat the entire hire claim
Introducing new expert evidence on appeal that was not before the first instance court may attract adverse costs orders even if the appeal succeeds
cases.significance: NI causation authority: pre-existing defect (unrelated to defendant) that independently renders vehicle unroadworthy breaks the causal link and defeats the entire credit hire claim.

cases.filename: Pattni v First Leicester [2011] EWCA Civ 1384.pdf
cases.case_name: Pattni v First Leicester Buses Ltd (conjoined with McBride v UKI Ltd)
cases.claimant: Saira Pattni (in Pattni); Mr McBride (in conjoined McBride v UKI Ltd)
cases.defendant: First Leicester Buses Ltd; UK Insurance Ltd (in the conjoined appeal)
cases.outcome: Conjoined Court of Appeal decisions clarifying (a) that contractual interest payable by a non-impecunious hirer under the terms of a credit hire agreement is not recoverable in damages from the negligent driver's insurer, and (b) that the basic hire rate is to be ascertained by reference to the model of vehicle actually hired in the same broad geographical area; where that model sits in a specialist market, small specialist comparators are properly admissible and the trial judge had been wrong to characterise their use as unreasonable.
cases.outcome_party: mixed (claimant succeeded on the BHR comparator point; defendants succeeded on the contractual-interest point)
cases.summary: Two conjoined appeals on consequential issues arising from the hire of a replacement vehicle on credit terms by hirers who could have afforded to hire without credit (i.e. who were not impecunious in the Lagden sense). Two principal questions arose. (1) Was the contractual interest the innocent driver was obliged to pay under the credit hire agreement recoverable from the negligent driver's insurer? The Court of Appeal held that, where the hirer is not impecunious, contractual interest of that kind is not a recoverable loss; the hirer's recoverable damages are confined to the basic hire rate of an equivalent replacement vehicle. Aikens LJ accepted in principle that a claimant may recover damages where he can prove he has suffered loss of money by reason of the tort, but on the facts the innocent driver had not actually paid out any interest, so no such loss had been incurred (the claim for discretionary statutory interest also failed for the same reason). Aikens LJ took the opportunity to disapprove the term 'spot rate', preferring 'basic hire rate'. (2) How is the basic hire rate to be calculated? Once it has been established that the claimant needed to hire a replacement and that the type of car actually hired was a reasonable choice, the court ascertains the basic hire rate for that model in the same broad geographical area, which is the rate the defendant has to pay. In Pattni itself the reasonable replacement had been a 2007 Aston Martin. The trial judge had treated the claimant's reliance on figures from 'very small scale specialist hirers' as unreasonable. The Court of Appeal held that finding was wrong on the facts: the market for that type of vehicle is specialist, so specialist hirers are admissible comparators; mainstream rates remain relevant evidence where available.
key_principles: Where the hirer is not impecunious, the recoverable damages are confined to the basic hire rate of an equivalent replacement vehicle. Contractual interest payable by the hirer to the credit hire company under the hire agreement is not a recoverable head of loss against the negligent driver's insurer.

A claimant may in principle recover damages for loss of money sustained by reason of a tort, but only where such loss has actually been incurred. A contractual liability to interest that the hirer has not in fact paid does not constitute a recoverable loss.
Aikens LJ disapproved the use of the term 'spot rate'; the proper terminology is 'basic hire rate' (BHR).

The BHR is calculated by reference to the model of vehicle actually hired in the same broad geographical area, once it is established that the claimant needed to hire a replacement and that the type of vehicle hired was a reasonable choice.

Where the market for the vehicle actually hired is specialist (e.g. a 2007 Aston Martin), figures from small-scale specialist hirers are admissible and a trial judge errs in treating reliance on them as unreasonable; mainstream rates remain relevant evidence if available.
cases.significance: Leading Court of Appeal authority (conjoined with McBride v UKI Ltd) on the calculation of the basic hire rate for non-impecunious claimants. Especially important for prestige / specialist-vehicle BHR cases, where it is authority that the comparator pool is the market for the vehicle actually hired in the same broad geographical area — and that small specialist hirers are properly admissible where that is the relevant market.
cases.source_notes: Summary updated 2026-05-30 from the user-supplied case note covering the Court of Appeal's decision in the conjoined appeals (BAILII: http://www.bailii.org/ew/cases/EWCA/Civ/2011/1384.html). Note this 2011 decision is distinct from McBride v UK Insurance [2017] EWCA Civ 144, which is a separate appeal on intervention rates and is tracked under case id 'mcbride-v-uki-2017-ewca-civ-144'.

cases.outcome: Defendants' appeal dismissed; Crash Services agreement held NOT to be a restricted use credit agreement or debtor-creditor agreement under CCA 1974
cases.summary: Novel CCA 1974 argument: defendants argued the Crash Services agreement was a 'restricted use credit agreement' under s.11(1)(c) (refinancing). McCloskey J rejected this: at the date of signing, the plaintiff had no pre-existing debt to refinance. The garage repair invoice only arose after the agreement was executed. Section 11(1)(c) was not engaged. The agreement was not a regulated credit agreement on this basis. Fact-specific finding.
key_principles: A Crash Services credit hire agreement executed before any repair invoice is issued is NOT a 'restricted use credit agreement' under CCA 1974 s.11(1)(c) — no pre-existing indebtedness to refinance
'Refinancing' under s.11(1)(c) requires pre-existing financial provision/indebtedness — a credit hire agreement that is the first and only financing is not refinancing
The relevant date for assessing existing indebtedness is the date of execution of the credit hire agreement, not when the repair invoice is later issued
This finding is explicitly fact-specific: different facts (pre-existing debt at date of agreement) could reach a different conclusion
Closes off the 'refinancing/restricted use' CCA 1974 argument in NI where the plaintiff has no pre-existing debt at signing
cases.significance: NI authority closing off the CCA 1974 'refinancing' argument against Crash Services agreements where no pre-existing debt exists at date of signing.

cases.summary: Four conjoined interlocutory appeals on discovery/interrogatories/witness summonses in NI credit hire cases. McCloskey J gave guidance: correspondence should be the primary mechanism for information exchange; formal applications are a last resort. County Court appeals are re-hearings — parties who change the evidential framework on appeal without justification risk adverse costs. Excessive interlocutory applications are inimical to the overriding objective.
key_principles: Pre-trial correspondence should be the primary mechanism for information exchange in NI credit hire cases — formal interlocutory applications are a last resort
County Court credit hire appeals are re-hearings; parties who substantially change the evidential framework on appeal without justification risk adverse costs orders
The overriding objective and proportionality limit the scope of interlocutory applications — costs of skirmishing may exceed the amount in dispute
A witness summons is valid if the documents are specifically identified, non-speculative, and necessary for fair disposal — targeted production does not offend Burdis v Livsey
Uncooperative plaintiffs who fail to respond to reasonable pre-trial enquiries will pay the price in costs; the court will draw appropriate inferences
cases.significance: McCloskey J's guidance on proportionate NI credit hire pre-trial procedure; correspondence first, formal applications last; re-hearing character of County Court appeals.

cases.filename: Smyth v Diamond; Phillips v Ritchie; Torrens v Tally; McCabe v Moffett [2010] NIQB 74.pdf
cases.case_name: Smyth v Diamond; Phillips v Ritchie; Torrens v Tally; McCabe v Moffett
cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Three plaintiff appeals (Smyth, Phillips, Torrens) allowed with agreed decrees; Defendant's appeal in McCabe dismissed
cases.summary:
Four conjoined appeals to the Northern Ireland High Court from District Judges across various County Court Divisions, concerning credit hire claims arising out of road traffic accidents. All four cases involved the same MIS Claims Service/Independent Car Hire (ICH) arrangement in which MIS (a motor claims management company) referred plaintiffs to approved repairers and facilitated credit hire agreements. The court comprehensively reviewed the governing principles of credit hire law in Northern Ireland, drawing extensively on English House of Lords authority (Giles v Thompson, Dimond v Lovell, Lagden v O'Connor) and the Northern Ireland Court of Appeal decision in O'Hagan v Wright.

The principal defence argument — that the MIS/ICH credit hire agreements were unenforceable, or that the Salt v Helley [2009] NIQB 69 conflict-of-interest principle applied to defeat the claims — was rejected. McCloskey J held that the standard MIS agreement was valid and enforceable, that the plaintiffs were literate adults who had signed binding documents, and that the non est factum defence had no application. Salt v Helley was distinguished on its facts: the specific non-disclosure and conflict of interest found in Salt were not replicated here, and the agency issue had not been properly argued before the court. The novus actus argument in Phillips (where Wrights diverted the plaintiff from her own insurance to MIS) was rejected on res inter alios acta grounds.

In McCabe (the only Defendant appeal), the past consideration argument was dismissed: substantial benefits postdating execution of the written agreement formed valid consideration, and an oral contract had in any event been concluded before the formal documents were signed. The court affirmed eleven governing principles of Northern Ireland credit hire law and emphasised that English House of Lords decisions on credit hire (Giles, Dimond, Lagden) form the basis of NI law, while decisions of the NI High Court bind NI County Courts.
key_principles: The standard MIS/ICH credit hire agreement is valid and enforceable in Northern Ireland: it constitutes a genuine commercial hiring and is not champertous
A plaintiff who signs a credit hire agreement as a literate adult in full possession of their faculties is bound by it; the non est factum defence is of narrow scope and rarely available

Salt v Helley [2009] NIQB 69 (agent's conflict of interest defeating credit hire recovery) is fact-sensitive and was distinguished: absent clear evidence of agency and non-disclosure, it does not apply
The res inter alios acta principle applies: a tortfeasor cannot require the plaintiff to invoke insurance policy entitlements (including a courtesy car) to mitigate loss

A novus actus interveniens argument fails where a repairer diverts a plaintiff from insurance provision to a credit hire company — this is a foreseeable commercial reality, not an extraneous new cause
Past consideration does not vitiate a credit hire agreement where substantial benefits were conferred both before and after formal execution, or where an oral contract was concluded in advance

McCloskey J's eleven principles of NI credit hire law: restitutio in integrum; reasonable necessity; duty to mitigate; expenditure must be reasonable; prima facie credit hire invoice amount is damages measure; onus on defendant to prove mitigation failure; additional benefits principle; spot rate measure; Lagden adjustment for impecunious plaintiffs; res inter alios acta
Northern Ireland High Court decisions on credit hire bind Northern Ireland County Courts; English High Court decisions do not have binding force in Northern Ireland
Copley v Lawn (CA, England) applied: an insurer's offer of a free vehicle does not require the plaintiff to accept it unless the insurer discloses the true cost, enabling a fair comparison
cases.significance: The foundational Northern Ireland High Court judgment on the enforceability of standard MIS/ICH credit hire agreements and the governing principles of NI credit hire law; essential reading for any NI practitioner, as English County Court and High Court decisions on these points do not bind NI courts.

cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Plaintiff's appeal allowed; pre-accident vehicle value £2,850 (retail); repair cost £2,298.97 reasonable and recoverable; decree varied to £3,147.10
cases.summary: Vehicle value/repair cost case. Liability admitted; credit hire agreed. Dispute: should the vehicle have been a write-off? Repair cost ~£2,300; plaintiff's assessor said £2,850 retail pre-accident value; defendant's assessor said £2,350 mid-book. McCloskey J held: (1) retail price is the correct valuation method, not mid-book (an insurance industry convention); (2) the write-off test is not rigid arithmetic — question is whether repair cost 'greatly exceeds' pre-accident value; (3) £2,298 vs £2,350 net value = repair justified. Also develops credit hire prima facie invoice principle for repair costs.
key_principles: For vehicle repair claims, the prima facie measure of damages is the repair cost; the defendant bears the onus of proving this is unreasonable — mirrors the credit hire invoice principle
The write-off threshold is not rigid arithmetic: the question is whether repair cost 'greatly exceeds' or is 'very much more than' pre-accident value (Darbishire v Warran; The Minnehaha)
Retail price (what a motor dealer charges) is the correct pre-accident vehicle value — mid-book (Glass's Guide midpoint) is an insurance industry convention, not a legal standard
Glass's Guide mid-book is a mechanism of convenience between insurers; courts must assess the actual market value based on all the evidence
A modest margin between repair cost and pre-accident value does not displace the normal repair measure; only a 'greatly exceeding' difference does so
Stokes establishes in NI that repair cost claims follow the same prima facie/defendant burden framework as credit hire rate claims
cases.significance: Establishes NI prima facie repair cost rule and retail price as pre-accident vehicle valuation standard; write-off test requires greatly excessive disparity, not mere arithmetic excess.

cases.filename: Turley v Black and PSNI [2010] NIQB 1.pdf
cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Defendants' interlocutory application for witness summons upheld; foundational credit hire framework for NI established
cases.summary: McCloskey J's first NI credit hire judgment. Established the taxonomy and framework for NI credit hire litigation: three types of appeals (substantive, interlocutory, interlocutory from Master); typical features of credit hire cases. An interlocutory dispute over a witness summons requiring Crash Services to disclose trade rate documents. The District Judge's witness summons order was upheld: documents specifically identified, non-speculative, necessary for fair disposal. Sets the template for all subsequent McCloskey J credit hire decisions.
key_principles: Turley v Black [2010] NIQB 1 establishes the foundational NI credit hire framework: McCloskey J identifies three types of NI credit hire appeals (substantive, interlocutory, and interlocutory from Master) and the typical features of all credit hire cases

In interlocutory disputes (discovery, interrogatories, witness summonses), documents must be specifically identified, non-speculative, and necessary for fair disposal — these conditions were satisfied for trade rate disclosure
The trade rate (the lower rate at which Crash sources vehicles) is potentially relevant evidence of the reasonableness of the credit hire rate and defendants are entitled to explore it
The overriding objective governs all NI credit hire litigation; candour and early settlement are paramount; formal applications should be proportionate to amounts in dispute
NI High Court decisions bind NI County Courts; Turley is the starting point for the entire McCloskey J credit hire line
cases.significance: McCloskey J's first credit hire decision — establishes NI credit hire litigation framework; template for all subsequent NI credit hire jurisprudence.

Company Call Centre Technology Ltd v Sheehan
Court: County Court (Birmingham)
Judgment Date: 26 February 2009
Where Reported: [2009] 2 WLUK 736
Judge: HHJ Worster
Summary
A judge was not wrong in refusing to order the defendant in a road traffic accident to
pay the claimant's car hire charges, as there were sufficient ambiguities, doubts and
holes in the claimant's evidence as to the existence of an agreement between it and the
hire company, and therefore in the liability for them to pay the hire charges. Abstract
The appellant company (C) appealed against a decision refusing to order the
respondent (S) to pay car hire charges as part of a road traffic accident claim. S had admitted liability for driving into a vehicle driven by C's employee (F).

cases.filename: Gilheaney v McGovern and McGovern [2009] NIQB 38.pdf
cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Plaintiff's appeal allowed; full Crash Services credit hire rate awarded as defendant failed to prove Reliable Cars Ltd was a reasonably available and discoverable alternative
cases.summary: Plaintiff used Crash Services (higher rate). Defendant argued plaintiff should have used Reliable Cars Limited (cheaper, second-tier subscriber). Defendant failed both stages of the mitigation test: (1) Reliable Cars had no visible presence in County Fermanagh; (2) plaintiff was sitting A-level exams. Defendant could not establish that the plaintiff knew or ought to have known of the cheaper alternative. Appeal allowed: full Crash rate of £1,092 plus VAT awarded.
key_principles: The defendant bears the burden of proving both (a) a cheaper credit hire alternative was available in the relevant area AND (b) the plaintiff ought reasonably to have used it
A credit hire company with no visible local presence fails the 'availability' limb — defendant cannot rely on a national company unknown in the plaintiff's area
A plaintiff sitting A-level exams is not required to undertake internet searches for cheaper credit hire alternatives — reasonableness is assessed in context
If the defendant fails to prove the plaintiff knew or ought to have known of a cheaper alternative, the mitigation argument fails and the full credit hire rate is awarded
Copley v Lawn applied in NI: the defendant must disclose the true cost and practical availability of any alternative before criticising the plaintiff's choice
cases.significance: Key NI authority: defendant's burden to prove both availability and discoverability of cheaper alternative; no visible local presence = defendant fails.

cases.filename: Gilheaney v McGovern and McGovern No. 2 [2009] NIQB 46.pdf
cases.case_name: Gilheaney v McGovern and McGovern (No. 2 — Costs)
cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Costs orders made: Gilheaney — plaintiff awarded costs; Kelly — plaintiff costs below, defendant costs on appeal
cases.summary: Costs ruling following Gilheaney v McGovern [2009] NIQB 38 and Kelly v Mackle [2009] NIQB 39. Guidance on proportionate costs in NI credit hire appeals. Normal costs orders; question of Senior Counsel costs for Taxing Master. Party cannot double costs by securing full County Court scale and equivalent again on appeal.
key_principles: Normal costs orders apply in NI credit hire appeals; the successful party recovers costs but proportionality and the amount genuinely in issue on appeal are relevant
Whether Senior Counsel costs are recoverable in a credit hire appeal is for the Taxing Master under Order 62 Appendix 2 — no judicial certification required
A plaintiff who succeeded at first instance but recovered less on appeal may be ordered to pay the defendant's appeal costs even though the overall decree remains in their favour
The amount genuinely in issue on appeal (not the total claim) determines the applicable costs scale in NI credit hire appeals
Parties cannot 'double' their costs by securing County Court scale costs plus equivalent High Court costs on appeal

cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Plaintiff's appeal partially allowed; hire awarded at trade rate £55/day × 34 days = £1,870 (not claimed credit hire rate of £227/day)
cases.summary: Taxi driver claimed £227/day credit hire rate through Crash Services. Crash's trade rate to Loughshore Autos was £55/day. Agreement was unenforceable under CCA 1974. Per Bee v Jenson, plaintiff still recovers general damages for loss of use but at the reasonable market (spot) rate. No spot rate evidence adduced. Court used trade rate of £55/day as the reference. Award: 34 days × £55 = £1,870.
key_principles: Where a credit hire agreement is unenforceable under CCA 1974, the plaintiff still recovers general damages for loss of use — but at the reasonable spot rate, not the credit hire rate
In the absence of spot rate evidence, the court may fix damages by reference to the trade rate paid by the credit hire company to its vehicle supplier
The trade rate becomes the ceiling where no better evidence of the spot rate is adduced; it is not automatically equivalent to the spot rate
Bee v Jenson applied in NI: a plaintiff who reasonably hired a replacement vehicle recovers reasonable hire cost regardless of contractual liability
Taxi drivers must adduce spot rate evidence for comparable taxis; otherwise recovery is limited to whatever trade rate evidence exists
cases.significance: Establishes in NI that unenforceable agreements limit recovery to spot rate; trade rate used as reference in absence of spot rate evidence.

cases.filename: Park Lane BMW v Whipp [2009] 5 WLUK 481.pdf
cases.case_name: Park Lane BMW Ltd v Whipp
judges: His Honour Judge Charles Harris QC
cases.outcome: Defendant succeeded — BMW 645 coupé was not a like-for-like replacement for a BMW 745 saloon; hire charges rejected in full
cases.summary:
NOTE: This is a County Court decision with no binding authority. It is fact-specific and is not referenced in hostile litigation. The like-for-like principle is better established through binding Court of Appeal authorities.

Park Lane BMW Ltd, a large car dealership, owned a BMW 745 long-wheelbase saloon which was damaged in April 2004 through the admitted negligence of Ms Whipp. Rather than hiring a comparable vehicle while repairs were carried out, Park Lane hired from Accident Exchange a BMW 645 Coupé — an expensive two-seat sports touring car wholly different in character from the damaged vehicle — at over £450 per day for 39 days, a total of approximately £20,700 including VAT.

HHJ Harris QC rejected the claim for the hire charges in full. He found that the vehicle hired bore no reasonable relationship to the vehicle damaged. The damaged car was a large limousine used for Park Lane's tourist and diplomatic department; a two-seat sports coupé was not a like-for-like replacement.
key_principles: A claimant is only entitled to recover the cost of a genuine like-for-like replacement vehicle, not one chosen for prestige or novelty
Where a replacement vehicle bears no reasonable relationship to the damaged vehicle, the hire charges will not be recoverable
Evidence of actual use and need for the hired vehicle is essential — absence of evidence may be fatal to the claim
A commercial claimant must demonstrate that the hire was genuinely required for business operations similar to those served by the damaged vehicle
cases.significance: County Court decision only — no binding authority. Fact-specific case not relied upon in hostile litigation. The like-for-like principle is better established through binding authorities such as Bee v Jenson [2007] EWCA Civ 923 and Beechwood Birmingham Ltd v Hoyer Group UK Ltd [2010] EWCA Civ 647. This case should not be cited as primary authority.

cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Defendant's appeal allowed; plaintiff's credit hire claim dismissed on grounds that MIS (agent) had a conflict of interest and was not entitled to remuneration
cases.summary:
The plaintiff was involved in a road traffic accident and was entitled under her own insurance policy to a free courtesy car. MIS Claims Service (MIS), acting as her agent, arranged for her to hire a vehicle from its wholly-owned subsidiary Independent Car Hire (ICH) instead of availing herself of the courtesy car to which she was entitled. The District Judge had awarded the plaintiff £852.82 in hire charges. On appeal, Stephens J examined the agency relationship between the plaintiff and MIS.

The court found that MIS, as the plaintiff's agent, owed her the highest standard of loyalty and was under a duty to act in her interests alone. MIS had a direct financial conflict of interest: it stood to profit by arranging a credit hire vehicle (through its subsidiary ICH) rather than advising the plaintiff to use her free courtesy car entitlement. The agent did not disclose this conflict, and had the plaintiff been informed, she would not have agreed to expose herself to any financial risk. Applying Imageview Management Ltd v Jack [2009] EWCA Civ 63, an agent whose own interests conflict with those of the principal must act 100% for the principal; undisclosed conflicts are a breach of good faith.

The remedy for such a breach is forfeiture of all remuneration: applying Rhodes v MacAllister (1893), once a conflict of interest is shown the agent's right to any remuneration ceases. Therefore ICH/MIS were not entitled to the hire charges, the plaintiff owed nothing to ICH, and had no recoverable loss to claim against the defendant. The defendant's appeal was allowed and the claim failed.
key_principles: An agent acting for a plaintiff in arranging a replacement vehicle owes the plaintiff the highest duty of loyalty and must act 100% in the plaintiff's interests

Where an agent (such as MIS) arranges credit hire through its own subsidiary (ICH) when a free courtesy car is available to the plaintiff under her insurance, there is an undisclosed conflict of interest constituting a breach of fiduciary duty
Once a conflict of interest is established, the agent forfeits all right to remuneration from the principal — the plaintiff therefore owes nothing under the credit hire agreement
If the plaintiff owes nothing to the credit hire company, she has no loss to recover from the defendant tortfeasor and her claim fails

The rule against requiring a plaintiff to invoke insurance entitlements (res inter alios acta) does not assist where the failure is the agent's own breach of duty to the plaintiff rather than any obligation imposed by the tortfeasor
Stephens J's finding was that MIS's procedures lacked transparency and it deliberately diverted clients from courtesy car entitlements for its own financial benefit
NI High Court decision: binds NI County Courts on this point; subsequently distinguished in Smyth v Diamond [2010] NIQB 74 where the specific MIS non-disclosure was not proved on the facts
cases.significance: The leading Northern Ireland authority on the agent's duty of good faith in credit hire: MIS/ICH arrangements are potentially unenforceable where MIS acted as the plaintiff's agent and diverted her from a free courtesy car for its own financial benefit; subsequently distinguished but not overruled by Smyth v Diamond [2010] NIQB 74.
