cases.significance: landmark Supreme Court case addressing costs issues.

cases.claimant: Clark, Dennard, Sen, Lagden, Burdis (via Helphire credit hire scheme)
cases.defendant: Ardington, Plant, Steelform Engineering, O'Connor, Livsey (and their insurers)
cases.outcome: Appeals allowed in part. Helphire credit hire scheme upheld as valid and not a sham. Credit hire agreements held exempt from CCA 1974. Repair costs recoverable at 100% (not limited to 90% factored amount). Impecunious claimant (Lagden) entitled to recover full credit hire rate. Hire rate is the actual cost incurred subject to mitigation, not an averaged 'Mainz plus 10%' figure. Delivery/collection charges recoverable where reasonable. Engineers' fees not a recoverable head of damage. Interest awarded on hire charges from date of payments.
cases.summary:
The leading Court of Appeal decision on credit hire, heard as five conjoined appeals under the banner 'Clarke v Ardington': (1) Clark v Ardington (B2/2001/2112), (2) Dennard v Plant (B2/2001/2113), (3) Sen v Steelform Engineering (B2/2001/2108), (4) Lagden v O'Connor (B2/2001/2102), and (5) Burdis v Livsey (B2/2001/1254). Described by the court as 'the third round of a contest between the motor insurance market and credit hire companies' (following Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142 and Dimond v Lovell [2002] 1 AC 384). All five claimants used the Helphire credit hire scheme after RTAs. The appeals raised issues across the full range of credit hire law: whether the scheme was a sham, CCA 1974 regulation, recoverability of repair costs, impecuniosity, hire rate calculation, different car/mitigation, delivery/collection, engineers' fees, and interest.

The Burdis v Livsey appeal specifically addressed whether a claimant could recover repair costs when the claimant had not personally paid for repairs — the costs were factored at 90% through Helphire's arrangements with garages. Gray J at first instance allowed Mr Livsey's appeal, holding that because the claimant had not paid, the loss had not been suffered. The Court of Appeal reversed this, drawing a fundamental distinction between repair costs (direct/immediate loss representing diminution in value of the chattel, recoverable whether or not the claimant has paid) and hire charges (consequential/special damage). Jones v Stroud [1986] 1 WLR 1141 was confirmed as good law. The claimant was entitled to recover 100% of the proper cost of repair, not the 90% factored amount.

On impecuniosity (the Lagden appeal), the Court held that a wrongdoer must take his victim as he finds him: if an impecunious claimant cannot afford to hire a replacement car except through a credit hire scheme, the full cost of the scheme is recoverable provided the claimant acted with commercial prudence. The Lagden appeal was allowed (later affirmed by the House of Lords in Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64).

On hire rate calculation, the Court rejected the first-instance judge's 'Mainz plus 10%' approach (averaging expert surveys and adding an arbitrary uplift). The Court held that a claimant is entitled to compensation for the actual loss suffered — the cost of hiring a replacement car — subject to the duty to mitigate. The claimant is prima facie entitled to recover even if the charge is at the top of the range, and the evidential burden then passes to the defendant to show it would have been reasonable to use a cheaper alternative. The ABI scheme figures, being a compromise between parties, cannot be taken in hostile litigation as the appropriate figures of loss.
key_principles: Helphire credit hire scheme is not a sham or pretence; it operates as described and is lawful (para 45).
Credit hire agreements under the Helphire scheme are exempt agreements under CCA 1974 s.16(6C); the 12-week limit in s.15 refers to the period of bailment, not the period for payment (para 48).

Fundamental distinction between repair costs (direct/immediate loss = diminution in value of the chattel) and hire charges (consequential/special damage). Repair costs are recoverable whether or not the claimant has personally paid for them (paras 84-86, 102).

Jones v Stroud [1986] 1 WLR 1141 remains good law: a claimant can recover the cost of repair even if they have not yet paid (para 102). The agreement between claimant and accident management company is collateral to the tort and res inter alios acta (para 104).
Claimant entitled to recover 100% of proper repair costs, not limited to the 90% factored amount paid through Helphire arrangements (para 114).
Defendant liable for hire costs for the full repair period including delay, provided the delay is not the fault of the claimant (paras 115-121).

Impecuniosity: a wrongdoer must take his victim as he finds him. An impecunious claimant who cannot afford spot hire can recover the full credit hire rate provided they acted with commercial prudence (paras 122-128). Lagden appeal allowed; later affirmed by UKHL.

Hire rate: the claimant's actual loss is the cost of hiring a replacement car, not an averaged or discounted figure. The claimant is prima facie entitled to recover the amount charged even at the top of the range; the evidential burden shifts to the defendant to show a lower rate was reasonably available (paras 146-148).
The 'Mainz plus 10%' approach (averaging survey rates and adding an arbitrary uplift) is rejected as wrong in principle (para 146).
ABI scheme rates cannot be taken in hostile litigation as appropriate figures of loss — they reflect a compromise, not an assessment of loss (para 150).

Different car/mitigation: the loss suffered is the cost actually incurred. If a claimant accepts a lower-specification car, the loss is the cost of hiring that car, not the theoretical cost of the car they were entitled to (paras 132-133).
Delivery and collection charges are recoverable in appropriate circumstances, subject to reasonableness and the duty to mitigate (paras 151-153).
Engineers' fees are not a recoverable head of damage (obiter — claims abandoned in these test cases), but there is no duty on a claimant to obtain competitive tenders for repair (paras 155-156).
Interest on subrogated credit hire claims runs from the date payments were made by the credit hire company (paras 157-162).
cases.significance: One of the most important Court of Appeal authorities in credit hire law. Often cited under the name 'Burdis v Livsey' (one of the five conjoined appeals). Establishes the fundamental distinction between repair costs (direct loss) and hire charges (consequential loss). Confirms Jones v Stroud on repair cost recoverability. Sets the framework for hire rate assessment — actual cost subject to mitigation, not averaged rates. The impecuniosity holding in Lagden was subsequently affirmed by the House of Lords in Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64. The case remains the starting point for most credit hire disputes on rate, repair costs, mitigation, and the validity of credit hire schemes generally.
key_paragraphs: §1: This is the third round of a contest between the motor insurance market and credit hire companies — described the litigation history from Giles v Thompson through Dimond v Lovell.
§45: Helphire scheme is not a sham or pretence; it operates as described.
§48: Section 15 CCA 1974 — 12-week limit refers to bailment period, not payment obligations; agreements are exempt under s.16(6C).
§84: Fundamental distinction: repair costs are direct/immediate loss (diminution in value); hire charges are consequential/special damage.
§102: Jones v Stroud remains good law — repair costs recoverable whether or not claimant has paid.
§104: The agreement between Burdis and Accident Assistance was collateral to the tort — res inter alios acta.
§114: Claimant entitled to 100% of proper repair costs, not limited to 90% factored amount.
§121: Defendant bears hire costs for full repair period including delay if no fault of claimant.
§127: Wrongdoer must take victim as found — impecunious claimant recovers full cost of credit hire scheme provided commercial prudence.
§147: Fundamental principle — claimant entitled to actual cost of hiring replacement car, prima facie entitled to recover even at top of range, subject to duty to mitigate.
§146: Mainz plus 10% approach rejected — cannot restrict claimant to average cost or apply arbitrary uplift.
§150: ABI scheme figures reflect a compromise, not an assessment of loss — cannot be used in hostile litigation.
§153: Delivery/collection charges recoverable subject to reasonableness and duty to mitigate.
cases.source_notes: Summary rewritten 2026-05-31 from full text of judgment. Five conjoined appeals heard together: Clark v Ardington (B2/2001/2112), Dennard v Plant (B2/2001/2113), Sen v Steelform (B2/2001/2108), Lagden v O'Connor (B2/2001/2102), Burdis v Livsey (B2/2001/1254). The case is commonly cited as either 'Clarke v Ardington' or 'Burdis v Livsey'. The Lagden impecuniosity point was subsequently taken to the House of Lords and affirmed in Lagden v O'Connor [2003] UKHL 64 (which has a separate entry in this database).

cases.court: High Court of Justice in Northern Ireland, Queen's Bench Division
cases.outcome: Plaintiff's appeal allowed; res inter alios acta principle applied — plaintiff not required to use own insurance to mitigate credit hire claim
cases.summary: The earliest NI credit hire authority establishing the res inter alios acta principle. The plaintiff hired through DAS. The defendant argued the plaintiff had suffered no loss and/or should have used insurance to mitigate. Nicholson LJ, applying Giles v Thompson and Parry v Cleaver, held that a tortfeasor cannot require the plaintiff to invoke insurance entitlements to mitigate loss. A contingent contractual liability for hire charges is a real loss. The defendant's insurers could not escape liability by arguing the plaintiff should use alternative insurance.
key_principles: Res inter alios acta principle in NI: a tortfeasor cannot require the injured party to invoke their insurance contract to mitigate loss — applies in full in Northern Ireland
A plaintiff who incurs a contingent contractual liability for credit hire charges suffers a real loss recoverable against the tortfeasor, even if not yet payable
The fact that an accident management company profits from the arrangement does not defeat the plaintiff's claim
The defendant's insurers cannot escape liability by arguing the plaintiff should have used cheaper insurance cover
Earliest NI credit hire authority; anticipates Giles v Thompson [1994] 1 AC 142 reasoning in the NI context
cases.significance: Earliest NI res inter alios acta authority in credit hire; establishes the principle that insurance entitlements cannot be invoked by the tortfeasor to defeat the claim.

cases.case_name: Norglen Ltd v Reeds Rains Prudential Ltd
cases.outcome: Appeal allowed — assignment of claims from a company in liquidation to its directors was not a sham and the directors could be substituted as plaintiffs
cases.summary:
Two consolidated appeals concerning whether assignments of legal claims from companies in liquidation to their directors — made so that the directors could prosecute the actions with legal aid — constituted 'sham' transactions. In both cases a company had claims it wished to pursue but could not fund without legal aid, which was unavailable to companies. The liquidator assigned the claims to directors who could access legal aid, allowing the actions to proceed.

The House of Lords held that the assignments were not shams. A transaction is only a 'sham' if the parties intend it to create different rights and obligations from those appearing on its face. Here the assignments were genuine — the directors did acquire the claims as a matter of law. The purpose of saving costs through legal aid access did not make the transaction a sham.

In credit hire litigation, Norglen is cited in arguments about whether credit hire agreements are 'shams' — i.e. whether they genuinely create an obligation on the claimant to pay hire charges, or whether the documentation is a facade disguising an arrangement that imposes no real liability. The Snook v West Riding definition of 'sham' (also in the library) is applied alongside Norglen.
key_principles: A transaction is a 'sham' only if both parties intend it to create different rights and obligations from those it purports to create
A genuine assignment does not become a sham merely because it is made for fiscal or tactical reasons
The fact that a transaction achieves a collateral purpose does not mean it lacks legal effect
The Snook definition of sham requires common intention to misrepresent the true position
cases.significance: House of Lords authority on the definition of 'sham' transactions; directly relevant to defendant arguments that credit hire agreements are shams that create no genuine liability on the claimant to pay.

cases.outcome: Claimant's appeal dismissed — ex turpi causa barred recovery; the plaintiff who encouraged dangerous and illegal driving could not recover from the rider's estate
cases.summary:
The plaintiff Pitts was a pillion passenger on a motor cycle ridden by Hunt. Both had been drinking; Pitts knew Hunt had no driving licence and was uninsured, and was actively encouraging Hunt to drive in a reckless and dangerous manner when the motorcycle collided with another vehicle. Hunt was killed and Pitts seriously injured. Pitts sued Hunt's personal representative in negligence.

The Court of Appeal dismissed the claim on grounds of ex turpi causa non oritur actio. The plaintiff's claim arose directly from the illegal joint enterprise they were engaged in. The court considered different approaches to ex turpi causa but all three judges reached the same result: where a claimant is so implicated in the illegal activity that gives rise to the harm, no duty of care arises or the claim is otherwise barred by the illegality.

In credit hire litigation, Pitts v Hunt is cited in the context of illegality defences — for example, cases where a claimant was driving without insurance, without a licence, or with an unroadworthy vehicle (no MOT), and defendants argue that ex turpi causa bars or reduces the credit hire claim. The case illustrates the limits of illegality defences and is often distinguished in credit hire cases where the illegality is less fundamental.
key_principles: A claimant who is party to a joint illegal enterprise from which the accident arises may be barred from recovery by ex turpi causa
Ex turpi causa applies where the claimant's wrong is so inextricably linked to the claim that the court cannot sensibly distinguish them
The defence is not simply triggered by any illegality on the claimant's part — the illegality must be sufficiently connected to the harm
Section 148(3) Road Traffic Act 1972 prevents the defence of volenti non fit injuria in road traffic cases but does not prevent ex turpi causa
cases.significance: Leading Court of Appeal authority on ex turpi causa; frequently cited and distinguished in credit hire cases where defendants invoke illegality defences based on vehicle compliance issues.

cases.outcome: Appeal allowed — right of way extended to the full width shown on the plan including verges
cases.summary:
A property law case concerning the interpretation of a right of way granted by conveyance in 1962. The right of way was over a private road serving a small development. The conveyance did not define 'private road' but the attached plan showed a strip of land including both a carriageway and verges on each side. Over time the verges were incorporated into an adjacent property by fencing. The plaintiffs sought a declaration that their right of way included the verges as shown on the plan.

The Court of Appeal held that the plan formed part of the conveyance and was admissible in construing the extent of the right of way. The right extended to the full width shown on the plan, including the verges.

Note: This case is not a credit hire case and appears to have been included in the library as a general authority on contract or document interpretation, possibly relevant to the interpretation of credit hire agreements. Its significance to credit hire practitioners is limited unless cited in the context of construing contractual documents.
key_principles: A plan attached to a conveyance forms part of it and is admissible as an aid to construction
The words of a grant are to be construed in the context of the whole document including any attached plan
Evidence of planning permission may be relevant to construing the extent of rights granted by conveyance
cases.significance: A property law case on document interpretation — of limited direct relevance to credit hire, possibly included for general principles of contractual construction.

cases.outcome: Claimant succeeded — cost of repairs recoverable without proof of actual payment
cases.summary:
The plaintiff purchased a house with defective foundations, causing structural damage following a drought. They sued the local authority for negligence in failing to inspect the foundations when the house was built. The council argued the claim was statute-barred and also that the claimant could not recover the cost of repairs without proving they had actually paid for them.

The Court of Appeal held the claim was not statute-barred (the cause of action accrued only when the defect created a danger to health or safety). More importantly for credit hire purposes, the court held that the cost of repairs necessary to eliminate a danger is recoverable as damages even if the claimant has not yet paid for them — the obligation to pay is itself the loss.

This principle is directly applicable in credit hire litigation. Credit hire companies advance hire vehicles on credit — the claimant has not yet paid and may never have to pay if the claim succeeds. Defendants have argued that until payment is made there is no loss. Jones v Stroud is cited for the proposition that an obligation or liability to pay — even contingent — constitutes recoverable loss.
key_principles: The cost of repairs or replacement is recoverable as damages even where the claimant has not yet paid for them
An obligation or liability to pay a third party constitutes a pecuniary loss recoverable from the tortfeasor
This principle supports the recoverability of credit hire charges even though the claimant has not yet paid the hire company
The relevant question is whether the claimant has incurred a genuine legal liability, not whether they have discharged it
cases.significance: Authority for the proposition that a claimant's contingent liability to pay credit hire charges constitutes a recoverable loss even before actual payment is made.

cases.summary:
Birmingham Corp v Sowsberry [1969] 7 WLUK 158
Court: High Court
Date: 31st July 1969
Judge: Geoffrey Lane J
Summary
The plaintiff, a corporation that was non-profit-making operated buses but maintained a spare
fleet in the event of emergencies. The defendant’s van collided with one of the buses, and the
plaintiffs claimed damages for (inter alia) loss of use of the damaged bus whilst it was being
repaired. During this period, the plaintiff utilised one of the spare buses.
cases.significance: Case on loss of use and related credit hire matters.

cases.outcome: Claimant succeeded — there was an implied contract between the car owner and the repairer; repairer obliged to complete repairs within a reasonable time
cases.summary:
A car owner whose vehicle was damaged in an accident took it to a garage for repair. The garage submitted an estimate which was accepted by the car owner's insurers, who agreed to pay. The repairs took an unreasonably long time to complete. The question was whether a contract existed between the car owner and the garage, or only between the insurer and the garage — and if a contract existed with the owner, whether the garage was in breach of an implied term to complete repairs within a reasonable time.

The Court of Appeal held that an implied contract did arise between the garage and the car owner, notwithstanding that payment was to be made by the insurer. The garage undertook the repairs for the benefit of the owner. Within that contract, there was an implied term that the repairs would be completed within a reasonable time.

This case is important in credit hire litigation because it establishes the principle that a repair period may be unreasonably long, entitling the car owner to argue that the hire period should correspondingly be limited. It supports defendants' arguments that excessive repair times (and hence excessive hire periods) are not necessarily the fault of the tortfeasor.
key_principles: An implied contract arises between the car owner and a repairer even where the insurer is paying
A term is implied that repairs will be completed within a reasonable time
An unreasonably prolonged repair period may limit the credit hire charges recoverable from the tortfeasor
The relevant 'reasonable time' for repairs is a question of fact in each case
cases.significance: Establishes that a repairer owes an implied duty to complete repairs within a reasonable time, limiting the credit hire period attributable to the tortfeasor where repairs are unnecessarily delayed.

cases.filename: Snook v London & West Riding Ivestments.pdf
cases.case_name: Snook v London and West Riding Investments Ltd
cases.defendant: London and West Riding Investments Ltd
cases.outcome: Defendant succeeded — hire-purchase transaction was not a sham; finance company's title was good
cases.summary:
Snook obtained a car through a complex refinancing arrangement. He had originally bought the car partly on hire-purchase from one finance company. Needing funds, he arranged for the car to be sold to agents and then re-sold to a new finance company, who let it back to him on hire-purchase. When he defaulted, the question arose whether the original hire-purchase company's interest had been properly extinguished and whether the new finance company had good title.

Snook argued the series of transactions was a 'sham' designed to circumvent hire-purchase control regulations and that the new hire-purchase company had constructive notice of the earlier transaction. The Court of Appeal held the transactions were not a sham. Diplock LJ gave the classic definition: a sham is an act or document intended by all parties to create different legal rights and obligations from those which it gives the appearance of creating.

The Snook definition of 'sham' is directly and frequently cited in credit hire litigation. Defendants often argue that credit hire agreements are shams — that the parties never intended the claimant to actually pay the hire charges, so no genuine liability arose and the charges are not recoverable. Courts apply the Snook test and generally find that a properly documented credit hire agreement is not a sham.
key_principles: A 'sham' transaction requires all parties to have a common intention that the document or act should not create the legal rights and obligations it purports to create
A transaction is not a sham merely because it achieves a purpose other than its apparent purpose
Constructive notice of an earlier transaction does not make a later transaction a sham

The Snook definition: 'acts done or documents executed by the parties to the sham which are intended by them to give to third parties or to the court the appearance of creating between the parties legal rights and obligations different from the actual legal rights and obligations (if any) which the parties intend to create'
cases.significance: The definitive authority on the meaning of 'sham' in English law; central to credit hire cases where defendants argue that hire agreements are sham documents that create no genuine obligation on the claimant to pay.

cases.outcome: Claimant succeeded in damages for breach of solicitor's duty — insurer's solicitors had admitted negligence on behalf of the insured without authority
cases.summary:
A motor accident insured person (Crocker) handed the defence of a third party's claim to his insurer, who under the policy terms had the right to conduct any proceedings brought against the insured. Without informing Crocker, the insurer's solicitors (Groom) admitted negligence on Crocker's behalf as part of a 'knock for knock' arrangement with the co-defendant's insurer. Crocker did not know about this until after judgment had been given against him.

Crocker brought an action against the solicitors for breach of their duty to him as his solicitors. The Court of Appeal held that the solicitors, despite being instructed and paid by the insurer, stood in a solicitor-client relationship with the insured and owed duties to him. They had no authority to admit his negligence and were in breach of duty in doing so.

This case is cited in credit hire litigation in the context of solicitor-client obligations where solicitors are instructed by a credit hire company or insurer but also represent the individual claimant, and in relation to the extent of any insurer's rights to conduct the litigation.
key_principles: Solicitors instructed and paid by an insurer nonetheless stand in a solicitor-client relationship with the insured
An insurer cannot, under standard motor policy terms, instruct solicitors to admit the insured's negligence without authority
The solicitor-client duty runs to the insured, not solely to the insurer who pays
An admission of negligence made without the client's authority is a breach of the solicitor's duty
cases.significance: Foundational authority on the duties of solicitors instructed by insurers to defend the insured; relevant to the conduct of credit hire litigation where solicitors act for both the hire company and the claimant.

cases.outcome: Claimant's damages limited — claimant failed to mitigate by refusing defendant's reasonable offer to supply at contract price for cash
cases.summary:
Payzu Ltd contracted to buy silk from Saunders on credit terms. After late payment of the first instalment, Saunders wrongfully refused to make further deliveries under the contract but offered to supply the goods at the contract price on a cash-on-delivery basis. Payzu refused this offer and bought the goods at a higher market price. The market price had risen significantly.

The Court of Appeal held that although Saunders was in breach of contract, Payzu had failed to mitigate their loss by refusing the reasonable offer to supply at the contract price. Damages were therefore limited to the small loss Payzu would have suffered if they had accepted that offer — not the full difference between the market price and the contract price.

This case is a foundational authority on the duty to mitigate and is frequently cited in credit hire cases where defendants argue that a claimant failed to mitigate by refusing a cheaper vehicle offered by the insurer, refusing a spot hire vehicle, or insisting on credit hire when a direct hire vehicle was reasonably available.
key_principles: A claimant has a duty to take reasonable steps to mitigate their loss
What is reasonable mitigation is a question of fact, not law
A claimant who unreasonably refuses a defendant's reasonable offer to reduce their loss will have their damages limited accordingly
The duty to mitigate applies even where the defendant is clearly in the wrong
cases.significance: Foundational Court of Appeal authority on the duty to mitigate; cited by defendants in credit hire cases to argue that claimants should have accepted cheaper hire arrangements offered by the insurer.

cases.case_name: British Westinghouse Electric and Manufacturing Co Ltd v Underground Electric Railways Co of London Ltd
cases.claimant: Underground Electric Railways Co of London Ltd
cases.defendant: British Westinghouse Electric and Manufacturing Co Ltd
cases.outcome: Defendant succeeded in reducing damages — benefit of mitigation steps taken by claimant was to be brought into account
cases.summary:
The foundational House of Lords authority on the principle that benefits accruing to a claimant as a result of steps taken to mitigate loss must be brought into account when assessing damages. The defendant supplied turbines under contract which were deficient in power and economy. The claimant railway company used the turbines for a period but ultimately replaced them with superior turbines from a different manufacturer. The claimant sought to recover the full cost of the replacement turbines as damages for breach of contract.

The House of Lords held that when assessing damages, the court must take into account any pecuniary advantage accruing to the claimant from the substituted transaction, even if the original turbines had met the contractual specification. Because the new turbines were superior and produced greater economic benefit, that advantage was to be credited against the damages claimed.

In credit hire litigation this case is frequently cited by defendants to argue that where a claimant has obtained a better or more advantageous situation than they would have had without the accident — for example, by obtaining a newer or better vehicle on hire — the value of that collateral benefit should reduce their damages claim.
key_principles: A claimant must take reasonable steps to mitigate their loss following a breach of contract or tort
Where steps taken to mitigate produce a pecuniary benefit, that benefit must be brought into account in assessing damages
The benefit must be the direct consequence of the breach and of the act of mitigation
This principle applies both in contract and in tort
cases.significance: Foundational authority on the duty to mitigate and the treatment of collateral benefits; cited in credit hire cases where defendants argue the claimant gained an advantage from the hire arrangement.

cases.summary:
Brewis v Robles
Misrepresentation of a credit hire contract and rescission
Andrew Hogan reported on LinkedIn last week that HHJ Bloom allowed a claimant's appeal in a
credit hire claim in Luton County Court, holding that a deputy district judge was wrong to
conclude that no damages for credit hire were recoverable on the basis of misrepresentations
from a hire company about the claimant's liability to pay hire charges: they could not render a
credit hire agreement "unenforceable". The first instance hearing in the case of Brewis v Robles was before DDJ Duncan in the
Hertford County Court on 3rd February 2020. In essence he found that the terms of the credit
hire agreement had been misrepresented to the Claimant and that she had a defence of
misrepresentation which removed her liability to pay the credit hire charges.
