cases.case_name: CILEX and others v Mazur and others
judges: Sir Colin Birss (Chancellor of the High Court)
Sir Geoffrey Vos (Master of the Rolls)
cases.defendant: Julia Mazur, Jerome Stuart, Charles Russell Speechlys LLP
cases.outcome: Appeal allowed. The High Court judge was wrong to hold that unauthorised persons were 'carrying on the conduct of litigation' when performing litigation tasks under the supervision of an authorised individual. An unauthorised person may lawfully perform tasks within the scope of the conduct of litigation for and on behalf of an authorised individual, provided proper supervision and delegation arrangements are in place.
cases.summary: Landmark Court of Appeal decision on the meaning of 'carry on the conduct of litigation' under the Legal Services Act 2007. The court overturned the High Court decision of Sheldon J ([2025] EWHC 2341 (KB)) which had held that unauthorised persons (paralegals, legal executives, clerks) could only support or assist a solicitor in conducting litigation but could not themselves conduct litigation under supervision. The Court of Appeal held that 'conduct of litigation' refers to the tasks to be undertaken, while 'carry on' refers to the direction, control and responsibility for those tasks. Therefore, an unauthorised person acting under the supervision of an authorised individual (who retains responsibility) is not themselves 'carrying on' the conduct of litigation and commits no offence under section 14 of the 2007 Act. The decision restored the long-established practice of delegation by solicitors to unqualified staff, which the High Court decision had threatened to criminalise.
key_principles: The words 'conduct of litigation' refer to the tasks to be undertaken, whilst the words 'carry on' refer to direction and control of, and responsibility for, those tasks (§162, §170, §187(iii)).

An unauthorised person may lawfully perform any tasks within the scope of the conduct of litigation, for and on behalf of an authorised individual such as a solicitor or appropriately authorised CILEX member, provided the authorised individual retains responsibility for the tasks delegated (§187(v)).
The authorised individual retains both formal responsibility for the task and the professional responsibilities under section 1(3) of the 2007 Act (§187(v), §26).

The delegation of tasks requires proper direction, management supervision and control, the details of which are a matter for the regulators. The degree of appropriate control and supervision depends on the circumstances (§187(vi)).

It is wrong to distinguish between (a) supporting or assisting an authorised solicitor in conducting litigation, and (b) conducting litigation under the supervision of an authorised solicitor — both activities are lawful where proper supervision arrangements are in place (§187(vii), §27).
The offence in section 14 of the 2007 Act is to be interpreted narrowly, consistent with the approach to its predecessor in section 70 of the 1990 Act (§187(ii)).

The 2007 Act did not make a significant change from the 1990 Act regarding the offence of carrying on the conduct of litigation. Parliament must be taken to have understood the widespread practice of delegation by solicitors to unqualified individuals (§20, §187(i)).

Purely mechanical or administrative tasks (such as a postal worker delivering a claim form) are not within the conduct of litigation at all. Whether an unauthorised person is carrying on the conduct of litigation is a question of fact and degree (§23, §187(iv)).

The following activities are unlikely to fall within the statutory definition of 'conduct of litigation': pre-litigation work, giving legal advice in connection with proceedings, conducting correspondence, gathering evidence, instructing experts and counsel, signing statements of truth, signing documents the CPR permits a legal representative to sign (§192).

The question in any given circumstances is whether the unauthorised person, in carrying out delegated tasks, is in truth acting on behalf of the authorised individual — if so, it is the authorised individual who is conducting the litigation (Lady Justice Andrews at §198).
cases.significance: Landmark Court of Appeal authority reversing the High Court decision that had caused significant concern across the legal profession by potentially criminalising the widespread practice of solicitors delegating litigation tasks to supervised but unauthorised staff such as paralegals, trainee solicitors, and legal executives. The decision clarifies that proper delegation under supervision is lawful and confirms the distinction between performing tasks (which can be delegated) and carrying on the conduct of litigation (which requires direction, control and responsibility by the authorised individual). Interveners included the Legal Services Board, the Law Centres Network, and the Association of Personal Injury Lawyers (APIL), reflecting the case's significance across the entire legal sector.
key_paragraphs: §25: An unauthorised person can lawfully perform any tasks, which are within the scope of the conduct of litigation, for and on behalf of an authorised individual such as a solicitor or appropriately authorised CILEX member. The authorised individual retains responsibility for the tasks delegated to the unauthorised person.

§162: The ordinary meaning of the words: 'conduct of litigation' refers to the tasks to be undertaken, whilst the words 'carry on' refer to the direction and control of, and the responsibility for, those tasks.

§187(v): An unauthorised person may lawfully perform any tasks which are within the scope of the conduct of litigation, for and on behalf of an authorised individual such as a solicitor or appropriately authorised CILEX member, provided the authorised individual retains responsibility for the tasks delegated to the unauthorised person.

§187(vi): The delegation of tasks by the authorised individual to the unauthorised person requires proper direction, management supervision and control, the details of which are a matter for the regulators. The authorised individual must put in place appropriate arrangements.

§187(vii): The judge was wrong to distinguish between (a) supporting or assisting an authorised solicitor in conducting litigation, and (b) conducting litigation under the supervision of an authorised solicitor. Both activities are lawful.

§192: Activities unlikely to fall within 'conduct of litigation': pre-litigation work; giving legal advice in connection with proceedings; conducting correspondence; gathering evidence; instructing experts and counsel; signing statements of truth; signing documents CPR permits legal representatives to sign.

§198 (Andrews LJ): The question in any given set of circumstances will be whether the unauthorised person, in carrying out whatever tasks which fall within the scope of 'conduct of litigation' have been delegated to him or her, is in truth acting on behalf of the authorised individual. If they are, it is the authorised individual who is conducting the litigation.

cases.case_name: Chan v (1) Peters (2) Advantage Insurance Company Ltd
cases.outcome: Claim dismissed — defendant driver was not negligent in the circumstances
cases.summary: A 17-year-old claimant was struck by a car while crossing a road near his school. Due to significant injuries and inability to recall the incident, the claimant could not give evidence. CCTV, eyewitnesses and collision reconstruction experts were called. Cavanagh J comprehensively set out 14 core principles governing liability and contributory negligence in RTA cases, drawing together the key authorities. The judge found the defendant was not negligent — the standard is that of a reasonably careful driver, not an ideal driver judged with 20/20 hindsight. The danger of the pedestrian entering the road was no more than a 'mere possibility' which would not have occurred to a reasonable driver in time to avoid the collision.
key_principles: The Defendant will be liable in negligence if she failed to attain the standard of a reasonable careful driver AND if the accident was caused as a result.
The burden of proof rests with the Claimant, who must establish on the balance of probabilities that the Defendant was negligent.

The standard of care is that of the reasonably careful driver, armed with common sense and experience of how pedestrians — and particularly children — are likely to behave (Moore v Pointer [1975] RTR).
If a real risk of a danger emerging would have been reasonably apparent to such a driver, then reasonable precautions must be taken.
If the danger was no more than a mere possibility, which would not have occurred to such a driver, there is no obligation to take extraordinary precautions (Foskett v Mistry [1984] 1 RTR 1).
The Defendant is not to be judged by the standards of an ideal driver, nor with the benefit of '20/20 hindsight' (Stewart v Glaze [2009] EWHC 704 (QB)).
Drivers must always bear in mind that a motorcar is potentially a dangerous weapon (Lunt v Khelifa [2002] EWCA Civ 801).
Drivers are taken to know the principles of the Highway Code.

Trial judges should be cautious about making findings of unwarranted precision not justified by the evidence — turning 'guesstimates' into secure findings of fact can lead to unjust results (Lambert v Clayton [2009] EWCA Civ 237 at §39).

Trial judges must exercise caution with accident reconstruction expert evidence — experts should not give opinions beyond their expertise or act as advocates, and reaction times, stopping distances and the like should not be elevated into a 'fixed framework or formula' against which the defendant's actions are rigidly judged (Stewart v Glaze).

Whether the Defendant's driving fell below the requisite standard must be assessed by reference to the actual circumstances of the actual collision, not in a vacuum (Sam v Atkins [2005] EWCA Civ 1452).
As for contributory fault, the apportionment of responsibility is inevitably a 'somewhat rough and ready exercise' (Jackson v Murray [2015] UKSC 5 at §28, per Lord Reed).
The age of a Claimant is not in itself a relevant factor for the determination of the extent of contributory negligence.
cases.significance: An important High Court judgment that comprehensively restates the 14 core principles governing liability and contributory negligence in RTA cases, drawing together and distilling the key authorities from Moore v Pointer through to Jackson v Murray. Particularly valuable as a single reference point for the complete framework of negligence assessment in road traffic accident litigation.

cases.outcome: Appeal allowed — contributory negligence reduced from 90% to 50%
cases.summary: A 13-year-old girl was struck by a car after stepping out from behind a minibus on an unlit rural road. The Supreme Court considered the correct approach to apportionment of contributory negligence. Lord Reed, giving the majority judgment, held that the apportionment of responsibility is inevitably a somewhat rough and ready exercise and that an appellate court should only interfere where the trial judge was clearly wrong. The 90% reduction for contributory negligence was held to be clearly wrong given that the driver was travelling too fast for the conditions and bore significant responsibility as the person in charge of the more dangerous vehicle.
key_principles: The apportionment of responsibility under the Law Reform (Contributory Negligence) Act 1945 is inevitably a 'somewhat rough and ready exercise' (Lord Reed at §28).
An appellate court should only interfere with a trial judge's apportionment where the judge was clearly wrong.
Responsibility involves both the causative potency of the party's conduct and their relative blameworthiness.
A driver must bear in mind that a car is potentially a dangerous weapon and has a greater responsibility when the risk of injury falls on a vulnerable road user such as a child.
key_paragraphs: §28: The apportionment of responsibility is inevitably a somewhat rough and ready exercise.
§42: The driver was driving too fast for the conditions and bore significant responsibility as the operator of the more dangerous vehicle.
cases.significance: Leading Supreme Court authority on the approach to apportionment of contributory negligence, establishing that the exercise is 'rough and ready' and that appellate courts should be slow to interfere. Frequently cited in RTA cases involving vulnerable road users.

cases.outcome: Appeal allowed — defendant driver held liable
cases.summary: A pedestrian was struck by a car. The Court of Appeal held that drivers must always bear in mind that a motorcar is potentially a dangerous weapon and must therefore exercise appropriate care, particularly when there are pedestrians in the vicinity. This principle is regularly cited in RTA negligence cases as establishing the heightened duty that arises from the inherent danger of motor vehicles.
key_principles: Drivers must always bear in mind that a motorcar is potentially a dangerous weapon.
The inherent danger of motor vehicles imposes a heightened standard of vigilance on drivers, particularly around pedestrians.
A driver who fails to take account of the potential for pedestrians to act unpredictably may be found negligent.
cases.significance: Court of Appeal authority regularly cited for the principle that a motor vehicle is 'potentially a dangerous weapon' — a foundational principle in RTA negligence assessment that raises the standard of care expected of drivers.

cases.outcome: Guidance on standard of care and expert evidence in RTA cases
cases.summary: Coulson J held that a defendant driver is not to be judged by the standard of an ideal driver with the benefit of '20/20 hindsight'. The judgment also cautioned trial judges about the dangers of accident reconstruction expert evidence: (i) such experts should not give opinions beyond their expertise or act as advocates seeking to usurp the role of the judge; and (ii) their evidence about reaction times, stopping distances and the like should not be elevated into a 'fixed framework or formula' against which the defendant's actions are then rigidly judged with mathematical precision.
key_principles: The Defendant is not to be judged by the standards of an ideal driver, nor with the benefit of '20/20 hindsight'.
Accident reconstruction experts must not give opinions beyond their expertise or act as advocates seeking to usurp the role of the judge.

Evidence about reaction times, stopping distances and the like should not be elevated into a 'fixed framework or formula' against which the defendant's actions are then rigidly judged with a mathematical precision.
cases.significance: Widely cited authority on two key points in RTA litigation: (1) the correct standard against which to judge a driver's conduct (the reasonable driver, not the ideal driver with hindsight); and (2) the limits of accident reconstruction expert evidence, particularly the danger of treating stopping distance calculations as rigid benchmarks.

cases.outcome: Appeal allowed — trial judge's findings of fact overturned
cases.summary: Smith LJ cautioned trial judges against making findings of fact with unwarranted precision when that precision was not justified by the evidence. To do so risked turning 'guesstimates' into secure findings of fact, which could easily lead to an unjust result either way. At §39, Smith LJ stated that where there are inherent uncertainties about the facts, it is dangerous to make precise findings, and the party bearing the burden of proof may be in difficulty — but that is one of the purposes behind a burden of proof: if the case cannot be demonstrated on the balance of probabilities, it will fail.
key_principles: Trial judges should not make findings of fact with unwarranted precision when the evidence does not justify such precision.
Turning 'guesstimates' into secure findings of fact can easily lead to an unjust result either way.
Where there are inherent uncertainties about the facts, it is dangerous to make precise findings.
If the case cannot be demonstrated on the balance of probabilities, it will fail — that is one of the purposes behind a burden of proof.
key_paragraphs: §39: If there are inherent uncertainties about the facts, as there were here, it is dangerous to make precise findings. This may well mean that the party who bears the burden of proof is in difficulties. But that is one of the purposes behind a burden of proof; that if the case cannot be demonstrated on the balance of probabilities, it will fail.
cases.significance: Court of Appeal authority on the limits of factual findings in RTA cases, cautioning against false precision. Regularly cited alongside Stewart v Glaze when challenging overly precise accident reconstruction analyses.

cases.outcome: Guidance on assessment of negligence by reference to actual circumstances
cases.summary: The Court of Appeal held that the assessment of whether a defendant's driving fell below the requisite standard cannot be conducted in a vacuum — it must be done by reference to the actual circumstances of the actual collision against which the standard is to be judged. This prevents an abstract or theoretical assessment divorced from the real-world conditions at the time of the accident.
key_principles: Whether the Defendant's driving fell below the requisite standard must be assessed by reference to the actual circumstances of the actual collision.
The assessment cannot be conducted in a vacuum or in the abstract — it must reflect the real conditions the driver faced.
cases.significance: Court of Appeal authority establishing that negligence must be assessed against the actual circumstances of the collision, not in the abstract. Cited in Chan v Peters as one of the core principles of RTA liability.

cases.outcome: Established the 'mere possibility' principle in RTA negligence
cases.summary: The Court of Appeal held that if the danger was no more than a mere possibility, which would not have occurred to a reasonably careful driver, then there is no obligation to take extraordinary precautions. This is distinguished from a 'real risk' of danger emerging, which would have been reasonably apparent, in which case reasonable precautions must be taken.
key_principles: If a real risk of danger emerging would have been reasonably apparent to a careful driver, then reasonable precautions must be taken.
If the danger was no more than a mere possibility, which would not have occurred to such a driver, there is no obligation to take extraordinary precautions.
The distinction between a 'real risk' and a 'mere possibility' is central to the assessment of negligence in RTA cases.
cases.significance: Court of Appeal authority establishing the important distinction between a 'real risk' (requiring precautions) and a 'mere possibility' (not requiring extraordinary precautions) in RTA negligence — a principle regularly applied in pedestrian and junction accident cases.

cases.outcome: Standard of care defined for reasonably careful driver
cases.summary: Established that the standard of care expected of a driver is that of the reasonably careful driver, armed with common sense and experience of the way pedestrians — and particularly children — are likely to behave. This formulation has been consistently adopted in subsequent RTA negligence cases as the benchmark against which a driver's conduct is measured.
key_principles: The standard of care is that of the reasonably careful driver, armed with common sense and experience of the way pedestrians and particularly children are likely to behave.
A driver should anticipate the possibility of unpredictable behaviour by pedestrians, especially children near schools or residential areas.
cases.significance: Authority establishing the standard formulation of the duty of care for drivers — the 'reasonably careful driver armed with common sense and experience' — consistently cited in RTA cases for five decades.

cases.filename: Diaw v ERS Syndicate Management Ltd.pdf
cases.case_name: Diaw v ERS Syndicate Management Ltd
cases.claimant: Ibrahima Diaw (and family members as related claimants)
cases.outcome: Claimants found fundamentally dishonest in respect of personal injury claims which were dismissed under s.57. However, Mr Diaw's property damage claim (vehicle repairs) was NOT dismissed - he was awarded damages of £975 for the damage to his car. The court held s.57 does not extend to non-personal injury elements of the claim.
cases.summary: Ibrahima Diaw and his family were involved in a road traffic accident in Manchester when their Vauxhall Corsa was hit by a van insured by ERS. Three personal injury claims were brought (Mr Diaw, his wife, and their son Ibrahima). After a contested trial, Recorder Mark Jones found that the accident was genuine and the defendant was liable, but that all three claimants had been fundamentally dishonest about their personal injuries. The central legal issue was whether s.57 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015, which requires dismissal of the 'primary claim' (the claim for damages in respect of personal injury) upon a finding of fundamental dishonesty, also required dismissal of Mr Diaw's separate, good claim for property damage to his vehicle (£975 for repairs). The Recorder conducted a detailed textual analysis of s.57 (paras 60-73) and concluded that the word 'claim' is used consistently throughout s.57 to mean 'claim for damages in respect of personal injury' only. The duty to dismiss under s.57(2) applies only to the personal injury claim and its elements (including good elements under s.57(3)), not to other heads of claim in the same proceedings such as property damage or credit hire. The Recorder explicitly disagreed with HHJ Saunders' decision in Basir v Larizadeh (paras 78-89), finding that the learned judge was wrong to interpret s.57 as extending to the whole claim including non-PI elements. He rejected the 'perverse results' argument, the reliance on Hansard (finding the Lord Faulks passage equivocal and doubting Pepper v Hart conditions were met), the analogy with QOCS provisions (which are drafted in materially different terms), and the reliance on four High Court cases (Molodi, Sinfield, Stanton v Hunter, Pinkus v Direct Line) which he found were either not binding, not on point, or distinguishable. This is the leading judgment providing a detailed, reasoned analysis of why s.57 does NOT extend to credit hire, vehicle repair, or other non-personal injury claims, directly contradicting the approach taken by some defendant insurers and their solicitors.
key_principles: Section 57 of the Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 applies only to claims for damages in respect of personal injury. The word 'claim' throughout s.57 consistently refers to 'claim for damages in respect of personal injury' and nothing else.

The duty to dismiss the 'primary claim' under s.57(2) extends to dismissal of any element of that personal injury claim (including good elements) under s.57(3), but does NOT extend to separate heads of claim such as property damage, credit hire, or vehicle repairs.

A claimant cannot escape s.57 by bolting on a property damage claim to a pure PI claim. The word 'proceedings' in s.57(1) ensures it applies to mixed claims (PI plus property damage). But once engaged, the operative provisions only bite on the PI claim itself.

HHJ Saunders was wrong in Basir v Larizadeh to hold that s.57 extends to the whole claim including non-personal injury elements. His reasoning on textual analysis, perverse results, legislative intent, and the cases cited was rejected.

The Hansard passage quoted by HHJ Saunders (Lord Faulks QC, 23 July 2014) is equivocal and does not clearly support extending s.57 beyond personal injury claims. The Pepper v Hart conditions for admitting Hansard were likely not met.
The QOCS provisions (CPR 44.13-44.16) are drafted in materially different terms to s.57 and caution should be exercised before transferring jurisprudence from one to the other.

There is nothing perverse about Parliament limiting the penalising effect of s.57 to the elements of a claim in respect of personal injury. The rights to compensation for property damage are ancient and fundamental, and the legislation is a proportionate response to the mischief of exaggerated personal injury claims.

The four High Court cases relied upon by HHJ Saunders (Molodi, Sinfield, Stanton v Hunter, Pinkus v Direct Line) were all distinguishable - none directly addressed or ruled on whether s.57 extends to non-PI claims, and none are binding authority for that proposition.
legal_topics: s57 Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015
cases.significance: The most detailed and reasoned judicial analysis of whether s.57 extends to non-personal injury elements of a claim. Directly contradicts Basir v Larizadeh and provides a comprehensive textual, contextual, and policy-based framework for arguing that credit hire and property damage claims survive a finding of fundamental dishonesty. Essential authority for any CHO solicitor facing a s.57 application that seeks to dismiss credit hire or vehicle repair claims.
cases.amount_at_stake: £975 (property damage awarded despite FD finding; PI claims dismissed)
key_paragraphs: Paras 60-73 (textual analysis of s.57), 78-81 (criticism of Basir v Larizadeh), 82-86 (detailed rebuttal of Basir reasoning), 87-89 (legislative intent/Hansard analysis), 90-95 (QOCS/procedural context), 96-103 (analysis of cited High Court cases), 106 (conclusion)

cases.citation: [2019] (Central London County Court, 20 February 2019, Case No. D67YX572)
cases.court: County Court (Central London) - Appeal from District Judge
cases.outcome: Appeal allowed. HHJ Saunders overturned the District Judge's order, holding that s.57 applied to the whole claim including the credit hire and vehicle repair elements, not just the personal injury claim. The entire claim was dismissed.
cases.summary: An appeal from the decision of District Judge Hugman dated 10 May 2018. The claimant (Basir) claimed damages for personal injuries, repairs, credit hire and miscellaneous expenses arising from a road traffic accident in Sloane Square, London on 17 October 2016. The defendant admitted liability but denied causation and quantum. The District Judge found the claims for repairs and credit hire were made out (awarding just over £3,000) but found the claimant fundamentally dishonest about his personal injuries. However, DJ Hugman refused to apply s.57 to dismiss the non-PI elements (credit hire and repairs), interpreting s.57 as only engaged where the claimant was entitled to damages in respect of the PI claim. On appeal, HHJ Saunders allowed the appeal, holding that s.57 applied to the whole claim including non-PI elements. He relied on: (1) the 'perverse results' argument (a claimant who completely fabricated injuries would keep their credit hire claim while one who merely exaggerated would lose everything); (2) the wording of s.57(3) referring to 'any element of the primary claim'; (3) Hansard (Lord Faulks's statement about dismissal of the 'entire claim'); (4) four High Court cases (Molodi, Sinfield, Stanton v Hunter, Pinkus v Direct Line). IMPORTANT NOTE: This decision was subsequently subjected to detailed criticism and disagreed with by Recorder Mark Jones in Diaw v ERS Syndicate Management Ltd [2020], who conducted a thorough textual analysis and found HHJ Saunders was wrong on every point. The claimant/respondent in Basir did not appear and was not represented at the appeal, meaning HHJ Saunders did not have the benefit of argument from both sides.
key_principles: HHJ Saunders held that the terms 'primary claim' and 'claim' in s.57 both refer to proceedings on a claim for damages in respect of personal injury which includes the whole of the claim (para 22) - NOTE: this interpretation was rejected in Diaw v ERS [2020].

The 'perverse results' argument: if s.57 only applied to the PI element, a claimant who completely fabricated injuries would retain their credit hire claim, while one who merely exaggerated would lose everything - NOTE: this argument was rejected as unpersuasive in Diaw v ERS.

Reliance on Hansard (Lord Faulks QC, committee stage) for the proposition that Parliament intended the 'entire claim' to be dismissed - NOTE: Recorder Mark Jones in Diaw found this passage equivocal and doubted Pepper v Hart conditions were met.
The claimant was unrepresented at the appeal hearing, meaning HHJ Saunders did not have the benefit of argument from both sides - a point noted by Recorder Mark Jones as significant.

This decision has been used as a template by defendant insurers (notably through DAC Beachcroft responses) to argue that credit hire claims should be dismissed upon a finding of fundamental dishonesty, despite it being a county court decision with no binding authority and having been subject to detailed judicial criticism.
legal_topics: s57 Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015
cases.significance: A county court appeal decision that held s.57 extends to dismiss the entire claim including credit hire and property damage. Frequently cited by defendant insurers (especially via DAC Beachcroft template responses) as authority for dismissing credit hire claims upon a finding of FD. However, this decision was made without the benefit of argument from the claimant's side and was subsequently subjected to detailed criticism and disagreed with in Diaw v ERS [2020] by Recorder Mark Jones, who found HHJ Saunders was wrong on textual analysis, legislative intent, the cases cited, and the perverse results argument. NOT binding authority and should be read alongside Diaw v ERS.
cases.amount_at_stake: £3,009.35 (credit hire and repairs at first instance) plus £7,000 defendant's costs; appeal reversed the award entirely
key_paragraphs: Paras 16-22 (s.57 analysis and wider interpretation), 23-26 (QOCS/Wagenaar analogy), 27-32 (reliance on High Court cases), 33 (Hansard/Lord Faulks), 35 (conclusion - appeal allowed)

cases.citation: (County Court at Liverpool, 23 February 2026, Case No. 080DC180, Appeal No. 88 of 2024)
cases.court: County Court (Liverpool) - Appeal from District Judge
judges: His Honour Judge Graham Wood KC (Designated Civil Judge)
cases.outcome: Appeal allowed. HHJ Wood KC set aside the decision of District Judge Metcalfe which had dismissed the credit hire claim on the basis that the Claimant had failed to establish need. The Court held that on the unchallenged factual evidence the Claimant had discharged the evidential burden as to need, and that the learned District Judge had erred (a) by effectively placing a legal burden of proof on the Claimant and (b) by conflating the issue of need with the reasonableness of the cost of the hire, which is a matter going to rate (and duration) and not to need. The appellate court indicated that remission for a short further hearing on outstanding issues of period and rate was likely, subject to written submissions from counsel.
cases.summary:
Appeal from District Judge Metcalfe (Liverpool County Court, fast track, 20 September 2024) in a typical credit hire claim brought by a food delivery rider whose Honda motorcycle had been struck by a forklift truck in May 2020. Liability was admitted. The claim included PAV (£1,225.80), storage (£1,107.60) and credit hire (£13,195.28 over 59 days). The Claimant conceded at trial that he could not rely upon impecuniosity, so the hire claim was ultimately advanced on a BHR basis of c.£3,884. The District Judge found the Claimant fundamentally dishonest in relation to the personal injury claim and dismissed the credit hire claim at the first hurdle on the basis that the Claimant had failed to establish need on the balance of probabilities, relying in particular on the availability of an e-bike and the “very significant cost” of the credit hire motorcycle.

HHJ Graham Wood KC, Designated Civil Judge for Cheshire and Merseyside, allowed the appeal. Drawing on Armstead v Royal and Sun Alliance Insurance Co Ltd [2025] AC 407, Giles v Thompson [1993] 3 All ER 32, Lagden v O’Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067, Pattni/Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17 and Wilding v British Telecommunications PLC [2002] ICR 1079, the Court reaffirmed that the legal burden of proving a failure to mitigate lies on the defendant, while the claimant bears only an evidential burden as to need. The District Judge's language of ‘not established on the balance of probabilities’ risked elevating that evidential burden to a legal one. The unchallenged evidence (pre-accident use of the motorcycle for social, domestic and pleasure purposes for up to 100 miles a week, running errands and limited range compared with an e-bike which required four-hour recharges) was plainly sufficient to discharge the evidential burden. The District Judge had further fallen into error by treating the cost of the credit hire as relevant to need; cost goes to rate (and perhaps duration) and must be considered under those separate headings. Need must be addressed in isolation, applying a ‘compartmentalised’ approach.

Argued by Mr Benjamin Williams KC (Bond Turner Solicitors) for the Appellant and Mr Paul Higgins (Horwich Farrelly Solicitors) for the Respondent.
key_principles: Need in credit hire: the claimant bears an evidential burden only. The legal burden of proving a failure to mitigate is on the defendant (applying Armstead v RSA [2025] AC 407 at [59]-[60]).

Practitioners frequently conflate the practical test (has the claimant acted reasonably?) with the legal test (has the defendant shown that the claimant acted unreasonably?). Strictly, it is the latter.

The threshold to discharge the evidential burden on need is low. Occasional use (errands, social and domestic use, the 'Sunday afternoon drive') can suffice; need does not require essentialness (Giles v Thompson).

A compartmentalised approach must be taken: need, period, type of vehicle and rate are distinct questions. The cost of the credit hire is relevant to rate (and perhaps duration) but is NOT relevant to need.

A finding of fundamental dishonesty in relation to a personal injury claim does not automatically taint the claimant’s evidence on other heads of loss. The court may still accept unchallenged factual evidence on need (subject to Summers v Fairclough Homes [2012] UKSC 26).

Where a claimant owns two different vehicles that serve different purposes (e.g. a motorcycle and an e-bike with limited range and charging requirements), the availability of the second vehicle does not, without more, defeat the need for a like-for-like replacement of the damaged vehicle.

Appellate courts should be slow to interfere with factual findings of experienced district and circuit judges, but will intervene where the judge has misdirected herself on burden of proof or has taken an irrelevant factor (such as cost) into account on the question of need.
cases.significance: Although a decision of a Circuit Judge on a County Court appeal and therefore not formally binding, this judgment carries significant persuasive weight for four reasons: (1) it was argued by Mr Benjamin Williams KC, a leading credit hire specialist; (2) it is a reasoned judgment of HHJ Graham Wood KC, Designated Civil Judge for Cheshire and Merseyside, whose decisions are regularly cited in first-instance credit hire trials in the region; (3) it draws directly on the Supreme Court in Armstead v RSA [2025] AC 407 and the House of Lords/Court of Appeal in Giles v Thompson, Lagden v O'Connor and Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction; and (4) it crystallises two points that are frequently mis-handled at first instance - the proper allocation of the legal and evidential burdens on need, and the impropriety of importing the cost of the hire into the analysis of need. It should be deployed whenever a defendant argues that a claimant has 'not established need' or that the claimant had an alternative vehicle available.
cases.amount_at_stake: £13,195.28 (credit hire, originally pleaded); reduced at trial by concession to BHR of c.£3,884
key_paragraphs: §34: 'an appellate court should be slow to interfere with decisions made on a factual basis and to avoid over analysing the legal approach which was taken by the judge at first instance'

§35: 'As the incurrence of credit hire charges goes to the issue of mitigation of loss, the starting point must be that there is no legal burden of proof on the Claimant to establish need… strictly speaking it is a defendant who has to show that a claimant has acted unreasonably.'

§36: 'It is clear from the opening sentence to paragraph 47 of her judgment that the learned district judge had the correct starting point… However concern does arise that in the last sentence of paragraph 48 her concluding finding is that the Claimant "has not established need on the balance of probabilities".'

§38: 'the cross examination of the Claimant by Mr Calvert is pertinent, and does not involve any challenge of the Claimant either in respect of his use for social domestic and pleasure purposes of the Honda, or the fact that the e-bike was only usable for three-hour periods at the time before a four hour charging was necessary. It is simply put to him that he had no need for a replacement.'

§39: 'a claimant’s evidential burden did not require him to show that a replacement motorbike was essential or that the need arose on a daily basis. The Court of Appeal and the House of Lords in Giles v Thompson used as an example where the motorist might have been acting unreasonably, the situation where the vehicle was hired when he or she had been in hospital or on holiday.'

§40: 'Where the vehicles are different and serve different purposes, that is a conclusion which is more difficult to sustain… having accepted the premise of the previous usage, in my judgment she had no alternative but to accept that the Claimant had discharged the evidential burden in relation to previous need.'

§41: 'there is demonstrably an error in her reference to the cost of the credit hire in the context of need. Whilst reasonableness of the cost would be at the forefront of the court’s consideration on the issue of rate of hire it has no relevance to the issue of need.'
§42: 'it is important that a compartmentalised approach is taken with the starting point addressing need only, where the rate of hire is not taken into account.'
§43: 'Accordingly this appeal is allowed on the basis of the two errors of law identified in grounds 1 and 2(a) of the grounds of appeal.'
cases_cited: Armstead v Royal and Sun Alliance Insurance Co Ltd [2025] AC 407
Bent v Highways and Utilities Construction [2012] RTR 17 (Pattni and Bent)
Wilding v British Telecommunications PLC [2002] ICR 1079
Banco de Portugal v Waterlow [1932] AC 242
Standard Chartered Bank v Pakistan National Shipping Corpn [2001] EWCA Civ 55
LE Jones (Insurance Brokers) Ltd v Portsmouth City Council [2002] EWCA Civ 1723

cases.filename: Gray v Going Places Leisure Travel Ltd 2005 PNLR 26.pdf
cases.case_name: Gray v Going Places Leisure Travel Ltd
cases.defendant: Going Places Leisure Travel Ltd (Appellant on appeal)
cases.outcome: Appeal dismissed. The Court of Appeal held that the district judge had no jurisdiction to make the wasted costs order because, in the absence of good reason, costs orders (including third-party costs orders) should be dealt with by the trial tribunal immediately after judgment disposing of the case. Although a late application is permissible in principle, the court entertaining such an application is not bound to grant it where there is no good reason for the delay.
cases.summary:
SCOPE: For credit-hire purposes the only relevant issue in this judgment is the principle that costs applications against non-parties should, in the absence of good reason, be made promptly and dealt with by the trial tribunal. See [11] and [15] per Neuberger LJ.

Authority shared by Mr Benjamin Williams KC (4 New Square) for use in defending the retrospective NPCO applications currently being pursued by Keoghs LLP against Credit Hire Organisations on historic cases in reliance on Tescher v DAML; AXA v Spectra [2025] EWCA Civ 733.

At [11] Neuberger LJ set out three propositions which are of direct application:
  First  — costs orders in proceedings that go to trial are in principle part of the overall order made at the conclusion of the trial.
  Second — in the absence of at least a good reason to the contrary, the costs of proceedings should be dealt with by the tribunal which determines the issue that disposes of the case, IMMEDIATELY after the judgment disposing of the case.
  Third  — 'in principle, there is no difference in this connection between a costs order against a party and a costs order against a non-party. They are all part of the judicial function involved in disposing of a case.'

At [15] Neuberger LJ added: an application for a wasted costs order CAN be made after the order disposing of the proceedings has been drawn up, 'but that is not to say the court entertaining the application late will necessarily grant it if there is no good reason for the delay.'

Although the judgment is framed in terms of wasted costs, the third proposition at [11] expressly treats party and non-party costs orders as indistinguishable in this respect. Defendant insurers and their solicitors pursuing late NPCO applications on historic credit hire cases must accordingly be prepared to show a good reason for the delay. 'Waiting for binding authority' (the explanation now being advanced by Keoghs in reliance on Tescher) is open to challenge on that footing.
key_principles: [11], proposition 2: 'in the absence of at least a good reason to the contrary, the costs of proceedings... should be dealt with by the tribunal which determines the issue which disposes of the case immediately after the judgment in disposing of the case.'

[11], proposition 3: 'in principle, there is no difference in this connection between a costs order against a party and a costs order against a non-party. They are all part of the judicial function involved in disposing of a case.' — This is the sentence which permits the promptness principle, originally stated for wasted costs, to be transposed into the NPCO context.

[15]: 'the application for a wasted costs order can be made after the order in relation to the proceedings have been drawn up. That is not to say the court entertaining the application late will necessarily grant it if there is no good reason for the delay.'

Practical consequence: a party pursuing a late third-party costs application carries an evidential burden to justify the delay. Mere reliance on subsequent binding authority (e.g. waiting for Tescher/Spectra) is unlikely, of itself, to constitute 'good reason' within the meaning of Gray.
cases.significance: Binding Court of Appeal authority for the proposition that third-party costs applications should be made promptly and should be determined by the trial tribunal absent good reason. Directly relevant to defending the retrospective NPCO applications currently being pursued by Keoghs LLP against CHOs on historic cases in reliance on Tescher v DAML; AXA v Spectra [2025] EWCA Civ 733. Shared for this purpose by Mr Benjamin Williams KC. SCOPE NOTE: Although this judgment addresses two questions — (a) the identity of the tribunal which should make a wasted costs order and (b) the correct destination of an appeal from a district judge on such an order — only the PROMPTNESS principle articulated at [11] and [15] has been treated as relevant for this database. The allocation-of-jurisdiction and routes-of-appeal analyses are NOT relevant to credit hire practice and should be disregarded when this case is deployed in a credit-hire NPCO context.
key_paragraphs: [11]: 'First, the making of an order as to who should bear the costs and on what basis in respect of proceedings which go to a trial are, in principle, part of the overall order made by the court at the conclusion of the trial… Secondly, in the absence of at least a good reason to the contrary, the costs of proceedings… should be dealt with by the tribunal which determines the issue which disposes of the case immediately after the judgment in disposing of the case. Thirdly, in principle, there is no difference in this connection between a costs order against a party and a costs order against a non-party. They are all part of the judicial function involved in disposing of a case.' (Neuberger LJ)

[15]: 'Sixthly, it is right to record that the application for a wasted costs order can be made after the order in relation to the proceedings have been drawn up. That is not to say the court entertaining the application late will necessarily grant it if there is no good reason for the delay… it is the court that determines the outcome of the proceedings which has the jurisdiction to make any order for wasted costs, and it is, in the absence of good reason, only that court which has that jurisdiction.' (Neuberger LJ)
cases_cited: Aaron v Shelton [2004] EWHC 1162; [2004] 3 All E.R. 561

cases.filename: Da Silva v Mihaylov - Keoghs Case Comment 27-02-2024.pdf
cases.case_name: Doglas Da Silva v Martin Mihaylov
cases.citation: (County Court, HHJ Carter, 2024; reported in case comment by Louise Milford, Keoghs LLP, 27 February 2024)
cases.claimant: Doglas Da Silva (Brazilian food delivery rider, Deliveroo / Uber Eats)
cases.outcome: Claim partially succeeded. Liability was admitted. The claimant established need for a replacement motorcycle (he used it for work as a delivery rider) but could not establish impecuniosity. Of the pleaded 511 days of credit hire at a total of GBP 145,870.08, only 41 days at BHR were awarded (GBP 570). The claimant was found fundamentally dishonest for knowingly concealing a Brazilian bank account on the impecuniosity disclosure. Section 57 CJCA 2015 was NOT engaged because the PSLA claim was not itself dishonest. However QOCS protection was lost, the claimant was ordered to pay 75 per cent of the defendant's costs on the indemnity basis, and there was no order for costs in the claimant's favour. The damages award was offset against an interim payment on account of the defendant's costs, resulting in nil net recovery for the claimant.
cases.summary: A food delivery rider on a Honda motorcycle was struck by a Volkswagen driven by the defendant. Liability was admitted. The claimant sought the PAV of the motorcycle (c. GBP 1,400), PSLA, a helmet, recovery and storage charges, and credit hire from Direct Accident Management Ltd (DAML) of GBP 145,870.08 over 511 days. Impecuniosity was pleaded and standard impecuniosity directions were made. The case was originally on the fast track but the initial trial was adjourned for insufficient hearing time and reallocated to the multi-track for two days. At trial, counsel conceded that the period from mid-May 2022 to late November 2022 was not recoverable because the claimant's solicitors had gone to sleep on the file. In cross-examination the claimant accepted that he had a Brazilian bank account into which money had been transferred using the Remitly app, that the Brazilian account and the Remitly account had not been disclosed, and that he had elected to disclose only his UK bank accounts. A mid-trial application for relief from sanctions and an extension of time to produce the Brazilian statements was refused. HHJ Carter found the claimant was wholly inconsistent, that the Brazilian account had been knowingly concealed, that the claimant was an unconvincing witness and that his evidence should be treated with considerable caution. On need, the claimant succeeded: he needed a replacement motorbike to work. On period, he was entitled to 41 days at BHR (GBP 570) until he was in a position to replace the motorcycle from his own funds. On fundamental dishonesty, HHJ Carter held the defendant had shown on the balance of probabilities that the claimant was fundamentally dishonest in relation to the impecuniosity element, which was substantial (and was the reason the claim had been reallocated to the multi-track). The judge observed that it was hard to envisage a clearer case of dishonesty being fundamental to a claim. Section 57 was not engaged because the PSLA limb was not itself dishonest, but QOCS protection was disapplied and the costs consequences followed. Reported in Keoghs' own case comment of 27 February 2024 (Louise Milford, Complex Credit Hire team).
key_principles: Financial disclosure for impecuniosity must be genuinely full and frank. A claimant with a Brazilian (or other foreign) bank account and a Remitly/transfer account who discloses only UK accounts risks a finding of fundamental dishonesty even if need and a modest BHR period are otherwise made out.

A finding of fundamental dishonesty can be confined to one limb of the claim (here the impecuniosity/credit hire limb) without engaging section 57 CJCA 2015, so long as the court is not satisfied that the PSLA limb is itself dishonest. But QOCS protection is still lost on the whole claim.

The costs consequences of a fundamental-dishonesty finding confined to the hire claim can still be severe: here, 75 per cent of the defendant's costs on the indemnity basis, no order for costs in the claimant's favour, and damages offset against the interim costs order so that nil net damages were recovered.
Mid-trial applications for relief from sanctions to produce absent disclosure of foreign-account material are unlikely to succeed where the absence has not been properly explained.

Forensic line-by-line scrutiny of impecuniosity disclosure is a practical imperative for defendant insurers, especially where the claimant has connections to a non-UK banking system. Month-end balances alone do not show the true financial picture.
cases.significance: A County Court decision of HHJ Carter, unreported save for Keoghs' own case comment of 27 February 2024. Although not binding, the decision is frequently cited by defendant insurers in support of (a) forensic scrutiny of foreign-account impecuniosity disclosure, (b) the proposition that fundamental dishonesty can be confined to the credit hire/impecuniosity limb without engaging section 57, and (c) the severity of costs consequences in such cases. It is also a cautionary illustration for claimant solicitors of the risk of going to sleep on the file during a long hire period. Users searching for this case may sometimes mis-remember the claimant's name - common aliases include Da Silva, Dasilva, De Silva and Mesquita - all of which should return this entry.
cases.amount_at_stake: Credit hire claim GBP 145,870.08 (511 days); awarded GBP 570 (41 days at BHR). Total claim including PSLA, PAV and associated losses.
key_paragraphs: HHJ Carter: hard to envisage a clearer case of dishonesty being fundamental to a claim.
HHJ Carter: the claimant was wholly inconsistent in his evidence about the Brazilian bank account. There was no explanation as to why this account had not formed part of his disclosure.
HHJ Carter: the claimant knowingly concealed his Brazilian account.

HHJ Carter: the claimant's responses to difficult questions were also lacking in credibility and overall he was an unconvincing witness. The court would treat his evidence with a considerable degree of caution.

On need: the claimant needed a replacement motorbike to work as a delivery driver. He was entitled to hire a replacement bike until he was in a position to replace it from his own funds (not being impecunious). This was assessed at 41 days, being the date the vehicle was scrapped.

On fundamental dishonesty: the defendant had shown on the balance of probabilities that the claimant was dishonest, that his dishonesty was fundamental to the claim and had had a substantial effect on the presentation of the claim. The impecuniosity element of the hire claim was not only substantial but was the reason the claim came out of the fast track and into the multi-track.
On section 57: because the PSLA claim was not itself found to be dishonest, a section 57 defence was not engaged.

On costs: QOCS protection was lost. The claimant was to pay 75 per cent of the defendant's costs of the claim on the indemnity basis. There was no order for costs in the claimant's favour despite his obtaining a judgment. The damages were offset against an interim on account of the defendant's costs, resulting in no payment to the claimant.

cases.filename: Hartshorn v NFU Mutual - DWF Case Comment.pdf
cases.citation: (County Court at Newcastle, HHJ Freeman on appeal, February 2022)
cases.court: County Court (Newcastle) - Appeal from District Judge
cases.outcome: Appeal dismissed. The first-instance award of damages based on loss of profit (GBP 2,716.48) plus fixed overheads (5 months of car finance at GBP 740.10) plus storage (GBP 1,024) - total GBP 4,480.58 - was upheld. The claimant did not beat the defendant's pre-action Part 36 offers (GBP 1,290 for recovery and storage plus GBP 9,850 for hire) and was subject to the resulting costs consequences. On appeal, HHJ Freeman upheld the trial judge's approach that (a) occasional SDP use of the taxi was not, on the facts, a Hussain v EUI exception that would justify hire at c. GBP 30,000, and (b) although the claimant was Lagden-impecunious, the household finances (including his partner's income as a cleaner and Working Tax Credits) could properly be taken into account to conclude that he did not satisfy the separate Hussain-impecuniosity exception (i.e. the need to work to survive).
cases.summary: The claimant was a taxi driver in Newcastle whose own vehicle was written off in an accident caused by an NFU Mutual insured. He instructed Winns Solicitors and hired a replacement taxi from On Hire (the linked credit hire company) for 133 days at GBP 29,877.12. He also incurred recovery and storage charges of GBP 1,344 (largely because correspondence had initially gone to the wrong insurer). Taxi BHR in Newcastle could not be obtained. The defendant instead obtained SDP rates in case the court awarded loss of profit plus an SDP element (per Hussain v EUI [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB)). At first instance the judge applied Hussain and awarded only loss of profit (GBP 2,716.48 over 133 days), fixed overheads (GBP 740.10) and storage (GBP 1,024), totalling GBP 4,480.58. The claimant failed to beat the defendant's Part 36 offers and suffered the costs consequences. On appeal, HHJ Freeman in Newcastle upheld the trial judge on both limbs. On the SDP exception, the court held that occasional SDP use is a matter of degree, not a binary question: even where some SDP use is established, this does not automatically put the claimant inside one of the Hussain exceptions. The trial judge's impressionistic assessment that a taxi could be used for the occasional SDP need was within his discretion. On the impecuniosity exception, HHJ Freeman distinguished Lagden-impecuniosity (whether a claimant can afford to hire at spot/BHR rates) from Hussain-impecuniosity (whether the claimant needs to work to survive). The Hussain standard is materially lower: the judge is bound to look at the bigger picture, including household finances and outgoings. Here the trial judge had considered the claimant's net profit of c. GBP 7,000 p.a., his partner's income as a cleaner, and the family's Working Tax Credits, and had concluded that the family would cope without the claimant's income while awaiting the PAV payment. That conclusion was open to him.
key_principles: HUSSAIN EXCEPTIONS ARE A MATTER OF DEGREE, NOT BINARY. Following Hussain v EUI [2019] EWHC 2647 (QB), where the cost of hire in a taxi case significantly exceeds loss of profit the court will ordinarily limit damages to lost profit. The three exceptions (future trading compromised, SDP use, impecuniosity) are discretionary. The word 'may' in the Hussain judgment does substantial work: establishing an exception does not mandate an award of hire charges. Occasional SDP use is insufficient on its own.

TWO DIFFERENT IMPECUNIOSITY TESTS. The impecuniosity test in Lagden v O'Connor [2004] 1 AC 1067 (can a claimant afford to hire a substitute at spot/BHR rates?) is DIFFERENT from the impecuniosity exception in Hussain (whether the claimant needed to work to survive). The Hussain standard is materially lower. A claimant who satisfies the Lagden test may still fail the Hussain exception.

HOUSEHOLD INCOME IS RELEVANT TO HUSSAIN-IMPECUNIOSITY. For the Hussain exception, the court must look at the bigger picture - including the claimant's spouse's/partner's income and the family's overall outgoings. It would be artificial to ignore other members of the household. The appeal judge in Hussain had this in mind when he talked about providing for families. This is an extension of the envelope in defendant-favourable terms.

THE TRIAL JUDGE'S IMPRESSIONISTIC ASSESSMENT IS RESPECTED ON APPEAL. Whether a particular level of SDP use justifies a replacement vehicle is an impressionistic assessment that the trial judge is uniquely positioned to make. An appellate court will interfere only where the assessment is outside the range reasonably open to the judge (cf Bourgrine v Natoora Ltd, HHJ Wood KC, 23 Feb 2026).

PRACTICAL CONSEQUENCE OF PART 36. The claimant's credit hire claim of c. GBP 30,000 over 133 days was reduced to an award of c. GBP 4,480 following application of Hussain. The defendant's Part 36 offers were not beaten, so the costs consequences of CPR Part 36.17(3) followed - a stark illustration of the risk of pursuing full credit hire in a taxi case where loss of profit is modest and the Hussain framework applies.
cases.significance: Persuasive first-instance-on-appeal County Court authority on three points of practical importance in taxi credit hire litigation. (1) The Hussain SDP exception is a matter of degree: occasional SDP use of a taxi is unlikely on its own to justify hire at credit hire rates. (2) The impecuniosity test in Hussain is materially different from, and lower than, the Lagden impecuniosity test - a claimant who is Lagden-impecunious may still fail the Hussain exception. (3) Household income (including a partner's income and benefits such as Working Tax Credits) may be taken into account when considering whether the claimant needs to work to survive. Point (3) is genuinely novel and is being deployed by defendant insurers (in this instance DWF, acting for NFU Mutual) to defeat Hussain-impecuniosity arguments. Claimant practitioners should anticipate the argument, prepare household financial evidence (or, conversely, evidence that the household genuinely relies on the claimant's income), and be ready to distinguish Hartshorn on the facts.
cases.amount_at_stake: Hire claimed GBP 29,877.12 (133 days); recovery and storage GBP 1,344. Awarded: loss of profit GBP 2,716.48, fixed overheads GBP 740.10, storage GBP 1,024 = GBP 4,480.58. Part 36 offers of GBP 1,290 (recovery/storage) and GBP 9,850 (hire) not beaten.
key_paragraphs: HHJ Freeman (on SDP): whilst there would be an element of inconvenience in using a taxi, this would not necessarily mean a claimant had made their case for the need to hire another vehicle. It is an impressionistic assessment that the trial judge is uniquely positioned to make.

HHJ Freeman (on household income): judges are bound to look at the bigger picture and what is needed by a household to discharge its outgoings, and it would be artificial not to take account of other members of the household. The appeal judge in Hussain had that in mind when he talked about 'providing for families'. In that sense he was looking at the overall picture, and that's what the trial judge in this case had done.

The trial judge (facts on impecuniosity, approved on appeal): the claimant had a declared net profit of around GBP 7,000 p.a.; his partner was a cleaner; the household received Working Tax Credits. On that basis the family would cope without the claimant's income, he was not the main breadwinner, and he did not need to replace the vehicle before receiving the PAV.

Source article (Gavin Perry, DWF): 'There is now a potential argument to defeat hire charges in a case where a claimant claims to use their taxi for SDP purposes. HHJ Freeman determined that even where some SDP use is established, this does not automatically put the claimant into one of the Hussain v EUI exceptions.'

Source article (Gavin Perry, DWF): 'It has been successfully argued that impecuniosity as it relates to credit hire (in a Lagden sense - can a claimant afford to hire on spot rates?) is materially different to the question of impecuniosity for the purposes of Hussain (i.e. whether the claimant needed to work to survive). In this instance, the latter was a fundamentally lower standard.'

cases.citation: (County Court at Cardiff, HHJ Jarman KC, 24 February 2023)
cases.outcome: Mixed. Liability admitted. Claimant held to be impecunious and entitled to credit hire rate. Reasonable hire period found to run until early October 2020 (shortly after D's interim payment). However, HHJ Jarman KC dismissed the hire claim under the FIRST TWO hire agreements (entered before the IVA) on the basis that they were 'unenforceable' because of the IVA entered by C on 10 August 2020 and because DAML had not proved the debt in the IVA. Only the 28 days of hire under the third agreement (entered after the IVA) were allowed, totalling GBP 13,624.56. Permission to appeal was granted by Nicklin J on the IVA/unenforceability point. The appeal was settled before the hearing. The case is therefore not a binding determination on the IVA issue, but its facts, reasoning and the skeleton arguments prepared by Mr Benjamin Williams KC for the abortive appeal have become the focal authority for defendants (particularly Keoghs) seeking to deploy an IVA defence to credit hire recovery.
cases.summary: The claimant's car was badly damaged in an RTA in Cardiff caused by the defendant's insured driver. Liability was admitted. The car was deemed a total loss. The claimant could not afford conventional non-credit hire and took a replacement from Direct Accident Management Ltd (DAML) on credit hire, remaining in hire for 215 days (24 March 2020 to 24 October 2020) across THREE separate hire agreements (24 March, 16 June, 2 September 2020). On 10 August 2020, during the second hire agreement, the claimant entered an Individual Voluntary Arrangement (IVA) with his creditors. The CHO was not notified of the IVA proposal, did not vote on it, and was not listed as a creditor. HHJ Jarman KC found C impecunious and entitled to the full credit rate; found the reasonable hire period ran until early October 2020 (post D's interim payment); rejected D's argument that C was debarred by breach of a peremptory order from relying on impecuniosity. HOWEVER, he dismissed the claim for hire under the first two agreements on the basis that the IVA rendered them 'unenforceable', drawing a (disputed) analogy with Dimond v Lovell [2000] UKHL 27. He allowed 28 days of hire under the third (post-IVA) agreement at GBP 13,624.56. PTA was refused on the impecuniosity point by Stacey J and Nicklin J, but granted on the IVA/unenforceability point. The appeal was settled before hearing.
key_principles: An IVA is a statutory contract (Insolvency Act 1986, s 260(2)) binding every creditor entitled to vote, whether or not they had notice. It is not an extinction of debts: Green v Wright [2017] EWCA Civ 111.

HHJ Jarman KC's holding that an IVA renders pre-IVA hire agreements 'unenforceable' by analogy with Dimond v Lovell [2000] UKHL 27 is, on the analysis advanced by Mr Benjamin Williams KC for the claimant's appeal, wrong in law: the IVA merely places a moratorium on enforcement by creditors while assets are collected, and does not permanently reduce the debtor's liability to those creditors.

The argument that an IVA entered after the first two hire agreements is res inter alios acta as between the claimant and the tortfeasor is supported by: (a) the collateral-arrangement analysis in The Elena d'Amico [1980] 1 Lloyd's Rep 75 and British Westinghouse v Underground Electric Railways [1912] AC 673, (b) AssetCo v Grant Thornton [2019] EWHC 150 (closest parallel - a scheme of arrangement is not mitigation), and (c) the policy considerations in Parry v Cleaver [1970] AC 1.

If an IVA did reduce the tortfeasor's liability, a perverse result would follow: C's creditors would be better off bankrupting C than entering the IVA. This militates in favour of treating IVA discounts as res inter alios acta for the purposes of damages.
cases.significance: Focal first-instance authority in the emerging IVA defence to credit hire. Although the judgment of HHJ Jarman KC is not binding and was the subject of granted-but-settled permission to appeal, it is routinely relied on by Keoghs LLP (and others) to resist recovery of credit hire charges where the claimant has entered an IVA. The claimant's skeleton arguments on the abortive appeal (drafted by Mr Benjamin Williams KC) contain the detailed rebuttal of the IVA defence. See accompanying counsel_article on Impecuniosity and IVAs by Steve Evans (The Credit Hire Forum).
cases.amount_at_stake: Total hire c. GBP 92,000 (215 days). Awarded GBP 13,624.56 (28 days under third agreement). Disallowed GBP 78,300.96 (first two agreements, on IVA grounds).
key_paragraphs: HHJ Jarman KC: In the absence of any timely application to challenge the IVA, or any application to extend time for doing so, I should proceed on the basis that the IVA binds DAML as if it were a party to the arrangement.

Appellant's argument (supported by grant of PTA): s 260(2)(b) of the Insolvency Act 1986 provides that the approved arrangement binds every person who was entitled to vote as if they were a party, and the IVA does not extinguish the debt - see Green v Wright [2017] EWCA Civ 111 at paras 21, 27 and 30-33.
cases_cited: British Westinghouse v Underground Electric Railways [1912] AC 673
Thai Airways v K I Holdings [2015] EWHC 1250
Merrett v Capitol Indemnity [1991] 1 Lloyd's Rep 169

cases.citation: (1966) 110 SJ 215, Court of Appeal — citation given in the Lyons Davidson commentary; not independently verified
cases.defendant: Moody (driver of car turning right out of side road through gap in stationary queue)
cases.outcome: Mixed - both parties found negligent. Liability apportioned 80% against the claimant motorcyclist and 20% against the defendant car driver. The Court held that any road user who 'jumps a queue of stationary vehicles by filtering on the offside' was 'undertaking an operation fraught with hazard' which had to be carried out with great care because it was always difficult to see, from the offside of a queue of stationary vehicles, gaps in the queue on its nearside from which traffic might emerge.
cases.summary: The claimant was riding his motorcycle on a busy London road and approached two lines of stationary traffic held up at an upcoming junction. He decided to filter past on the offside. The defendant driver had edged forward in a side road intending to turn right and was beckoned out by a milk tanker driver who had left a gap in the queue. The motorcyclist and the car collided. The Court of Appeal found both negligent: the motorcyclist for filtering on the offside in circumstances where he could not see, and the car driver for failing to anticipate the actions of other road users. Liability apportioned 80/20 against the claimant. The case is now over 60 years old but is still routinely cited by defendant insurers in motorcycle filtering cases for the broad proposition that filtering on the offside is inherently hazardous and the motorcyclist will bear the lion's share of the blame. That broad reading is open to substantial challenge: the case is fact-specific (busy London road, milk tanker beckoning, particular geometry) and predates several material developments in the law and the Highway Code, including the introduction of the Hierarchy of Road Users (rules H1, H2, H3) in January 2022, which places higher duties on drivers of larger vehicles to look out for vulnerable road users including motorcyclists.
key_principles: Filtering past stationary traffic on the offside is described as 'an operation fraught with hazard' that requires great care because it is difficult to see gaps in the queue on the nearside from which traffic might emerge.
Where two road users each fail to anticipate the actions of others, both may be found contributorily negligent and liability apportioned.

Apportionment is fact-specific: the 80/20 outcome in Powell v Moody reflects the particular circumstances of a busy London road where the motorcyclist filtered past TWO lines of stationary traffic into a junction with a milk tanker beckoning the car driver out.

The case is now over 60 years old and predates: (a) the modern body of authority on filtering, (b) the codification of vulnerable-road-user duties in the 2022 Highway Code Hierarchy of Road Users (rules H1, H2, H3), (c) the Court of Appeal's later treatment of motorcyclist apportionment in cases like Smith v Chief Constable of Nottinghamshire [2012] EWCA Civ 161 (50/50), Davis v Schrogin [2006] EWCA Civ 974 and Powell v Phillips [1972] 3 All ER 864.

The Lyons Davidson commentary itself acknowledges the principle that 'each case must be assessed on its own particular facts' and that motorcyclists 'are entitled to carry out manoeuvres such as filtering'.
legal_topics: right turn out of side road
cases.significance: The leading defendant-side authority cited in motorcycle filtering accidents to support apportionment heavily against the claimant. As the Lyons Davidson 2019 commentary acknowledges, the case 'is still commonly used by insurers acting for defendants, even though the case is now over 50 years old'. Claimant practitioners faced with reliance on Powell v Moody should challenge it on three fronts: (1) fact-specificity (the particular circumstances of the case rarely match the modern accident geometry), (2) age (the case predates the modern Highway Code, the 2022 Hierarchy of Road Users, and many later Court of Appeal authorities on motorcycle apportionment), and (3) the principle in the case itself that each case turns on its own facts.
key_paragraphs: Per the trial judge: any road user who jumps a queue of stationary vehicles by filtering on the offside was 'undertaking an operation fraught with hazard'.

Per the trial judge: the manoeuvre had to be carried out with great care because it was always difficult to see 'from the offside of a queue of stationary vehicles, gaps in the queue on its nearside from which traffic might emerge'.
Outcome: liability apportioned 80% against the claimant motorcyclist and 20% against the defendant car driver.
